source_text: End-of-Life Vehicle Directive revision Ref. Ares(2020)6706387 - 13/11/2020 European Aluminium’s top priorities for the revision of the ELV Directive May 2020 In the context of the revision of the End of Life Vehicle Directive and following the workshop on “The Evaluation of the ELV Directive” on the 5th of February, European Aluminium would like to share its top priorities. The following points are focusing on issues that can improve the recycling of End of Life Vehicles (ELVs) in general and the recycling of aluminium from these vehicles in particular. 1. There is no need for minimum recycled content rates for materials like aluminium. Introducing a minimum recycled content for materials like steel and aluminium makes no sense. These materials are already today showing high recycling rates. Approximately 95% of the aluminium in an End of Life Vehicle is profitably recycled and all secondary material put on the market will be used for new products. On the contrary, if a minimum recycled content target for aluminium components in cars would be introduced, it can lead to unintended consequences. Due to the long lifespan of volume-wise dominant aluminium applications such as buildings and transport vehicles, the available quantity of end-of-life aluminium scrap today is limited to what was put on the market many years ago
context_after: As this limited scrap volume cannot meet the increasing demand for aluminium, the shortfall must be met by the primary aluminium industry Imposing minimum recycled content figures for cars will simply take recycled aluminium from other products. While this may reduce the environmental impact of producing cars, it will not affect the overall picture. Instead, it will only displace the burdens and the benefits from one product to the other and may induce longer scrap transportation distances. 2. Well proven aluminium solutions that can combine the goals of making a vehicle lighter AND fully and economically recyclable already exist Making a car lighter and more energy efficient using aluminium instead of steel does not make the vehicle more difficult to recycle. The ELV Directive should not promote less or non-recyclable materials. If the goal of the ELV Directive remains to limit the production of waste and to increase the rates of reuse, recycling and recovery, the ELV Directive should continue to focus on the end-of-life stage and promote materials that are easy to recycle, in particular metals like aluminium. 3. Design for recycling and post shredder separation technologies should be incentivised further The most cost-efficient End-of-Life Vehicle treatment is only achievable if all actors work together
provision_effect: Article 6(4) requires the Commission to assess the feasibility of establishing minimum recycled content requirements for aluminium and its alloys in vehicle types, and empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts setting such minimum shares.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: European Aluminium explicitly argues that "there is no need for minimum recycled content rates for materials like aluminium" and that introducing such targets "makes no sense" and could lead to "unintended consequences." Article 6(4) directly establishes a process — a feasibility assessment followed by empowerment to set binding minimum recycled content requirements — for aluminium in vehicles, which is precisely the mechanism the organisation opposes.

article_title: Declaration on recycled content present in vehicles
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers to declare the share of recycled content of aluminium (and other materials) present in each vehicle type at the time of type-approval.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: European Aluminium explicitly opposes minimum recycled content targets for aluminium, but Article 10 establishes a declaration/reporting requirement rather than a binding target. The org's position is specifically against mandatory recycled content rates, not against transparency declarations per se — yet a mandatory declaration obligation is a precursor to (and closely linked with) a target regime, and the org's concern about "unintended consequences" of recycled content requirements could extend to this mechanism. It is genuinely unclear whether the org would view a declaration-only obligation (with no target) as acceptable or as an unwanted step toward the targets it opposes, making the direction ambiguous.

source_text: End-of-Life Vehicle Directive revision For the remaining part the End-of-Life Vehicle that could not be economically dismantled, ELV processing plants and manufacturers should be required to accelerate development of post-shredder separation technologies that will be able to sort aluminium alloys in different alloy families. 4. The EU must act to get a better understanding of where the End-or-Life Vehicles of unknown whereabouts end up. The EU must also take swift action against any illegal shipment or treatment of End-of-Life Vehicles The large number of End-of-Life Vehicles with unknown whereabouts must be addressed. The deregistration system must be improved with the aim to stop any illegal shipment or treatment of End-of-Life Vehicles. These aspects negatively affect the economy and the environmental vision pushed by the European Commission due to the loss of valuable secondary materials. / 2
context_before: 3. Design for recycling and post shredder separation technologies should be incentivised further The most cost-efficient End-of-Life Vehicle treatment is only achievable if all actors work together. Post- shredder treatments are of course part of the solution but dismantling before shredding also makes sense for parts that can easily be recycled into similar applications again (e.g. hoods, doors). The easiness and economic feasibility of dismantling before shredding depends on parts location but also on if the product was designed for easy dismantling. The design and production of new vehicles shall therefore consider design for recycling, design for dismantling and design for reuse. To facilitate the most appropriate treatment of End-of-Life Vehicles by recyclers and dismantlers it would be important to introduce a better tracking of materials put in the car, for example through the International Dismantling Information System (IDIS) or other methods. The information should include the materials and alloys used in each component and the instructions for the disassembly and recycling.
article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Establishes obligations for authorised treatment facilities regarding acceptance, storage, depollution, dismantling, and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, including requirements to apply best available techniques and comply with minimum treatment standards.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: European Aluminium advocates for requiring ELV processing plants to develop post-shredder separation technologies for aluminium alloy sorting, and for tackling illegal shipments/treatment of ELVs. Article 27 sets out treatment obligations for authorised facilities but does not specifically address post-shredder separation technology development or illegal shipment/deregistration issues. There is a real connection to ELV treatment obligations, but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the organisation's specific asks.

provision_effect: Establishes mandatory collection systems for end-of-life vehicles, requiring producers or producer responsibility organisations to set up comprehensive, territory-wide collection networks including collection points, free-of-charge delivery to authorised treatment facilities, and electronic documentation of vehicle receipt.
label: ALIGNED

Article 23 directly responds to this by mandating comprehensive collection systems covering whole territories, requiring electronic documentation of ELV receipt, and ensuring all vehicles are delivered to authorised treatment facilities, which collectively address the problem of vehicles escaping proper collection channels.

article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory depollution requirements for end-of-life vehicles at authorised treatment facilities, including separate collection of fluids, removal of hazardous substances, battery removal, and documentation of the depollution process.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on post-shredder aluminium alloy separation technologies, illegal ELV shipments, and deregistration system improvements — none of which are addressed by this provision. The provision deals with depollution procedures at authorised treatment facilities, which is a distinct subject from what European Aluminium advocates for, making the texts substantively unrelated.

article_text:
Article 36 — Shipments of end-of-life vehicles
1. Treatment of end-of-life vehicles may be undertaken outside the Union, provided that the shipment of end-of-life vehicles is in compliance with Regulation (EC) No 1013/2006.

2. Shipments of end-of-life vehicles from the Union to a third country in accordance with paragraph 1 shall only count towards the fulfilment of obligations and targets set out in Article 34 if the exporter of the end-of-life vehicles provides documentary evidence approved by the competent authority of destination demonstrating that the treatment took place in conditions that are broadly equivalent to the requirements laid down in this Regulation and to human health and environmental protection requirements laid down in other Union legislation.
provision_effect: Establishes conditions under which end-of-life vehicle treatment outside the EU is permitted and counts toward regulatory targets, requiring documentary evidence of broadly equivalent treatment standards.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: European Aluminium explicitly calls for swift action against illegal shipments and treatment of end-of-life vehicles and improved deregistration systems to stop such practices. Article 36 directly addresses this by conditioning the legality and regulatory recognition of ELV shipments to third countries on compliance with Regulation (EC) No 1013/2006 and proof of equivalent treatment standards, which is a concrete regulatory mechanism targeting the very problem the org identified.

article_title: Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
article_text:
Article 30 — Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, are removed from an end-of-life vehicle prior to shredding, after the depollution operations referred to in Article 29, have been completed.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if an authorised treatment facility demonstrates, that post-shredder technologies separates materials from parts and components listed in Part C, entries 13 to19, of Annex VII, as efficiently as manual dismantling processes or semi-automated disassembly processes.
For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the authorised treatment facility shall provide the information listed in Part G of Annex VII.

3. In addition to the obligations set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall maintain records of the end-of-life vehicles that are processed without the prior removal of parts, components and materials in accordance with paragraph 2, including the name and address of the treatment facilities, and the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the end-of-life vehicles concerned.
The authorised treatment facilities shall provide the information in the records referred to in the first subparagraph to the competent authority in accordance with Article 49(6).
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to remove specified parts and components from end-of-life vehicles prior to shredding, with an exemption if post-shredder technologies can separate materials as efficiently as manual/semi-automated dismantling.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: European Aluminium explicitly advocates for accelerating development of post-shredder separation technologies for the parts of ELVs that cannot be economically dismantled. Article 30(2) directly incorporates this logic by providing an exemption from mandatory pre-shredder removal when post-shredder technologies can separate materials as efficiently as manual dismantling, thereby incentivising and recognising the role of such technologies — which is precisely the direction the organisation advocated for.

article_text:
Article 17 — Register of producers
1. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 35 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall establish a register of producers which shall serve to monitor compliance of producers with the requirements of this Chapter.
The register shall provide links to other national registers of producers’ websites to facilitate, in all Member States, registration of producers or appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

2. Producers shall register in the register referred to in paragraph 1. They shall to that end submit an application for registration in each Member State where they make a vehicle available on the market for the first time.
Producers shall submit the application for registration via an electronic data-processing system as referred to in paragraph 8, point (a).
Producers shall only make available vehicles on the market of a Member State, if they or, in case of authorisation, their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility, are registered in such Member State.

3. The application for registration shall include the information listed in Annex VIII. Member States may request additional information or documents, as necessary, to use the register of producers in an efficient manner.

4. By way of derogation from paragraph 3, the information referred to in point 1 (d) of Annex VIII shall be provided either in the application for registration under paragraph 3 or in the application for authorisation under Article 19.

5. Where a producer has appointed a producer responsibility organisation in accordance with Article 18, the obligations under this Article shall be met by that organisation mutatis mutandis unless otherwise specified by the Member State in which the vehicle has been made available on the market for the first time.

6. The obligations under this Article may be fulfilled on a producer’s behalf by an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility.

7. Member States may decide that the registration procedure pursuant to this Article and the authorisation procedure pursuant to Article 19 constitute a single procedure, provided that the application for authorisation meets the requirements set out in paragraphs 3 to 6 of this Article.

8. The competent authority shall:
(a)make available on its website information about the application process via an electronic data-processing system;
(b)grant registrations and provide a registration number within a maximum period of 12 weeks from the moment that all the information required under paragraphs 2 and 3 is provided.

9. The competent authority may:
(a)lay down modalities with regard to the requirements and process of registration without adding substantive requirements to those laid down in paragraphs 2 and 3;
(b)charge cost-based and proportionate fees to producers for the processing of the applications referred to in paragraph 2.

10. The competent authority may refuse to register a producer or withdraw the producer’s registration where the information referred to in paragraph 3 and related documentary evidence are not provided or are not sufficient, or where the producer no longer meets the requirements laid down in Annex VIII, point 1 (d).
The competent authority shall withdraw the producer’s registration if the producer has ceased to exist.

11. The producer, or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation appointed on behalf of the producers it represents shall without undue delay notify the competent authority of any changes to the information contained in the registration and of any permanent cessation as regards the making available on the market within the territory of the Member State of the vehicles referred to in the registration.

12. The producer or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation shall report to the competent authority responsible for the register on the performance of extended producer responsibility obligations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory register of producers for vehicles, requiring producers to register in each Member State where they place vehicles on the market, and sets out procedures for registration, reporting, and compliance monitoring under extended producer responsibility (EPR).
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation advocates for a strengthened and clarified EPR framework — including mandatory producer cost coverage, financing calculations, and prohibition of monopolistic EPR systems — and explicitly supports converting the Directive into a European Regulation to ensure proper implementation. The provision establishes a producer register as part of the EPR compliance mechanism, which is related to the org's advocacy for a robust EPR system, but the org's specific concerns (cost obligations, financing structures, anti-monopoly rules) are not directly addressed or contradicted by this registration article. A genuine connection to EPR exists, but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific positions.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6813014 - 16/11/2020 The revision of the ELV Directive is the right opportunity to implement urgently needed measures to solve practical challenges linked to the Directive. GRC therefore proposes the following measures: Well-framed financial incentives for the last holder to deliver a vehicle to authorized treatment facilities in exchange of a CoD The certificate of destruction is the cornerstone of the ELV Directive. The issue linked to “ELV whereabouts” is rooted in the uneven enforcement across Europe of the certificate of destruction (CoD). Incentives can take the form of a refundable tax paid by the owner of the vehicle on an annual basis alongside with existing taxes paid on an annual basis which can only be refunded, as an incentive, to the last owner of the vehicle when delivering the vehicle for proper treatment at its end-of- life. Incentives can also use existing obligations with which each owner of a vehicle has to comply with, such as insurances’ premium. All types vehicles inclusion is necessary: It is necessary to include all vehicles in the scope of the regulations. Not only a regulated and correct management of vehicles of less than 3,500 tons (passenger vehicles), but the rest of typologies must be included, both of greater and lesser weight: motorcycles and motorcycles, industrial vehicles (trucks, caravans, industrial, agricultural machinery, of public works, ...), and treated in ATFs
context_after: The lack of regulation regarding the environmental treatment of these vehicles and of the facilities licensed for this activity leads to environmentally wrong practices resulting in serious consequences for the environment, public health and the economy of the industry. This in turn means that a high percentage of industrial vehicles taken out of commission are not managed or treated by duly prepared and licensed companies, which in parallel results in activities and an economy completely outside all tax legislation. Improve registration and de-registrations systems GRC recommends to implement a harmonized vehicle registration and de- registration system within the EU. Currently, each Member State maintains its own system and for example, national vehicle registration authorities are not obliged to report on export or import of used vehicles within the EU. However, information on export/import of used vehicles is necessary to validate reported numbers on treated ELVs in Member States and prevent loopholes contributing to unknown ELVs’ whereabouts. Equally, de-registration systems have to improve as well since improperly treated ELVs pose a significant risk to the environment. article_text:
Article 25 — Certificate of destruction
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall issue a certificate of destruction for every treated end-of-life vehicle to the last owner of the end-of-life vehicle. The certificate of destruction shall contain the information listed in Annex IX.

2. The certificate of destruction shall be issued in an electronic format and provided through an electronic notification procedure to the relevant authorities of the Member State, including the competent authorities designated under Article 14.

3. In case the end-of-life vehicle, for which a certificate of destruction has been issued in a Member State, is registered in another Member State, the vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the certificate of destruction was issued shall inform the relevant vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the vehicle is registered that a certificate of destruction has been issued for the vehicle in question.

4. The relevant authorities of a Member State shall cancel the registration of an end-of-life vehicle only after receiving the certificate of destruction for that vehicle.

5. Certificates of destruction issued in a Member State shall be recognised in all other Member States.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory electronic certificates of destruction (CoD) to be issued by authorised treatment facilities, with cross-border recognition and registration cancellation tied to CoD receipt.

article_text:
Article 45 — Electronic systems
1. The MOVE-HUB electronic system developed by the Commission shall be used for exchanging Vehicle Identification Number and information on the vehicle registration and roadworthiness status between national vehicle registers and electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States, as well as, to interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, where necessary for controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. The MOVE-HUB electronic system, referred to in paragraph 1 shall provide at least the following functionalities:
(a)exchange the data in real-time with the national vehicle registers, national electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States interconnected with it;
(b)enable an automated electronic check of data provided in a roadworthiness certificate as referred to in Annex II of Directive 2014/45/EU of the date of first registration of a vehicle, as well as of the Member State where a vehicle was last registered, as referred to in Directive 1999/37/EC, to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with the requirements set out in Article 38, Article 39(1) and Article 40;
(c)interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2399 for the purpose of exchanging data and support the process of exchange of information referred to in Articles 39(1) and Article 40(2), as well as support the notifications referred to in Articles 41 to 43;
(d)for the purpose of cooperation with third countries under Article 44(2), allow electronic exchange of information with the competent authorities of third countries which have notified to the Commission pursuant to Article 40(2) the specific conditions for the import of used vehicles that they apply.

3. Member States shall interconnect their national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness with the MOVE-HUB electronic system referred to in paragraph 1. That interconnection shall be operational within 2 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

4. The Commission shall interconnect the MOVE-HUB system referred to in paragraph 1 to the EU Customs Single Window Certificate Exchange System established in Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2399, so that the automated controls referred to in Article 39 and Article 40(2) and the notifications referred to in Articles 41, 42 and 43 can be performed. That interconnection shall be operational within 4 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

5. The Commission shall adopt the implementing acts laying down the necessary arrangements for the implementation of the functionalities of the MOVE-HUB referred to in paragraph 2, including the technical aspects necessary for the interconnection of national electronic systems to the MOVE-HUB, the conditions of connection to MOVE-HUB, the data to be transmitted by the national systems and the format for the transmission of that data through the interconnected national systems.
The implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Establishes the MOVE-HUB electronic system to interconnect national vehicle registers and roadworthiness systems across Member States for real-time data exchange, automated checks on used vehicles for export, and integration with EU Customs systems.

reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a harmonised EU vehicle registration and de-registration system, noting that national systems are not interconnected and that information on export/import of used vehicles is necessary to validate ELV treatment data and prevent loopholes. The provision directly responds to this by establishing the MOVE-HUB system to interconnect national vehicle registers in real-time and enable automated checks on used vehicles being exported, which is precisely the kind of harmonised, cross-border registration/de-registration infrastructure the organisation called for. Additionally, the org's suggestion that roadworthiness test results could help distinguish used cars from ELVs is directly reflected in the provision's functionality to use roadworthiness certificates for automated compliance checks.

article_title: Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
article_text:
Article 38 — Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] used vehicles to be exported shall be subject to the controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. This Section is without prejudice to any other provisions of this Regulation as well as to other Union legal acts governing the release for export of goods, in particular Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and its Articles 46, 47, 267 and 269.

3. Used vehicles may be exported only if they are:
(a)not end-of-life vehicles based on the criteria listed in Annex I;
(b)considered roadworthy in the Member State where the vehicles were last registered, in accordance with Article 5(1), points (a) and (b), and Article 8 of Directive 2014/45/EU.

4. The following information shall be provided or made available to customs authorities for each used vehicle to be exported:
(a)the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the used vehicle and the identification of the Member State where the vehicle was last registered;
(b)a statement confirming that the used vehicle fulfills the requirements set out in paragraph.

5. In order to verify the compliance with this Section on allowing a used vehicle to be released for export:
(a)until the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 44, and, where necessary, shall take into account such exchange of information and cooperation in order to allow a used vehicle to be released for export;
(b)once the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, Articles 39, 40(2) and (3) and 42(3) shall apply, and notifications and other exchanges under Articles 41 to 43 shall take place by means of those electronic systems.

6. A used vehicle to be exported shall not:
(a)be placed under a customs procedure based on a simplified declaration pursuant to Article 166 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)be subject to an entry in the declarant’s records pursuant to Article 182 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)be subject to self-assessment pursuant to Article 185 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts, in accordance with Article 50 of this Regulation, to amend the criteria listed in Annex I determining whether a used vehicle is an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes controls and requirements for the export of used vehicles, including criteria to distinguish used vehicles from end-of-life vehicles (ELVs) and roadworthiness checks, with information-sharing obligations for customs authorities.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation specifically advocates for a harmonised system to track used vehicle exports/imports and a clear, easy-to-implement distinction between used vehicles and ELVs based on roadworthiness tests — both of which are directly delivered by this provision, which conditions export on the vehicle not being an ELV (per Annex I criteria) and on passing a roadworthiness check (per Directive 2014/45/EU), while also requiring information exchange with customs authorities on exported vehicles.

article_title: Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
article_text:
Article 44 — Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
1. Member States shall mutually assist one another in the implementation of this Section through exchange of information at bilateral level, in particular for the purpose of verifying the status of a vehicle, including the verification of its registration status in the Member State in which it was previously registered.

2. Where appropriate, competent authorities of Member States shall also cooperate with administrative authorities from third countries. Such cooperation may include sharing of relevant information, conducting joint inspections, and other forms of mutual assistance as deemed necessary to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing the export of used vehicles.

3. Customs authorities and competent authorities of Member States shall cooperate in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and exchange information necessary for the fulfilment of their functions under this Regulation, including via electronic means. The customs authorities may communicate, in accordance with Article 12(1) and Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, confidential information acquired by the customs authorities in the course of performing their duties, or provided to the customs authorities on a confidential basis, to the competent authority of the Member State where the operator or trader is established.

4. Where the competent authorities have received information in accordance with paragraphs 1 to 3 of this Article, those competent authorities may communicate that information to competent authorities from other Member States.

5. Risk-related information shall be exchanged as follows:
(a)between customs authorities in accordance with Article 46(5) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)between customs authorities and the Commission in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)between customs authorities and competent authorities, including competent authorities from other Member States, in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework for cooperation and exchange of information among Member State authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities regarding the verification and export of used vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly recommends implementing a harmonised vehicle registration and de-registration system within the EU, noting that national authorities are not obliged to report on export/import of used vehicles and that such information is necessary to validate ELV treatment numbers and prevent loopholes. Article 44 directly responds to this by establishing mandatory cooperation and information exchange between Member State authorities (including verification of registration status) and with third-country authorities regarding used vehicle exports, which is precisely the cross-border information-sharing mechanism the organisation advocated for.

article_title: Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
article_text:
Article 39 — Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
1. Before releasing used vehicles for export, customs shall verify electronically and automatically via the electronic systems referred to in Article 45, that based on the Vehicle Identification Number and the information on the Member State of last registration, the vehicle is considered roadworthy in accordance with Article 38(3), point (b).

2. Where the information provided or made available to customs does not correspond to the information in the national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness pursuant to paragraph 1, customs authorities shall not release that vehicle for export and shall inform the economic operator concerned thereof through these systems.
provision_effect: Requires customs to electronically verify, via Vehicle Identification Number and national registers, that used vehicles are roadworthy before releasing them for export, and to block export and notify the operator if the information does not match.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for improved registration/de-registration systems to track used vehicle exports and for using roadworthiness tests as the practical distinction between used cars and ELVs. The provision directly acts on both points: it mandates automated cross-checking of roadworthiness status via national registers before export clearance, which is precisely the kind of harmonised, export-linked verification mechanism the organisation called for to prevent unknown ELV whereabouts and enforce the used car/ELV distinction.

article_text:
Article 40 — Risk management and customs controls
1. For the purpose of enforcing the provisions laid down in Article 38, customs authorities shall carry out controls on used vehicles to be exported in accordance with Articles 46 and 47 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013. Without prejudice to Article 39, such controls shall primarily be based on risk analysis, as established in Article 46(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

2. In addition to the risk management referred to in paragraph 1, once the interconnection referred to in Article 45 is operational, customs shall use these electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1) to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with specific conditions linked to the protection of the environment or road safety in accordance with paragraph 3 of this Article.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to supplement this Regulation by setting out the compliance conditions referred to in paragraph 2, including specific conditions applied to the import of used vehicles by the third country of import linked to the protection of the environment and road safety, when such conditions have been notified by that third country to the Commission. Those conditions shall be verifiable against the information available in the electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1).
provision_effect: Establishes risk-based customs controls on used vehicles for export and empowers the Commission to set compliance conditions linked to environmental protection and road safety using interconnected electronic systems.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a harmonised EU vehicle registration/de-registration system and better tracking of used vehicle exports/imports to prevent environmental loopholes and unknown ELV whereabouts. Article 40 directly responds to this by mandating customs controls on exported used vehicles using interconnected electronic systems and allowing compliance conditions tied to environmental protection — concrete action in the same direction the org advocated for (better oversight and tracking of used vehicle exports to close environmental and regulatory gaps).

context_after: introduced in Italy with the effect that only a car which has passed such a roadworthiness test can be exported to another country. GRC strongly recommends such an obligation to be included in the ELV Directive to distinguish ELVs from used cars and prevent this substantial loophole or at the least, to include proper definitions into the ELV Directive framework. Improvement in the information exchange about the fate of temporary de-registered vehicles GRC strongly supports that temporary de-registrations must be accompanied by information about the fate of the vehicle to prevent abuses. Furthermore, not distinguishing between final and temporarily de-registered vehicles causes uncertainty and leads to statistical gaps, especially when different authorities to not have the necessary data and cannot determine the ELV whereabouts. The application of a tax (reduced) while the vehicle is on temporary leave, can help to surface its reality. Emphasize the important role of insurers in case of total loss Insurance companies should play a more important in the enforcement of the ELV Directive. When a car accident results in a “total loss” of the vehicle as declared by an insurance company, such as totally destroyed vehicles due to an accident, the vehicle must be automatically classified as an ELV to contribute to a better enforcement of the ELV Directive
article_title: Distinction between used vehicles and end-of-life vehicles
article_text:
Article 37 — Distinction between used vehicles and end-of-life vehicles
1. For the purpose of transferring ownership of a used vehicle, the vehicle owner shall be able to demonstrate to any natural or legal person interested in acquiring ownership of the concerned vehicle or to the competent authorities that the vehicle is not an end-of-life vehicle. When assessing the status of a used vehicle, the vehicle owner, other economic operators and competent authorities shall verify if the criteria laid down in Annex I are met in order to determine whether it is not an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework requiring vehicle owners to demonstrate a vehicle is not an end-of-life vehicle when transferring ownership, using criteria in Annex I to distinguish used vehicles from end-of-life vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a clear and easy-to-implement distinction between used cars and ELVs, and the provision directly establishes such a distinction mechanism by requiring verification against specific criteria (Annex I) to determine whether a vehicle is an ELV or a used vehicle. The provision takes concrete action in the exact direction the organisation advocated for.

context_after: introduced in Italy with the effect that only a car which has passed such a roadworthiness test can be exported to another country. GRC strongly recommends such an obligation to be included in the ELV Directive to distinguish ELVs from used cars and prevent this substantial loophole or at the least, to include proper definitions into the ELV Directive framework. Improvement in the information exchange about the fate of temporary de-registered vehicles GRC strongly supports that temporary de-registrations must be accompanied by information about the fate of the vehicle to prevent abuses. Furthermore, not distinguishing between final and temporarily de-registered vehicles causes uncertainty and leads to statistical gaps, especially when different authorities to not have the necessary data and cannot determine the ELV whereabouts. The application of a tax (reduced) while the vehicle is on temporary leave, can help to surface its reality. Emphasize the important role of insurers in case of total loss Insurance companies should play a more important in the enforcement of the ELV Directive. When a car accident results in a “total loss” of the vehicle as declared by an insurance company, such as totally destroyed vehicles due to an accident, the vehicle must be automatically classified as an ELV to contribute to a better enforcement of the ELV Directive
article_title: Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
article_text:
Article 39 — Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
1. Before releasing used vehicles for export, customs shall verify electronically and automatically via the electronic systems referred to in Article 45, that based on the Vehicle Identification Number and the information on the Member State of last registration, the vehicle is considered roadworthy in accordance with Article 38(3), point (b).

2. Where the information provided or made available to customs does not correspond to the information in the national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness pursuant to paragraph 1, customs authorities shall not release that vehicle for export and shall inform the economic operator concerned thereof through these systems.
provision_effect: Requires customs to automatically verify via electronic systems whether a used vehicle is roadworthy (based on VIN and Member State of last registration) before releasing it for export, and to block export if the information does not match national registers.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for using roadworthiness/technical control tests as the practical mechanism to distinguish used cars from ELVs. The provision directly implements this logic by making roadworthiness status (verified electronically against national registers) the gating criterion for export release, which is precisely the distinction the org called for and described as easy to implement.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6820362 - 17/11/2020 Stand: 17.11.2020 Stellungnahme der WV Stahl zur Folgenabschätzung in der Anfangsphase Revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles Ref. Ares(2020)5755999 - 22/10/2020 Die Wirtschaftsvereinigung Stahl begrüßt die Anstrengungen der Kommission, die Altfahr- zeugdirektive, auch im Hinblick auf die Anforderungen durch die Elektromobilität, zu reformie- ren. Die Kommission betrachtet dabei drei Optionen: • Verbesserung der Umsetzung und Durchsetzung der Altfahrzeugrichtlinie, ohne ihre Bestimmungen zu ändern • Gezielte Änderung der Altfahrzeugrichtlinie durch Anpassung an die EU-Abfallgesetz- gebung • Überarbeitung der Altfahrzeugrichtlinie durch weitreichende Änderungen, die den Au- tomobilsektor verändern und vollständig kreiswirtschaftsfähig machen sollen Die Europäische Richtlinie über Altfahrzeuge (ELV) hat sich im Laufe der Jahre als wichtiges Instrument zur Nachweis einer ordnungsgemäßen Kreislaufwirtschaft erwiesen, durch die Fahrzeuge und ihre Teile an ihrem Lebensende in den Materialkreislauf zurückgeführt werden sollen. Aufgrund neuer technischer und sozialer Entwicklungen ist es wichtig, sie in einer ge- eigneten Form zu überarbeiten, die die Herausforderungen zukünftiger Mobilitätskonzepte und den Anspruch an eine zirkuläre Gesellschaft berücksichtigt. Dies hängt auch mit der durch die Elektromobilität künftig veränderten Materialzusammensetzung von Fahrzeugen zusammen
context_after: Dies hängt auch mit der durch die Elektromobilität künftig veränderten Materialzusammensetzung von Fahrzeugen zusammen. Die ELV-Richtlinie wurde im Jahr 2000 als Instrument der Abfallwirtschaft geschaffen, um auf die Bedürfnisse einer ordnungsgemäßen Entsorgung von Altfahrzeugen einzugehen. Die Festlegung von Zielen für das gesamte Fahrzeug war bei der Entstehung der ELV ein wirk- sames Instrument zur Förderung der Verwertung und des Recyclings. Die erreichten Recyc- lingraten haben diesen Ansatz bestätigt, allerdings in einer Form, dass in der Gesamtbilanz die gut recycelbaren Materialen geholfen haben, die Gesamtquoten zu erfüllen, ohne dass das Recycling schlecht recycelbarer Materialien vorangetrieben wurde. Durch die aktuell anstehenden Veränderungen im Fahrzeugsektor – insbesondere die Elekt- romobilität – verändern sich die Materialanteile im Fahrzeug stark. Dies liegt hauptsächlich an der schweren Batterie, die bisher als vollständig recycelbar bewertet wird, aber auch am Einsatz leichterer, jedoch schlechter recycelbarer Materialien. Allein der Massenanteil der Batterie und der Metalle, insbesondere Stahl, erlauben es, den Anteil der schlechter oder nicht recycelbaren Materialien zu steigern, ohne dass die Gesamtrecyclingquote unterschrit- ten wird. Eine tatsächliche Einsparung natürlicher Ressourcen, wie sie durch permanente Stoffe bzw. das Multirecycling von Stahl aber auch durch z. B. zirkuläres Design, effizientere Werkstoffe (z. B
article_text:
Article 9 — Circularity strategy
1. For each vehicle type that is type-approved under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], the manufacturer shall draw up a circularity strategy.

2. The circularity strategy shall describe which actions the manufacturers will take to follow-up on their obligations to ensure that the circularity requirements in Chapter II, which are verified in the type-approval procedures and which are applicable to the vehicle type concerned, are met.

3. The circularity strategy shall contain the elements laid down in Part A of Annex IV.

4. The manufacturer shall provide a copy of the circularity strategy to the Commission within 30 days after the type-approval for the concerned vehicle type has been granted.

5. The manufacturer shall monitor and follow up on the actions contained in the circularity strategy and update the strategy every five years in accordance with Part B of Annex IV. The updated circularity strategy shall be provided to the type-approval authority that issued the type-approval for the vehicle type and to the Commisssion.

6. The Commission shall make the circularity strategies and any updates to those strategies publicly available, except for confidential information.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Part B of Annex IV by adapting the requirements on the content of the circularity strategy and the updates to that strategy to technical and scientific progress in vehicle manufacturing and management of end-of-life vehicles, to market developments in the automotive sector and to regulatory changes.

8. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 83 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] and every six years thereafter, the Commission shall draw up and publish a report on the circularity of the automotive sector. The report shall be based in particular on circularity strategies and updates to such strategies.
provision_effect: Requires vehicle manufacturers to draw up, submit, monitor, and publicly disclose a circularity strategy for each type-approved vehicle type, with periodic updates and Commission reporting on automotive sector circularity.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text expresses only a general welcome for the Commission's efforts to reform the ELV Directive and acknowledges the need for revision to address future mobility and circular economy challenges, without taking any specific directional stance on manufacturer circularity strategies, reporting obligations, or public disclosure requirements. This is a general endorsement without a specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against the provision's concrete requirements.

source_text:
To optimize the effect of the Directive we wish to draw the Commission’s attention to – 

·	Legal national barriers to reuse / recycling / recovery (as we see in our country)

·	The lack of norms and standards regarding transport, storing, dismantling, and general handling of high voltage batteries in EV’s in consideration of the implications for road safety (transport), environment and working conditions

·	In accordance with the spirit of EU Regulation 2018/858 the ELV Directive should oblige car manufacturers, in clear terms, to ensure ATF’s and other relevant companies access to technical data, types of materials used, etc.

·	Make it a requirement that also components such as ECU’s and other electronic devices can be unlocked and re-coded for use in other identical vehicles

·	Contribute to a harmonization of rules of registration, deregistration, options for export/import, etc. to ensure higher transparency as to the destiny of the individual car as a means to prevent illegal scrapping.

November 2020 DANSK AUTOGENBRUG
context_before:
Dansk Autogenbrug (DAG) – representing the Danish auto dismantlers – is pleased to comment on the Commission’s plan and thoughts for a revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles.

On the whole, we fully agree to the aims and measures foreseen in the Impact Assessment of October, 2020.

Particularly, we are delighted that – 

·	One key point is the aim to promote more circular business models by linking design issues to end-of-life treatment

·	A revised ELV Directive will seek coherence with related directives and regulations, such as the Batteries Directive, the Waste Framework Directive, etc.

·	The Commission will be focusing on “missing vehicles”, since they represent an environment problem on the one hand and, on the other, a problem of unfair competition to ATF’s– 

·	A revised ELV Directive would increase the EU market for recyclates and secondary raw materials so as to create a larger degree of profitability for ATF’s and shredding companies when extracting further components/materials from ELV’s – 

·	The Commission acknowledges that ATF’s are among those “at the core of the circular economy” – 

·	The Commission is aware that “most countries” are currently not actively enforcing public inspections –


To optimize the effect of the Directive we wish to draw the Commission’s attention to – 

·	Legal national barriers to reuse / recycling / recovery (as we see in our country)
article_title: Information on removal and replacement of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers to provide waste management and repair operators with unrestricted, standardised, non-discriminatory access to information enabling safe removal and replacement of key vehicle parts (including EV batteries, e-drive motors, critical raw materials, and digitally coded components), and mandates cooperation through communication platforms.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly called for the ELV Directive to oblige car manufacturers to ensure ATFs and other relevant companies access to technical data and types of materials used (mirroring Article 11's information access requirement), and specifically requested that components such as ECUs and other electronic devices can be unlocked and re-coded for use in other identical vehicles (directly mirrored by Article 11(1)(f) on digitally coded components). The provision delivers both of these specific asks.

article_text:
Article 8 — General obligations
1. Manufacturers shall demonstrate that new vehicles that they have manufactured and that are placed on the market, are type-approved in accordance with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2018/858 and of this Regulation.

2. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements in Articles 4, 5, 6 or 7, the manufacturer shall provide the documentation showing compliance with those requirements and shall:
(a)include it in the information folder referred to in Article 24 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858; and
(b)submit it to the type-approval authority in accordance with Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

3. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirement in Article 9 applies, the manufacturer shall submit the circularity strategy to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

4. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 10 apply, the manufacturer shall draw up the information referred to in Article 10(1) and submit it, in accordance with Article 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.

5. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 11 apply, the manufacturer shall submit the declaration confirming compliance with the requirement set out in Article 11(1), in accordance with 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes general obligations for manufacturers to demonstrate type-approval compliance and submit documentation, circularity strategies, and declarations to type-approval authorities for vehicles placed on the market.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that the ELV Directive should oblige car manufacturers, in clear terms (and "in accordance with the spirit of EU Regulation 2018/858"), to ensure ATFs and other relevant companies access to technical data and materials information. Article 8 directly operationalises manufacturer obligations under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 by requiring manufacturers to submit documentation on compliance, circularity strategies, and material information to type-approval authorities — concrete action in the direction the org advocated for (binding manufacturer obligations tied to 2018/858).

article_title: Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
article_text:
Article 12 — Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
1. Manufacturers and their suppliers shall use the nomenclature of the component and material coding standards listed in points 1 to 3 of Annex VI for the labelling and identification of parts, components and materials of vehicles.

2. Manufacturers shall ensure that e-drive motors containing permanent magnets bear a conspicuous, clearly legible and indelible label indicating the information listed in point 4 of Annex VI.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VI in order to adapt it to technical and scientific progress.
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers and suppliers to use standardised nomenclature for labelling and identifying parts, components and materials in vehicles, and mandates specific labelling for e-drive motors containing permanent magnets.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Dansk Autogenbrug explicitly calls for obliging car manufacturers to ensure ATFs and other relevant companies have access to technical data and types of materials used. Article 12's mandatory labelling and identification standards for parts, components and materials directly responds to this ask by requiring standardised, accessible material/component information on vehicles, moving in the same direction the organisation advocated for.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6859938 - 18/11/2020 Comment on the roadmap by the European Commission to revise the End-of- Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive Berlin, November 2020 Contact person: Michael Püschner Head of Competence Center for Environmental Policy michael.pueschner@vda.de In General The Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) represents more than 600 companies in the automotive industry -manufacturers of motor vehicles and their engines, set-ups, installations, containers and vehicle parts and equipment -which produce in Germany. The automotive industry is the highest-turnover branch and generated a revenue of more than 435 billion euros in 2019. Approximately 833,000 employees manufactured about 4.7 million cars in Germany -from 16 million cars worldwide. This does not include commercial vehicles (trucks and buses) produced by our member companies. Together we research and produce for a clean, safe, and sustainable mobility of the future. The German automotive industry is fully aware of its responsibility throughout the entire cycle of a car’s production and use. Therefore, the VDA supports the goal of the European Commission to reduce waste and foster the reuse of resources. All political initiatives should be directly linked to the primary goal of the European Union to achieve climate neutrality until 2050. Overall, the current ELV Directive is a success story. Only slight improvement is needed to sustain the Directive for future requirements
context_after: Overall, the current ELV Directive is a success story. Only slight improvement is needed to sustain the Directive for future requirements. The VDA will support this process. The automotive industry is pioneer for circular economy Due to the ELV Directive a high-quality, certified take-back and recycling network for ELVs has been established in Europe by car manufactures and its business partners. In this network take- back is free of charge for the last holder and an environment friendly treatment of ELVs is guaranteed. The products of car manufacturers and suppliers are pioneer for circular economy and are benchmark for other industries due to ▪ high amounts of recycled steel and NF metals in new vehicles, ▪ established remanufacturing processes with high volumes, ▪ very good reparability, ▪ longevity of their products and ▪ significant reduction of hazardous materials.

label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text expresses only general support for the Commission's goal to reduce waste and foster resource reuse, and a broad view that the current ELV Directive needs only "slight improvement." There is no specific advocacy position on whether a mandatory circularity strategy mechanism should or should not exist, nor any directional stance on its content, scope, or procedural requirements that could be evaluated against this provision.

source_text: Comment on the roadmap by the European Commission to revise the End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive 2 Guiding principle for the ELV-revision The revision of the ELV Directive should reinforce its existing strengths, should foster innovations, and should trust in existing market mechanism. 1. Passenger cars are no ordinary consumer products Passenger cars are different from other consumer products due to their complexity and longevity. A vehicle consists of about 5.000 to 7.000 components and a multiple subcomponents. It is 15 to 22 years in use while being exposed to a temperature range of 100°C for the car interior, guarantee crash safety, flame retardancy and high material stress during the whole lifetime. 2. The ELV Directive should focus on passenger cars The heavy-duty vehicle (HDV) should remain outside the scope of the ELV Directive. Unlike in the passenger car segment, there are no ‘standard’ vehicle models in the HDV segment. 3. The ELV Directive should cover the different requirements by the member states Waste systems are designed by national authorities and are valid within the limits of the national borders. There is no European economic area for waste and one-size-fits all solution might be difficult. Instead, future ELV legislation should be flexible regarding national requirements and status (e.g. population density, number of collection points, decentralized take-back and recycling networks and different age of ELV in member states). 4
context_before: Overall, the current ELV Directive is a success story. Only slight improvement is needed to sustain the Directive for future requirements. The VDA will support this process. The automotive industry is pioneer for circular economy Due to the ELV Directive a high-quality, certified take-back and recycling network for ELVs has been established in Europe by car manufactures and its business partners. In this network take- back is free of charge for the last holder and an environment friendly treatment of ELVs is guaranteed. The products of car manufacturers and suppliers are pioneer for circular economy and are benchmark for other industries due to ▪ high amounts of recycled steel and NF metals in new vehicles, ▪ established remanufacturing processes with high volumes, ▪ very good reparability, ▪ longevity of their products and ▪ significant reduction of hazardous materials.
context_after: population density, number of collection points, decentralized take-back and recycling networks and different age of ELV in member states). 4. The traction battery is crucial for a self-sustaining business model in future Take-back and treatment of ELVs is a self-sustaining business model today. The value of an ELV sufficiently covers the cost of all necessary treatment steps throughout the entire recycling value chain. Raw materials are highly valuable goods. Therefore, automotive manufactures have already established closed loops of materials, re-use, and re-manufacturing applications. This trend will be enhanced in the next years and will be boosted if the traction battery is – in a legal perspective of the ELV Directive – a component of the vehicle. 5. A future-proof recycling system by digitizing deregistration and certificate of destruction Key factor for an effective governance of the ELV is the obligatory submission of a certificate of destruction for final deregistration and its consequent control. Deregistration and submission of a certificate of destruction is mainly an analogue and time-consuming process with a high error rate. Digitizing these processes and certificates has enormous potential for innovation and business. It is key for a better enforcement of existing legislation by preventing unauthorized operators and illegal recycling activities
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to review and report on the application of the Regulation, including an evaluation of whether to extend its scope to additional vehicle categories (L3e–L7e, M2, M3, N2, N3, O) beyond passenger cars.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: VDA explicitly advocates that heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs) should remain outside the scope of the ELV Directive, arguing that the regulation should focus on passenger cars. Article 55(2)(a) directly addresses this by requiring the Commission to evaluate the need to extend the scope to additional vehicle categories (including heavy-duty categories M2, M3, N2, N3), meaning the question of HDV inclusion is subject to a future evidence-based review rather than being mandated immediately — which aligns with VDA's position that HDVs should not be brought into scope.

The ELV Directive should cover the different requirements by the member states Waste systems are designed by national authorities and are valid within the limits of the national borders. There is no European economic area for waste and one-size-fits all solution might be difficult. Instead, future ELV legislation should be flexible regarding national requirements and status (e.g. population density, number of collection points, decentralized take-back and recycling networks and different age of ELV in member states). 4
context_before: Overall, the current ELV Directive is a success story. Only slight improvement is needed to sustain the Directive for future requirements. The VDA will support this process. The automotive industry is pioneer for circular economy Due to the ELV Directive a high-quality, certified take-back and recycling network for ELVs has been established in Europe by car manufactures and its business partners. In this network take- back is free of charge for the last holder and an environment friendly treatment of ELVs is guaranteed. The products of car manufacturers and suppliers are pioneer for circular economy and are benchmark for other industries due to ▪ high amounts of recycled steel and NF metals in new vehicles, ▪ established remanufacturing processes with high volumes, ▪ very good reparability, ▪ longevity of their products and ▪ significant reduction of hazardous materials.
context_after: population density, number of collection points, decentralized take-back and recycling networks and different age of ELV in member states). 4. The traction battery is crucial for a self-sustaining business model in future Take-back and treatment of ELVs is a self-sustaining business model today. The value of an ELV sufficiently covers the cost of all necessary treatment steps throughout the entire recycling value chain. Raw materials are highly valuable goods. Therefore, automotive manufactures have already established closed loops of materials, re-use, and re-manufacturing applications. This trend will be enhanced in the next years and will be boosted if the traction battery is – in a legal perspective of the ELV Directive – a component of the vehicle. 5. A future-proof recycling system by digitizing deregistration and certificate of destruction Key factor for an effective governance of the ELV is the obligatory submission of a certificate of destruction for final deregistration and its consequent control. Deregistration and submission of a certificate of destruction is mainly an analogue and time-consuming process with a high error rate. Digitizing these processes and certificates has enormous potential for innovation and business. It is key for a better enforcement of existing legislation by preventing unauthorized operators and illegal recycling activities
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory circularity vehicle passport for each vehicle placed on the market, containing environmental and circularity information in a digital, interoperable, and machine-readable format, with the manufacturer responsible for its accuracy.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The VDA's position text focuses on guiding principles for the ELV Directive revision — such as scope (excluding HDVs), flexibility for national requirements, and the complexity of passenger cars — but contains no specific advocacy position on the creation or design of a circularity vehicle passport. There is no directional stance expressed regarding digital passports, interoperability requirements, or manufacturer information obligations that would allow a meaningful comparison with Article 13.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6863089 - 18/11/2020 Feedback Belgium Inception Impact Assessment Revision ELV Directive We want to stress that the unknown whereabouts is the most important improvement to make. Introducing a digital vehicle passport linked to the registration/deregistration of vehicles is very important to tackle the problem of the unknown whereabouts. The lack of traceability of vehicles hampers the collection of all ELVs because we don’t know who the holders/owners of vehicles to be considered as ELV are. It should not be possible for registration authorities to make a distinction between deregistration and final deregistration and not have a trace of the owner after (a not final) deregistration or export. When there is a full and digital traceability obliged and done, we would know the holder/owner and would be able to automatically stimulate or oblige them to deliver a vehicle that has become an ELV to an ATF. Therefore, also an objective definition of ELVs is needed. Only then, a digital and automatic system needed to enforce the collection of all ELV, could take place. We can share our good experience in Belgium with defining ELVs in a more objective manner. ELVs are defined based on the data of the documents of the vehicles, legally it’s a further defining of “must discard” of the general waste definition
context_after: ELVs are defined based on the data of the documents of the vehicles, legally it’s a further defining of “must discard” of the general waste definition. When a vehicle meets one of the following criteria, it’s considered as an ELV: a) a vehicle becomes a waste when the holder/owner cannot provide a valid registration and/or a valid technical inspection document within a month (for vehicles with a registration and inspection obligation); b) the validity of the technical inspection has expired for two years (when there is an obligation to have a technical inspection for the vehicle); c) the vehicle is 6 years old and never had a technical inspection (when there is an obligation to have a technical inspection for this vehicle); d) the vehicle is declared as technical total loss by an automobile expert. If the vehicle fulfils one of these criteria and is thus an ELV, it has to be delivered to an ATF within: a) one month after the period of one month to show the valid registration and valid technical inspection document; b) two years after the last validation date of the technical inspection; c) 6 years after the first use when the vehicle never had a technical inspection; d) one month after the declaration of technical total loss (unless an official rehabilitation is set up)
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory digital Circularity Vehicle Passport for all vehicles placed on the market, containing vehicle information in an open, interoperable, machine-readable digital format, maintained by the manufacturer and accessible free of charge.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: OVAM explicitly advocates for introducing a digital vehicle passport linked to vehicle registration/deregistration to enable full traceability of vehicles and their owners/holders, directly addressing the problem of unknown whereabouts of ELVs. Article 13 establishes precisely such a mandatory digital vehicle passport with open, interoperable, and machine-readable data requirements, which responds directly to OVAM's specific ask for a digital traceability instrument.

context_after: In addition, we are of the opinion that there should also be objective criteria defining vehicles as waste when the environmental impact of the further use is higher in comparison with the recycling of the vehicle. For example, the further use of a vehicle with EURO standard 3 could have a higher environmental impact than recycling it. Also, age could be a criterion. In Belgium (Flanders) we want to perform a study during 2021 to know when this environmental impact balance is in favour of recycling to know the criteria to define vehicles as ELVs in addition with above criteria. For the issue of export, we are in favour of a European obligation of roadworthiness inspections. Furthermore, coherence with customs legislation is needed to come to an enforceable ELV legislation. Only by also tackling the export problem, the problem of the unknown whereabouts can be tackled. The objective defining of ELVs on criteria such as age or EURO standard as mentioned above is also good enforceable for export.
article_text:
Article 26 — Obligations for the vehicle owner
1. The owner of a vehicle that becomes an end-of-life vehicle shall:
(a)deliver the end-of-life vehicle to an authorised treatment facility or, in cases referred to in Article 23(4), to a collection point, without undue delay after receiving information that the vehicle meets any of the criteria for irreparability laid down in Part A, points 1 and 2, of Annex I;
(b)present a certificate of destruction to the relevant registration authority.
provision_effect: Requires the owner of an end-of-life vehicle to deliver it to an authorised treatment facility (or collection point) without undue delay after receiving information that the vehicle meets irreparability criteria, and to present a certificate of destruction to the registration authority.
label: ALIGNED

Both the org's position and the provision establish a mandatory delivery obligation on the vehicle owner to an ATF upon the vehicle meeting ELV/irreparability criteria, making the alignment clear and specific.
