article_title: Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
article_text:
Article 19 — Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
1. A producer, in the case of individual fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, and producer responsibility organisations appointed in the case of collective fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, shall apply for an authorisation from the competent authority.

2. The authorisation shall be granted only where it is demonstrated that the requirements laid down in Article 8a(3), points (a) to (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC are complied with and the measures put in place by the producer or producer responsibility organisation are sufficient to meet the obligations set out in this Chapter with regard to the number of vehicles made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State by the producer or producers on whose behalf the producer responsibility organisation acts.

3. Member States shall, in their measures laying down administrative and procedural rules referred to in Article 14(3), point (b), include the details of the authorisation procedure, which may differ according to whether it relates to individual or collective fulfilment of the extended producer responsibility obligations, and the modalities for verifying compliance of producers or producer responsibility organisations, including the information to be provided by producers or producers responsibility organisations to that end.

4. The producer or the producer responsibility organisations shall notify the competent authority without undue delay of any changes to the information contained in the authorisation, of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation or of the permanent cessation of operations.

5. The self-control mechanism provided for in 8a(3), point (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC shall be carried out regularly, and at least every 3 years, and upon the request by the competent authority, in order to verify that the provisions in that point are complied with and the conditions for authorisation referred to in paragraph 2 continue to be met. The producer or the producer responsibility organisation shall, upon request, present a self-control report and, where necessary, the draft corrective action plan to the competent authority. Without prejudice to the competencies under paragraph 6, the competent authority may make observations on the self-control report and on the draft corrective action plan, and shall communicate any such observations to the producer or the producer responsibility organisation. The producer or producer responsibility organisation shall draw up and implement the corrective action plan based on those observations.

6. The competent authority may decide to revoke the authorisation if the producer or producer responsibility organisation no longer fulfils the requirements with regard to the organisation of the collection and treatment of end-of-life, fails in relation to reporting to the competent authority, fails to notify the competent authority of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation, or has ceased operations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory authorisation system for producers and producer responsibility organisations fulfilling extended producer responsibility (EPR) obligations, including compliance verification, self-control mechanisms, and revocation powers for competent authorities.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for defining and regulating the extended producer responsibility (EPR) scope in legislation, including collection, transport, and treatment of ELVs and their components. The provision directly establishes a concrete EPR authorisation and compliance framework — including self-control mechanisms and oversight by competent authorities — which responds to the organisation's call for a clearly defined and enforceable EPR system with mechanisms to detect failures and apply corrections.

Article 20 directly establishes that producer financial contributions must cover collection and treatment costs not already offset by waste management operator revenues (i.e., when net value is negative), which aligns with the org's specific ask that producers bear costs when treatment yields a net negative outcome.

article_title: Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
article_text:
Article 7 — Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
1. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] shall be designed in a way which does not hinder the removal by authorised treatment facilities of the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the concerned vehicle during the waste phase of the vehicle.

2. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall be designed, as regards joining, fastening and sealing elements, so as to enable, in a readily and non-destructive manner, the removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries and e-drive motors from the vehicle by authorised treatment facilities or repair and maintenance operators during the use phase and waste phase of the vehicle.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend paragraph 2 by revising the list of parts and components that are to be designed for removal and replacement from vehicles, in order to include in that paragraph additional parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, taking into account technical and scientific progress.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts laying down the conditions for the design for removal and replacement of parts and components referred to in paragraph 2 where necessary to ensure harmonised implementation of the obligation set out in paragraph 2.
Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Requires vehicles to be designed so that authorised treatment facilities can remove and replace specific parts and components (including EV batteries and e-drive motors) in a readily and non-destructive manner during both the use and waste phases.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for including new propulsion systems (electric vehicles, hybrids, etc.) within the regulatory scope and for ensuring vehicles are manageable by authorised treatment facilities (ATFs). Article 7 directly responds to this by mandating design requirements that enable non-destructive removal and replacement of EV batteries and e-drive motors by authorised treatment facilities, aligning with the org's push to bring new propulsion technologies under controlled dismantling frameworks.

The provision takes concrete action in the direction the org advocated by broadening the definitional scope of vehicles covered.

article_title: Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
article_text:
Article 22 — Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
1. Where a vehicle becomes an end-of-life vehicle in another Member State than the Member State within whose territory the vehicle was made available on the market for the first time, the producer of that vehicle or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, the producer responsibility organisation shall ensure that the net costs of waste management operations referred to in Article 20 incurred by waste management operators in other Member States are covered.

2. A producer or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, a producer responsibility organisation shall:
(a)designate by a written mandate an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility in each Member State;
(b)establish cross-border cooperation mechanisms with the waste management operators carrying out waste management operations referred to in Article 20.

3. The Member State where the vehicle became an end-of-life vehicle shall monitor producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’ compliance with paragraphs 1 and 2. The monitoring shall be based on the information reported and verified by producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’, to the competent authorities on the implementation of paragraphs 1 and 2, in particular on the calculation and allocation of costs for the management of end-of-life vehicles referred to in paragraph 1, with due regard for business confidentiality and other concerns regarding competitiveness.

4. Where necessary to ensure compliance with this Article and avoid distortion of the single market, the Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 this Regulation by laying down detailed rules on the obligations of the producers, Member States and waste management operators and the features of the mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a cross-border cost allocation mechanism requiring producers or producer responsibility organisations to cover net waste management costs when a vehicle becomes an end-of-life vehicle in a Member State other than where it was first placed on the market.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates that producers must bear the costs of ELV treatment, including when the global calculation is negative and in sectors where treatment costs exceed recycling benefits, and that Member States should be able to adopt financing mechanisms to cover treatment costs not covered by producers. Article 22 directly responds to this by mandating that producers (or their PROs) cover the net costs of waste management operations incurred by waste management operators across Member States, establishing a concrete cost-coverage obligation on producers in cross-border scenarios — precisely the direction the organisation advocated for.

article_text:
Article 17 — Register of producers
1. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 35 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall establish a register of producers which shall serve to monitor compliance of producers with the requirements of this Chapter.
The register shall provide links to other national registers of producers’ websites to facilitate, in all Member States, registration of producers or appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

2. Producers shall register in the register referred to in paragraph 1. They shall to that end submit an application for registration in each Member State where they make a vehicle available on the market for the first time.
Producers shall submit the application for registration via an electronic data-processing system as referred to in paragraph 8, point (a).
Producers shall only make available vehicles on the market of a Member State, if they or, in case of authorisation, their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility, are registered in such Member State.

3. The application for registration shall include the information listed in Annex VIII. Member States may request additional information or documents, as necessary, to use the register of producers in an efficient manner.

4. By way of derogation from paragraph 3, the information referred to in point 1 (d) of Annex VIII shall be provided either in the application for registration under paragraph 3 or in the application for authorisation under Article 19.

5. Where a producer has appointed a producer responsibility organisation in accordance with Article 18, the obligations under this Article shall be met by that organisation mutatis mutandis unless otherwise specified by the Member State in which the vehicle has been made available on the market for the first time.

6. The obligations under this Article may be fulfilled on a producer’s behalf by an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility.

7. Member States may decide that the registration procedure pursuant to this Article and the authorisation procedure pursuant to Article 19 constitute a single procedure, provided that the application for authorisation meets the requirements set out in paragraphs 3 to 6 of this Article.

8. The competent authority shall:
(a)make available on its website information about the application process via an electronic data-processing system;
(b)grant registrations and provide a registration number within a maximum period of 12 weeks from the moment that all the information required under paragraphs 2 and 3 is provided.

9. The competent authority may:
(a)lay down modalities with regard to the requirements and process of registration without adding substantive requirements to those laid down in paragraphs 2 and 3;
(b)charge cost-based and proportionate fees to producers for the processing of the applications referred to in paragraph 2.

10. The competent authority may refuse to register a producer or withdraw the producer’s registration where the information referred to in paragraph 3 and related documentary evidence are not provided or are not sufficient, or where the producer no longer meets the requirements laid down in Annex VIII, point 1 (d).
The competent authority shall withdraw the producer’s registration if the producer has ceased to exist.

11. The producer, or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation appointed on behalf of the producers it represents shall without undue delay notify the competent authority of any changes to the information contained in the registration and of any permanent cessation as regards the making available on the market within the territory of the Member State of the vehicles referred to in the registration.

12. The producer or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation shall report to the competent authority responsible for the register on the performance of extended producer responsibility obligations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory national register of producers under extended producer responsibility, requiring producers to register before placing vehicles on the market and to report compliance information to competent authorities.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation advocates for registration and traceability controls over operators handling damaged/end-of-life vehicles (repairers, storage facilities, insurers, auction companies), which shares a thematic connection with the provision's establishment of a producer register. However, the provision specifically targets vehicle producers under extended producer responsibility obligations, not the downstream operators (repairers, storage facilities, insurers) that the organisation is concerned about, making it genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply bypasses the organisation's specific asks.

label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation advocates for specific control, registration, and traceability mechanisms over damaged vehicle operators and storage facilities, which relates to the oversight and registration functions assigned to competent authorities under this article. However, the provision focuses on the institutional designation and administrative setup of competent authorities, not on the specific substantive controls the organisation requests (e.g., repair vehicle registration, facility waterproofing standards, traceability of damaged vehicles). There is a real connection — the competent authorities designated here would be the bodies enforcing the controls the org seeks — but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the org's specific asks.

source_text: Vehicle missing report 1.- Introduction. As established in Royal Decree 1383/2002, of December 20, the Authorized Treatment Centers (ATFs) of End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs) are the only facilities capable of receiving, decontaminating and eliminating ELV . Royal Decree 20/2017, of January 20, reinforces this obligation, establishing in its .3 a) second paragraph the following: "The extraction of parts and components for their preparation for reuse and marketing can only be carried out in an ATF ...". During the validity of the previous Royal Decree, the controlled decontamination processes of the ELVs carried out by the authorized treatment centers (ATF) have guaranteed the correct extraction of hazardous waste from vehicles and the disassembly of their components in perfect condition (reusable parts ), tasks that were complemented by the telematic annotation of the definitive deregistrations of vehicles in the registry of the General Directorate of Traffic (DGT). Instruction 16 / V-122 of the General Directorate of Traffic extended the obligation to deal in an ATFs, in order to process their definitive deregistration, to other vehicles (mopeds, industrial, agricultural, etc.) that were not included in the RD 1383/2002

article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory obligations for authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) regarding the acceptance, storage, depollution, dismantling, and treatment of end-of-life vehicles and their parts, in compliance with specific technical and environmental requirements.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation's text explicitly advocates for ATFs as the sole authorised facilities for receiving, decontaminating, and treating ELVs, and highlights the importance of controlled decontamination and dismantling processes carried out by ATFs. The provision directly reinforces this by establishing binding obligations on ATFs for the proper treatment, depollution, and dismantling of ELVs, acting in the same direction the organisation advocates — formalising and strengthening the central role of ATFs in ELV treatment at EU level.

article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to depollute end-of-life vehicles as soon as possible after delivery, including separate collection of fluids, removal of hazardous substances, battery removal, and documentation of the depollution process.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation's text emphasises that only Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) are legally empowered to receive, decontaminate, and process end-of-life vehicles, and highlights the importance of controlled decontamination processes carried out by ATFs to ensure correct extraction of hazardous waste. Article 29 directly reinforces this position by mandating that authorised treatment facilities perform depollution of ELVs — including removal of fluids, hazardous substances, and batteries — in compliance with specific minimum requirements, which aligns with the organisation's advocacy for formalised, ATF-led depollution obligations.

source_text: Vehicle missing report 1.- Introduction. As established in Royal Decree 1383/2002, of December 20, the Authorized Treatment Centers (ATFs) of End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs) are the only facilities capable of receiving, decontaminating and eliminating ELV . Royal Decree 20/2017, of January 20, reinforces this obligation, establishing in its .3 a) second paragraph the following: "The extraction of parts and components for their preparation for reuse and marketing can only be carried out in an ATF ...". During the validity of the previous Royal Decree, the controlled decontamination processes of the ELVs carried out by the authorized treatment centers (ATF) have guaranteed the correct extraction of hazardous waste from vehicles and the disassembly of their components in perfect condition (reusable parts ), tasks that were complemented by the telematic annotation of the definitive deregistrations of vehicles in the registry of the General Directorate of Traffic (DGT). Instruction 16 / V-122 of the General Directorate of Traffic extended the obligation to deal in an ATFs, in order to process their definitive deregistration, to other vehicles (mopeds, industrial, agricultural, etc.) that were not included in the RD 1383/2002

article_title: Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
article_text:
Article 30 — Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, are removed from an end-of-life vehicle prior to shredding, after the depollution operations referred to in Article 29, have been completed.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if an authorised treatment facility demonstrates, that post-shredder technologies separates materials from parts and components listed in Part C, entries 13 to19, of Annex VII, as efficiently as manual dismantling processes or semi-automated disassembly processes.
For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the authorised treatment facility shall provide the information listed in Part G of Annex VII.

3. In addition to the obligations set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall maintain records of the end-of-life vehicles that are processed without the prior removal of parts, components and materials in accordance with paragraph 2, including the name and address of the treatment facilities, and the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the end-of-life vehicles concerned.
The authorised treatment facilities shall provide the information in the records referred to in the first subparagraph to the competent authority in accordance with Article 49(6).
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to mandatorily remove specific parts and components from end-of-life vehicles prior to shredding, with limited exceptions where post-shredder technologies are demonstrated to be equally efficient.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text describes the existing Spanish regulatory framework (Royal Decree 1383/2002 and RD 20/2017) governing Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) and their role in decontaminating and dismantling ELVs, but does not express a specific advocacy position on mandatory pre-shredder removal of parts and components. The text is largely background/descriptive in nature, outlining current legal obligations without taking a directional stance on whether such mandatory removal requirements should be introduced, strengthened, or opposed at EU level.

article_text:
Article 25 — Certificate of destruction
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall issue a certificate of destruction for every treated end-of-life vehicle to the last owner of the end-of-life vehicle. The certificate of destruction shall contain the information listed in Annex IX.

2. The certificate of destruction shall be issued in an electronic format and provided through an electronic notification procedure to the relevant authorities of the Member State, including the competent authorities designated under Article 14.

3. In case the end-of-life vehicle, for which a certificate of destruction has been issued in a Member State, is registered in another Member State, the vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the certificate of destruction was issued shall inform the relevant vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the vehicle is registered that a certificate of destruction has been issued for the vehicle in question.

4. The relevant authorities of a Member State shall cancel the registration of an end-of-life vehicle only after receiving the certificate of destruction for that vehicle.

5. Certificates of destruction issued in a Member State shall be recognised in all other Member States.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to issue electronic certificates of destruction for every treated end-of-life vehicle, with cross-border recognition and mandatory registration cancellation upon receipt.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text is largely descriptive background, summarising existing Spanish national legislation (Royal Decrees and DGT instructions) regarding the role of Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) in handling end-of-life vehicles and their deregistration obligations. It does not express a specific directional advocacy position on the certificate of destruction mechanism, its electronic format, cross-border recognition, or registration cancellation procedures established in Article 25 — it merely describes the current regulatory framework without advocating for or against any particular element of the provision.

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing, labelling, and warranting removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles to determine their fitness for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, recycling, or other treatment, and specifies that parts fit for reuse/remanufacturing/refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation advocates that only Authorised Treatment Centres (ATFs) should carry out the extraction of parts and components for reuse from end-of-life vehicles, emphasising controlled decontamination and proper disassembly processes. Article 31 establishes concrete requirements for the assessment, labelling, and documentation of removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing, or refurbishment — directly regulating the same activity (extraction and preparation of parts for reuse) that the organisation argues should be subject to strict, controlled procedures within authorised facilities. The provision takes concrete action in the direction the organisation advocates by formalising and regulating the process of parts removal and reuse assessment.

source_text: EXPANDED LIABILITY OF THE PRODUCER 1.- Introduction We must be aware of the change that waste has undergone as our technologies have evolved, which without entering into dangerousness, has changed the typology of waste from being simple objects and substances to incorporating complex waste, from the manufacture of products made up of various components (ELV / WEEE), whose products when they reach the end of their useful life, implies the obligation to reuse the components in order to avoid the environmental impact of the manufacture of those same components and get closer to the circular economy. As stated in Directive 53/2000 / CE itself, it is those who manufacture or import them to a member country who are responsible for their products and components when they reach the end of their useful life, although in the case of Spain there is no has officially registered an Expanded Responsibility System (EPR). 2.- Problem Identification All the costs of the management and treatment of ELVs and their components due to the lack of Expanded Responsibility Systems in Spain have fallen since 2003 on recyclers, who have had to pay and financially support their companies from the sale of waste and components with positive value that could be obtained in the treatment. However, this support is no longer possible

article_text:
Article 16 — Extended producer responsibility
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] producers shall have extended producer responsibility for vehicles that they make available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State. The scheme established by producers to exercise that responsibility shall be consistent with Articles 8 and 8a of Directive 2008/98/EC and comply with the requirements of this Chapter.
The extended producer responsibility shall include the obligation for producers to ensure that:
(a)vehicles which they have made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State and which become end-of-life vehicles
(i) collected in accordance with Article 23;
(ii) treated in accordance with Article 27;
(b)the waste management operators treating end-of-life vehicles referred to in point (a) meet the targets laid down in Article 34.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory extended producer responsibility (EPR) for vehicles, requiring producers to ensure end-of-life vehicles are collected and treated according to specified standards and targets.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for the implementation of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) systems for ELVs, criticising the absence of an officially registered EPR scheme in Spain and the resulting financial burden on recyclers. The provision directly establishes a mandatory EPR scheme for vehicle producers, which is precisely what the organisation was calling for.

article_title: Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
article_text:
Article 22 — Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
1. Where a vehicle becomes an end-of-life vehicle in another Member State than the Member State within whose territory the vehicle was made available on the market for the first time, the producer of that vehicle or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, the producer responsibility organisation shall ensure that the net costs of waste management operations referred to in Article 20 incurred by waste management operators in other Member States are covered.

2. A producer or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, a producer responsibility organisation shall:
(a)designate by a written mandate an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility in each Member State;
(b)establish cross-border cooperation mechanisms with the waste management operators carrying out waste management operations referred to in Article 20.

3. The Member State where the vehicle became an end-of-life vehicle shall monitor producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’ compliance with paragraphs 1 and 2. The monitoring shall be based on the information reported and verified by producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’, to the competent authorities on the implementation of paragraphs 1 and 2, in particular on the calculation and allocation of costs for the management of end-of-life vehicles referred to in paragraph 1, with due regard for business confidentiality and other concerns regarding competitiveness.

4. Where necessary to ensure compliance with this Article and avoid distortion of the single market, the Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 this Regulation by laying down detailed rules on the obligations of the producers, Member States and waste management operators and the features of the mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a cross-border cost allocation mechanism requiring producers or producer responsibility organisations to cover the net costs of end-of-life vehicle waste management when vehicles become ELVs in a Member State other than where they were first placed on the market.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for expanded producer responsibility (EPR) systems, arguing that producers/importers should bear the costs of ELV waste management rather than recyclers. The provision directly establishes a mechanism requiring producers (or their PROs) to cover waste management costs, which is precisely the direction the organisation advocates for — shifting financial responsibility from recyclers to producers.

article_text:
Article 18 — Producer Responsibility Organisation
1. Producers may choose to fulfil their extended producer responsibility obligations either individually or may entrust a producer responsibility organisation authorised in accordance with Article 19 to fulfill the extended producer responsibility obligations on their behalf.

2. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure the confidentiality of the data in their possession as regards proprietary information or information directly attributable to individual producers or their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

3. In addition to the information referred to in Article 8a(3), point (e), of Directive 2008/98/EC, producer responsibility organisations shall publish on their websites at least each year, subject to commercial and industrial confidentiality, the information on the collection of end-of-life vehicles and achievement of targets on reuse and recycling, reuse and recovery and plastic recycling by the producers which entrusted the producer responsibility organisation.

4. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure a fair representation of producers and waste management operators in their governing bodies.
provision_effect: Establishes the framework for Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs), including their authorisation, data confidentiality obligations, reporting requirements, and governance structure requiring fair representation of producers and waste management operators.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for the establishment and proper implementation of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) systems for ELVs, arguing that the absence of such systems has unfairly burdened recyclers with management costs. Article 18 directly creates and structures Producer Responsibility Organisations as the mechanism through which EPR obligations are fulfilled, which is precisely the kind of formal EPR system the organisation is calling for. Notably, Article 18(4) requires fair representation of waste management operators in PRO governing bodies, which directly addresses the organisation's concern that recyclers have been left to bear costs without adequate representation or support.

source_text: EXPANDED LIABILITY OF THE PRODUCER in companies, and causing the impossibility of treating ELV in ATFs that end up in clandestine dismantlers. Recently in Spain Royal Decree 731/2020, of August 4, has been published (published in the BOE Friday, August 7, 2020) this regulation is drawn up in order to cover the costs of collection and treatment of End of Life Tires that are generated in flows other than the new spare tires which do have their own EPR (Signus, ..). As it is understood, the end-of-life tires that are generated after reuse in ATFs do not have an EPR and therefore a collection and treatment system, which are being paid for by the EPR of new spare parts causing economic imbalances, and in order to avoid this, the obligation is established for ATFs that do not mount second-hand tires to register in the product producer register, and to adhere to the EPR of the new spare part that implies the imposition of a new eco-tax on the used tire before sale. This situation is aggravating the reuser and making the reused producer more expensive, in addition to clearly applying to the recycler an obligation that clearly falls to the vehicle producer, assuming a future risk when more ELV components begin to present negative value. The lack of an EPR for vehicles and their components in Spain that covers the costs of treating ELVs and their components after reuse is causing a situation of economic unsustainability in ATFs

article_text:
Article 16 — Extended producer responsibility
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] producers shall have extended producer responsibility for vehicles that they make available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State. The scheme established by producers to exercise that responsibility shall be consistent with Articles 8 and 8a of Directive 2008/98/EC and comply with the requirements of this Chapter.
The extended producer responsibility shall include the obligation for producers to ensure that:
(a)vehicles which they have made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State and which become end-of-life vehicles
(i) collected in accordance with Article 23;
(ii) treated in accordance with Article 27;
(b)the waste management operators treating end-of-life vehicles referred to in point (a) meet the targets laid down in Article 34.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory extended producer responsibility (EPR) for vehicles, requiring producers to ensure end-of-life vehicles are collected and treated in accordance with specified articles and that waste management operators meet set targets.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for the creation of an EPR scheme via a European Regulation that obliges producers to cover the costs of collection and treatment of ELVs and their components, directly addressing the economic unsustainability of ATFs. The provision does exactly this — it establishes mandatory EPR for vehicles through a Regulation, requiring producers to ensure collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, which directly responds to the organisation's specific ask.

article_title: Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
article_text:
Article 19 — Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
1. A producer, in the case of individual fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, and producer responsibility organisations appointed in the case of collective fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, shall apply for an authorisation from the competent authority.

2. The authorisation shall be granted only where it is demonstrated that the requirements laid down in Article 8a(3), points (a) to (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC are complied with and the measures put in place by the producer or producer responsibility organisation are sufficient to meet the obligations set out in this Chapter with regard to the number of vehicles made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State by the producer or producers on whose behalf the producer responsibility organisation acts.

3. Member States shall, in their measures laying down administrative and procedural rules referred to in Article 14(3), point (b), include the details of the authorisation procedure, which may differ according to whether it relates to individual or collective fulfilment of the extended producer responsibility obligations, and the modalities for verifying compliance of producers or producer responsibility organisations, including the information to be provided by producers or producers responsibility organisations to that end.

4. The producer or the producer responsibility organisations shall notify the competent authority without undue delay of any changes to the information contained in the authorisation, of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation or of the permanent cessation of operations.

5. The self-control mechanism provided for in 8a(3), point (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC shall be carried out regularly, and at least every 3 years, and upon the request by the competent authority, in order to verify that the provisions in that point are complied with and the conditions for authorisation referred to in paragraph 2 continue to be met. The producer or the producer responsibility organisation shall, upon request, present a self-control report and, where necessary, the draft corrective action plan to the competent authority. Without prejudice to the competencies under paragraph 6, the competent authority may make observations on the self-control report and on the draft corrective action plan, and shall communicate any such observations to the producer or the producer responsibility organisation. The producer or producer responsibility organisation shall draw up and implement the corrective action plan based on those observations.

6. The competent authority may decide to revoke the authorisation if the producer or producer responsibility organisation no longer fulfils the requirements with regard to the organisation of the collection and treatment of end-of-life, fails in relation to reporting to the competent authority, fails to notify the competent authority of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation, or has ceased operations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory authorisation procedure for producers and producer responsibility organisations fulfilling extended producer responsibility (EPR) obligations, including compliance verification, self-control mechanisms, and grounds for revocation.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for the creation and clarification of EPR obligations for vehicle producers, including mandatory coverage of collection and treatment costs for ELVs, and calls for a binding European Regulation to enforce these obligations. The provision directly establishes a formal authorisation and compliance framework for EPR fulfilment, operationalising the very EPR obligations the organisation was advocating for — moving in the same direction of making EPR mandatory and enforceable at the EU level.

article_text:
Article 17 — Register of producers
1. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 35 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall establish a register of producers which shall serve to monitor compliance of producers with the requirements of this Chapter.
The register shall provide links to other national registers of producers’ websites to facilitate, in all Member States, registration of producers or appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

2. Producers shall register in the register referred to in paragraph 1. They shall to that end submit an application for registration in each Member State where they make a vehicle available on the market for the first time.
Producers shall submit the application for registration via an electronic data-processing system as referred to in paragraph 8, point (a).
Producers shall only make available vehicles on the market of a Member State, if they or, in case of authorisation, their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility, are registered in such Member State.

3. The application for registration shall include the information listed in Annex VIII. Member States may request additional information or documents, as necessary, to use the register of producers in an efficient manner.

4. By way of derogation from paragraph 3, the information referred to in point 1 (d) of Annex VIII shall be provided either in the application for registration under paragraph 3 or in the application for authorisation under Article 19.

5. Where a producer has appointed a producer responsibility organisation in accordance with Article 18, the obligations under this Article shall be met by that organisation mutatis mutandis unless otherwise specified by the Member State in which the vehicle has been made available on the market for the first time.

6. The obligations under this Article may be fulfilled on a producer’s behalf by an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility.

7. Member States may decide that the registration procedure pursuant to this Article and the authorisation procedure pursuant to Article 19 constitute a single procedure, provided that the application for authorisation meets the requirements set out in paragraphs 3 to 6 of this Article.

8. The competent authority shall:
(a)make available on its website information about the application process via an electronic data-processing system;
(b)grant registrations and provide a registration number within a maximum period of 12 weeks from the moment that all the information required under paragraphs 2 and 3 is provided.

9. The competent authority may:
(a)lay down modalities with regard to the requirements and process of registration without adding substantive requirements to those laid down in paragraphs 2 and 3;
(b)charge cost-based and proportionate fees to producers for the processing of the applications referred to in paragraph 2.

10. The competent authority may refuse to register a producer or withdraw the producer’s registration where the information referred to in paragraph 3 and related documentary evidence are not provided or are not sufficient, or where the producer no longer meets the requirements laid down in Annex VIII, point 1 (d).
The competent authority shall withdraw the producer’s registration if the producer has ceased to exist.

11. The producer, or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation appointed on behalf of the producers it represents shall without undue delay notify the competent authority of any changes to the information contained in the registration and of any permanent cessation as regards the making available on the market within the territory of the Member State of the vehicles referred to in the registration.

12. The producer or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation shall report to the competent authority responsible for the register on the performance of extended producer responsibility obligations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory register of producers in each Member State to monitor compliance with extended producer responsibility (EPR) requirements, including registration obligations, procedures, and reporting duties for producers.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for the creation of proper EPR systems through a European Regulation, including clear obligations on producers to cover collection and treatment costs for ELVs. Article 17 establishes a mandatory producer register to enforce EPR compliance, directly operationalising the kind of binding, EU-level EPR framework the organisation called for. The provision takes concrete action in the direction the organisation advocated — formalising producer obligations under EPR through a Regulation rather than a Directive.

article_title: Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
article_text:
Article 19 — Authorisation on fulfilment of extended producer responsibility
1. A producer, in the case of individual fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, and producer responsibility organisations appointed in the case of collective fulfilment of extended producer responsibility obligations, shall apply for an authorisation from the competent authority.

2. The authorisation shall be granted only where it is demonstrated that the requirements laid down in Article 8a(3), points (a) to (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC are complied with and the measures put in place by the producer or producer responsibility organisation are sufficient to meet the obligations set out in this Chapter with regard to the number of vehicles made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State by the producer or producers on whose behalf the producer responsibility organisation acts.

3. Member States shall, in their measures laying down administrative and procedural rules referred to in Article 14(3), point (b), include the details of the authorisation procedure, which may differ according to whether it relates to individual or collective fulfilment of the extended producer responsibility obligations, and the modalities for verifying compliance of producers or producer responsibility organisations, including the information to be provided by producers or producers responsibility organisations to that end.

4. The producer or the producer responsibility organisations shall notify the competent authority without undue delay of any changes to the information contained in the authorisation, of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation or of the permanent cessation of operations.

5. The self-control mechanism provided for in 8a(3), point (d), of Directive 2008/98/EC shall be carried out regularly, and at least every 3 years, and upon the request by the competent authority, in order to verify that the provisions in that point are complied with and the conditions for authorisation referred to in paragraph 2 continue to be met. The producer or the producer responsibility organisation shall, upon request, present a self-control report and, where necessary, the draft corrective action plan to the competent authority. Without prejudice to the competencies under paragraph 6, the competent authority may make observations on the self-control report and on the draft corrective action plan, and shall communicate any such observations to the producer or the producer responsibility organisation. The producer or producer responsibility organisation shall draw up and implement the corrective action plan based on those observations.

6. The competent authority may decide to revoke the authorisation if the producer or producer responsibility organisation no longer fulfils the requirements with regard to the organisation of the collection and treatment of end-of-life, fails in relation to reporting to the competent authority, fails to notify the competent authority of any changes that concern the terms of the authorisation, or has ceased operations.
provision_effect: Establishes an authorisation system for producers and producer responsibility organisations fulfilling extended producer responsibility obligations, including compliance verification, self-control mechanisms, and revocation procedures.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org supports expanded producer liability (EPR) and advocates for periodic economic audits to review ELV treatment costs and update EPR financing, as well as mechanisms to address non-compliance. The provision establishes an authorisation and self-control framework for EPR compliance, which touches on the org's concerns about oversight and accountability, but the provision does not specifically address the economic audit obligation or eco-tax fallback the org proposes. There is a genuine connection to EPR governance, but it is unclear whether this authorisation mechanism satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the org's specific asks.

article_text:
Article 17 — Register of producers
1. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 35 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall establish a register of producers which shall serve to monitor compliance of producers with the requirements of this Chapter.
The register shall provide links to other national registers of producers’ websites to facilitate, in all Member States, registration of producers or appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

2. Producers shall register in the register referred to in paragraph 1. They shall to that end submit an application for registration in each Member State where they make a vehicle available on the market for the first time.
Producers shall submit the application for registration via an electronic data-processing system as referred to in paragraph 8, point (a).
Producers shall only make available vehicles on the market of a Member State, if they or, in case of authorisation, their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility, are registered in such Member State.

3. The application for registration shall include the information listed in Annex VIII. Member States may request additional information or documents, as necessary, to use the register of producers in an efficient manner.

4. By way of derogation from paragraph 3, the information referred to in point 1 (d) of Annex VIII shall be provided either in the application for registration under paragraph 3 or in the application for authorisation under Article 19.

5. Where a producer has appointed a producer responsibility organisation in accordance with Article 18, the obligations under this Article shall be met by that organisation mutatis mutandis unless otherwise specified by the Member State in which the vehicle has been made available on the market for the first time.

6. The obligations under this Article may be fulfilled on a producer’s behalf by an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility.

7. Member States may decide that the registration procedure pursuant to this Article and the authorisation procedure pursuant to Article 19 constitute a single procedure, provided that the application for authorisation meets the requirements set out in paragraphs 3 to 6 of this Article.

8. The competent authority shall:
(a)make available on its website information about the application process via an electronic data-processing system;
(b)grant registrations and provide a registration number within a maximum period of 12 weeks from the moment that all the information required under paragraphs 2 and 3 is provided.

9. The competent authority may:
(a)lay down modalities with regard to the requirements and process of registration without adding substantive requirements to those laid down in paragraphs 2 and 3;
(b)charge cost-based and proportionate fees to producers for the processing of the applications referred to in paragraph 2.

10. The competent authority may refuse to register a producer or withdraw the producer’s registration where the information referred to in paragraph 3 and related documentary evidence are not provided or are not sufficient, or where the producer no longer meets the requirements laid down in Annex VIII, point 1 (d).
The competent authority shall withdraw the producer’s registration if the producer has ceased to exist.

11. The producer, or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation appointed on behalf of the producers it represents shall without undue delay notify the competent authority of any changes to the information contained in the registration and of any permanent cessation as regards the making available on the market within the territory of the Member State of the vehicles referred to in the registration.

12. The producer or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation shall report to the competent authority responsible for the register on the performance of extended producer responsibility obligations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory register of producers in each Member State to monitor compliance with extended producer responsibility (EPR) requirements, including registration procedures, obligations, and reporting duties for producers.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org supports expanded producer liability (EPR) and raises specific concerns about EPR system governance — notably opposing monopolistic EPR systems and advocating for economic audits and eco-tax fallback mechanisms. While Article 17 operationalises the EPR framework through a producer register, it does not address the org's specific asks (audit obligations, anti-monopoly safeguards, or eco-taxes). There is a real thematic connection to EPR, but the provision neither delivers nor contradicts the org's specific advocacy positions, making the direction genuinely ambiguous.

article_text:
Article 18 — Producer Responsibility Organisation
1. Producers may choose to fulfil their extended producer responsibility obligations either individually or may entrust a producer responsibility organisation authorised in accordance with Article 19 to fulfill the extended producer responsibility obligations on their behalf.

2. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure the confidentiality of the data in their possession as regards proprietary information or information directly attributable to individual producers or their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

3. In addition to the information referred to in Article 8a(3), point (e), of Directive 2008/98/EC, producer responsibility organisations shall publish on their websites at least each year, subject to commercial and industrial confidentiality, the information on the collection of end-of-life vehicles and achievement of targets on reuse and recycling, reuse and recovery and plastic recycling by the producers which entrusted the producer responsibility organisation.

4. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure a fair representation of producers and waste management operators in their governing bodies.
provision_effect: Establishes rules for Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs), including how producers may fulfil EPR obligations, data confidentiality requirements, transparency/reporting obligations, and fair representation of producers and waste management operators in PRO governing bodies.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation supports expanded producer responsibility and specifically opposes monopolistic EPR systems, advocating for periodic economic audits and eco-tax fallback mechanisms. Article 18 establishes PRO governance rules including fair representation of waste management operators, which partially touches the org's concern about monopolies and fair treatment chain representation, but the provision does not address audits, eco-taxes, or anti-monopoly safeguards the org specifically requested. A genuine connection exists (EPR system structure and governance), but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific asks.

article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory obligations for authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) to accept, store, depollute, and treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts in compliance with specific minimum requirements, best available techniques, and waste hierarchy principles, including record-keeping of treatment operations.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly advocates that ELV depollution and treatment processes should be carried out by Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) only, and that ATFs should have reporting requirements on collected parts and materials. The provision directly establishes binding obligations on ATFs for treatment, depollution, and electronic record-keeping of treatment operations — concretely delivering on both of the org's specific asks regarding ATF-exclusive treatment and data reporting requirements.

article_title: Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
article_text:
Article 30 — Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, are removed from an end-of-life vehicle prior to shredding, after the depollution operations referred to in Article 29, have been completed.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if an authorised treatment facility demonstrates, that post-shredder technologies separates materials from parts and components listed in Part C, entries 13 to19, of Annex VII, as efficiently as manual dismantling processes or semi-automated disassembly processes.
For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the authorised treatment facility shall provide the information listed in Part G of Annex VII.

3. In addition to the obligations set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall maintain records of the end-of-life vehicles that are processed without the prior removal of parts, components and materials in accordance with paragraph 2, including the name and address of the treatment facilities, and the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the end-of-life vehicles concerned.
The authorised treatment facilities shall provide the information in the records referred to in the first subparagraph to the competent authority in accordance with Article 49(6).
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to mandatorily remove specific parts and components from end-of-life vehicles prior to shredding, with an exception if post-shredder technologies are demonstrated to be equally efficient, and mandates record-keeping and reporting of vehicles processed without prior removal.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly advocates that depollution and ELV treatment should be carried out exclusively by Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) with reporting requirements on all parts and materials separated before shredding. Article 30 directly establishes mandatory pre-shredder removal obligations on ATFs and imposes record-keeping and reporting duties on those facilities, which aligns with the org's specific ask for ATF-exclusive treatment and data reporting on pre-shredding operations.

label: ALIGNED
reasoning: ArcelorMittal explicitly advocates for creating conditions that facilitate depollution and treatment operations of ELVs, stating that operators need information to "disassemble the largest number of valuable parts before the shredding process." The provision directly mandates pre-shredding removal of parts and components, which aligns with ArcelorMittal's position that dismantling valuable parts before shredding should be facilitated and encouraged. However, ArcelorMittal also argues against material- or part-specific targets in favour of whole-vehicle targets, which creates some tension with the mandatory part-specific removal requirements — but the core advocacy for pre-shredding dismantling is clearly reflected in the provision's action.

article_text:
Article 1 — Subject matter
1. This Regulation lays down circularity requirements on vehicle design and production related to reusability, recyclability and recoverability and the use of recycled content, which are to be verified at type-approval of vehicles, and on information and labelling requirements on parts, components and materials in vehicles. It also lays down requirements on extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, as well as on the export of used vehicles from the Union to third countries.
provision_effect: Establishes the scope of the regulation, covering circularity requirements on vehicle design/production, recycled content, type-approval verification, information/labelling, extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, and export of used vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: ArcelorMittal advocates for specific positions on ELV recycling/recovery targets, information requirements for depollution and treatment operations, and reporting of recycling performance per material or part — all of which fall directly within the subject matter this provision establishes (circularity requirements, information/labelling, and ELV treatment). The provision's scope encompasses the exact regulatory areas ArcelorMittal is advocating on, and the organisation takes concrete directional stances (e.g. whole-vehicle targets over material-specific ones, information flows to treatment operators) that align with the regulation taking action in these areas.

source_text: Figure 1. Circularity check of different reference model of vehicles; source: Arcelor Mittal1) In the past setting-up recovery and recycling targets for the entire vehicle has proven to be a very effective option while splitting this target into different material (or part) specific targets will block any trend of optimisation of the vehicle design and recyclability. Recovery and recycling targets measured on the entire vehicle will ensure more flexibility during the design and help in reducing trade-offs between EU policies on ELV that focus on different aspects (i.e. CO2 emission of vehicles; recovery and recycling of ELVs). h. The parts of the vehicle collected during the de-pollution steps and not recycled yet have not to be reported in the ELV recycling rate unless having factorised in the recycling yields (e.g. batteries), if any; i. Information about the recycling performance per material or per parts of the ELV could be reported as additional information necessary for assessing the different factors contributing to the circularity of vehicles and thus helping the automotive industries in improving the design of vehicles. 5. It is important to create the conditions for facilitating the depollution and treatment operations of ELVs. The operators and more in general waste treatment companies need to receive the information necessary for disassemble the largest number of valuable parts before the shredding process. 6
context_before: In the following points there is the explanation in detail: g. the recycling target of ELVs should be measured on the entire vehicle and not per material; switching from measuring the recycling performance of the vehicle to measuring the recycling performance per material will put at serious risk the effectiveness of the ELV. This will be also more true during the electrification trend in automotive. Battery electric vehicles (BEVs) have an important share of their total weight made out of batteries and can be assembled with much more plastic than the actual models. Such composition makes the recycling performance of BEVs drastically lower than the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles, due to recycling yields of batteries (much lower than 100%; e.g. recycling yield at 50% for Li-Ion- and at 65% for Pb- batteries) and low plastic recyclability. Figure 1 shows how the different type of power train and the different recycling yields combined with the BoMs of different vehicle types can heavily and negatively affect vehicle circularity (in orange it is reported ELVs recovery rate; in blue the real ELVs recycling rate). article_text:
Article 34 — Reuse, recycling and recovery targets
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the calendar year following 36 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation], Member States shall ensure that the following targets are met by the waste management operators:
(a)the reuse and recovery, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 95 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year;
(b)the reuse and recycling, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 85 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year.

2. From [OP: please insert a date = the first day of the calendar year following 60 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation] Member States shall ensure that waste management operators achieve a yearly target for the recycling of plastics of at least 30 % of the total weight of plastics contained in the vehicles delivered to the waste management operators.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory reuse, recovery, and recycling targets for end-of-life vehicles, including a specific plastics recycling target, to be met by waste management operators within defined timeframes.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: ArcelorMittal explicitly argues against splitting recycling targets into material-specific targets (such as the plastics-specific target in Article 34(2)), advocating instead for whole-vehicle recovery and recycling targets to preserve design flexibility. The provision does exactly what the org opposes: it introduces a material-specific plastics recycling target (30%) alongside the overall vehicle targets, directly contradicting ArcelorMittal's stated position.

source_text: An overhaul of the ELV Directive 8. EUROFER positively considers the extension of the Directive scope for covering other EU fleets such as trucks and motor-bikes; putting these two streams into the scope can help in improving EoL management and in possibly contributing to more recycling in EU. 9. ELVs Directive had positive contributions to waste management and to the EU recycling value chain of ELVs. However, it has been evident that it did not succeed in improving vehicles design, i.e. design for higher quality recycling/ circular design. Thus, EUROFER fully support the introduction of product requirements focusing on making vehicle design more circular. Electrification trend, light weighting and more stringent emission limits, focused only on mitigating climate change, can negatively influence car recyclability and resource efficiency. Moreover, it should not be underestimated the trade-off between the very high CO2 intensity of light-materials and their extremely low, if not zero, recyclability. Therefore, ELV should work as an instrument for reducing trade-offs between light- weighting/electrification and recycling and re-balancing the design process towards an optimum outcome. 10. The proposal of setting up the requirement for the mandatory use of recycled plastic in new cars can have a positive outcome as long as it is applied only to plastic
context_before: in order to improve the quality of the materials coming out from the ELV shredding process, it is advisable to require the installation of Post-Shredding Technologies (PST) for enhancing a better segregation of different materials; o. the shredding campaigns on ELVs at ATFs should be carried out using normal operating conditions of the shredding equipment; moreover, the ELVs shredding operations should be carried out, to the extent possible, without mixing ELVs with other EoL products, for avoiding cross-contamination. Most of the measures on ATFs mentioned above are already included in the BAT 2 of the Implementing Decision 2018/1147/EU and they will help in creating a win-win synergy. ATFs will become a crucial step for ensuring true circularity of ELVs in the EU; they will help in closing data gaps and ensuring the necessary conditions for the next high quality recycling steps. In addition, the proposed measures will help ATFs in increasing revenues from highest quality fractions and collecting more valuable materials out of steel for instance. Moreover, the reporting requirements will be instrumental to policy makers to monitor vehicle design trends and car industry to improve its circularity and resource efficiency.
