context_after: 10. The proposal of setting up the requirement for the mandatory use of recycled plastic in new cars can have a positive outcome as long as it is applied only to plastic. The content of recyclates in materials and products strongly depends on the dynamic (or existence) of the market of secondary raw materials and on the properties of the specific material into consideration. This requirement for plastic makes sense as an instrument for increasing the demand of recycled plastic and for supporting the improvement of its quality. On the contrary, for metals and for steel in particular, the situation is at the opposite because the demand of ferrous scrap always outpaces the supply and it preserves its inherent properties after the recycling processes when the recycling value chain works properly, while it is not the case yet for plastic. However, it is also necessary to remind here that recyclability should be ensured also for parts containing recyclates, otherwise after the first recycling cycle the material has to be incinerated or landfilled. Ensuring for instance recyclability is a more powerful instrument for creating market for secondary raw materials and ensuring high quality recycling of ELVs. 11. The IIA proposes to use Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) schemes for ELVs in order to increase the responsibility on car manufacturers
article_text:
Article 8 — General obligations
1. Manufacturers shall demonstrate that new vehicles that they have manufactured and that are placed on the market, are type-approved in accordance with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2018/858 and of this Regulation.

2. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements in Articles 4, 5, 6 or 7, the manufacturer shall provide the documentation showing compliance with those requirements and shall:
(a)include it in the information folder referred to in Article 24 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858; and
(b)submit it to the type-approval authority in accordance with Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

3. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirement in Article 9 applies, the manufacturer shall submit the circularity strategy to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

4. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 10 apply, the manufacturer shall draw up the information referred to in Article 10(1) and submit it, in accordance with Article 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.

5. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 11 apply, the manufacturer shall submit the declaration confirming compliance with the requirement set out in Article 11(1), in accordance with 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes general obligations for manufacturers to demonstrate type-approval compliance and submit documentation (including circularity strategies, material information, and recycled content declarations) to type-approval authorities as part of the vehicle approval process.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly supports the introduction of product requirements focusing on making vehicle design more circular, and Article 8 operationalises exactly this by requiring manufacturers to submit circularity strategies and compliance documentation for circular design requirements (Articles 9–11) as part of type-approval. The provision takes concrete action in the direction the org advocated — embedding circular design obligations into the type-approval framework.

article_text:
Article 16 — Extended producer responsibility
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] producers shall have extended producer responsibility for vehicles that they make available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State. The scheme established by producers to exercise that responsibility shall be consistent with Articles 8 and 8a of Directive 2008/98/EC and comply with the requirements of this Chapter.
The extended producer responsibility shall include the obligation for producers to ensure that:
(a)vehicles which they have made available on the market for the first time within the territory of a Member State and which become end-of-life vehicles
(i) collected in accordance with Article 23;
(ii) treated in accordance with Article 27;
(b)the waste management operators treating end-of-life vehicles referred to in point (a) meet the targets laid down in Article 34.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory extended producer responsibility (EPR) for vehicle producers, requiring them to ensure end-of-life vehicles are collected, treated, and meet recycling targets.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: The organisation explicitly critiques the EPR scheme as proposed, arguing that responsibility should be shared along the entire value chain among all actors involved — not placed solely on car manufacturers. The provision, however, establishes EPR obligations squarely on producers, which is the opposite of the shared-responsibility model the organisation advocates for.

article_text:
Article 18 — Producer Responsibility Organisation
1. Producers may choose to fulfil their extended producer responsibility obligations either individually or may entrust a producer responsibility organisation authorised in accordance with Article 19 to fulfill the extended producer responsibility obligations on their behalf.

2. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure the confidentiality of the data in their possession as regards proprietary information or information directly attributable to individual producers or their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

3. In addition to the information referred to in Article 8a(3), point (e), of Directive 2008/98/EC, producer responsibility organisations shall publish on their websites at least each year, subject to commercial and industrial confidentiality, the information on the collection of end-of-life vehicles and achievement of targets on reuse and recycling, reuse and recovery and plastic recycling by the producers which entrusted the producer responsibility organisation.

4. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure a fair representation of producers and waste management operators in their governing bodies.
provision_effect: Establishes rules for Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs), including their authorisation, data confidentiality obligations, reporting requirements, and governance structure requiring fair representation of producers and waste management operators.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org advocates that EPR responsibilities should be shared along the entire value chain among all actors involved, not just car manufacturers. Article 18(4) requires fair representation of producers and waste management operators in PRO governing bodies, which partially touches on the org's concern about shared responsibility — but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's broader ask for value chain-wide responsibility sharing. The provision addresses governance structure of PROs but does not directly resolve whether responsibilities are distributed across all value chain actors as the org demands.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6920471 - 19/11/2020 CEWEP feedback on End-of-life vehicles – revision of EU rules CEWEP welcomes the Commission's new initiative on the Revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of- life vehicles, in order to update this current legislation and to improve its coherence with the aims of the green deal and the circular economy action plan. CEWEP would like to highlight the importance to maintain a holistic approach and to strike the right balance between policy goals (e.g. zero pollution and circular economy). This balance is extremely important to ensure that the overall aim of tackling environmental, health and climate impacts is achieved, avoiding the risk of undermining the overarching sustainability objectives included in the Green Deal. We would like to highlight the following for the foreseen review: • Vehicles are made of various material fractions and different types of waste streams are extracted from them at the end of their life. These streams should undergo treatments that are in line with the whole waste treatment hierarchy, e.g. not excluding energy recovery when waste streams are not suitable for reuse or recycling. The integrated approach helps to avoid any harm to the environment or human health, in accordance with the ambitions of a non-toxic environment
context_after: The integrated approach helps to avoid any harm to the environment or human health, in accordance with the ambitions of a non-toxic environment. • When assessing the possible improvements of recycling specific waste streams such as plastics, it is important that recycled content calculations be based on solid methodologies and not theoretical allocations that do not provide sufficient trust in green claims. • Definitions contained in the ELVD should be adapted to be consistent with the Waste Framework Directive, e.g. recycling should exclude production of fuel. Brussels, 19th November 2020 Contact : info@cewep.eu; www.cewep.eu CEWEP, Confederation of European Waste-to-Energy Plants, is the umbrella association of the operators of Waste-to-Energy (incineration with energy recovery) plants, representing about 410 plants from 23 countries. They make up 80% of the Waste-to-Energy capacity in Europe. Its members are committed to ensuring high environmental standards, achieving low emissions and maintaining state of the art energy production from remaining waste that cannot be recycled in a sustainable way.
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to review and report on the application of the ELV Regulation, including evaluating the scope, targets, substances of concern, and penalties, by a specified deadline.
label: NOISE
reasoning: CEWEP's position is a general welcome of the Commission's initiative and a broad call for a holistic, balanced approach that does not exclude energy recovery — it does not take a specific directional stance on the review mechanism itself (its timing, scope of evaluation, or procedural requirements). The org text does not advocate for or against any specific element of what Article 55 establishes, making it too general to constitute specific alignment or opposition.

article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to treat end-of-life vehicles and their parts in accordance with the waste hierarchy and minimum treatment requirements, including depollution, dismantling, and best available techniques.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CEWEP specifically advocates that end-of-life vehicle waste streams "should undergo treatments that are in line with the whole waste treatment hierarchy," and Article 27(3)(d) explicitly requires authorised treatment facilities to treat ELVs "in accordance with the waste hierarchy." This is a direct and specific match between the org's ask and the provision's concrete requirement.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6920471 - 19/11/2020 CEWEP feedback on End-of-life vehicles – revision of EU rules CEWEP welcomes the Commission's new initiative on the Revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of- life vehicles, in order to update this current legislation and to improve its coherence with the aims of the green deal and the circular economy action plan. CEWEP would like to highlight the importance to maintain a holistic approach and to strike the right balance between policy goals (e.g. zero pollution and circular economy). This balance is extremely important to ensure that the overall aim of tackling environmental, health and climate impacts is achieved, avoiding the risk of undermining the overarching sustainability objectives included in the Green Deal. We would like to highlight the following for the foreseen review: • Vehicles are made of various material fractions and different types of waste streams are extracted from them at the end of their life. These streams should undergo treatments that are in line with the whole waste treatment hierarchy, e.g. not excluding energy recovery when waste streams are not suitable for reuse or recycling. The integrated approach helps to avoid any harm to the environment or human health, in accordance with the ambitions of a non-toxic environment
context_after: The integrated approach helps to avoid any harm to the environment or human health, in accordance with the ambitions of a non-toxic environment. • When assessing the possible improvements of recycling specific waste streams such as plastics, it is important that recycled content calculations be based on solid methodologies and not theoretical allocations that do not provide sufficient trust in green claims. • Definitions contained in the ELVD should be adapted to be consistent with the Waste Framework Directive, e.g. recycling should exclude production of fuel. Brussels, 19th November 2020 Contact : info@cewep.eu; www.cewep.eu CEWEP, Confederation of European Waste-to-Energy Plants, is the umbrella association of the operators of Waste-to-Energy (incineration with energy recovery) plants, representing about 410 plants from 23 countries. They make up 80% of the Waste-to-Energy capacity in Europe. Its members are committed to ensuring high environmental standards, achieving low emissions and maintaining state of the art energy production from remaining waste that cannot be recycled in a sustainable way.
provision_effect: Establishes the scope of financial contributions producers must pay under extended producer responsibility, covering collection, treatment, awareness campaigns, notification systems, and data reporting costs for end-of-life vehicles.
label: NOISE
reasoning: CEWEP's position focuses on advocating for a holistic waste treatment hierarchy approach — specifically that energy recovery should not be excluded for unsuitable waste streams — and does not express any specific stance on the financial responsibility mechanisms, producer contribution structures, or EPR cost allocation rules established in Article 20. The org text is a general welcome of the initiative combined with a specific point about waste treatment hierarchy, which has no directional connection to the financial responsibility provisions in this article.

article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory obligations for authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) to accept, store, depollute, and treat end-of-life vehicles and their parts in compliance with specific minimum requirements, best available techniques, and the waste hierarchy.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EUROFER explicitly advocates that ELVs should undergo depollution and treatment processes carried out by Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) only, to ensure appropriate treatments and better data reporting. Article 27 directly delivers this by imposing mandatory obligations exclusively on ATFs for the storage, depollution, and treatment of ELVs, aligning with EUROFER's specific ask. Additionally, EUROFER's call for reporting requirements on collected parts and materials separated before shredding is partially reflected in the provision's record-keeping and treatment documentation requirements.

article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to depollute end-of-life vehicles before further treatment, including separate collection of fluids, removal of hazardous substances, battery removal, and documentation of the depollution process.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EUROFER explicitly advocates that ELVs should undergo depollution and treatment processes carried out by Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs) only, and that these installations should have reporting requirements on all collected parts and materials separated before shredding. Article 29 directly delivers both of these asks: it mandates depollution by authorised treatment facilities and requires documentation/recording of the depollution process, aligning with EUROFER's specific positions.

article_text:
Article 1 — Subject matter
1. This Regulation lays down circularity requirements on vehicle design and production related to reusability, recyclability and recoverability and the use of recycled content, which are to be verified at type-approval of vehicles, and on information and labelling requirements on parts, components and materials in vehicles. It also lays down requirements on extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, as well as on the export of used vehicles from the Union to third countries.
provision_effect: Establishes the subject matter of the Regulation, covering circularity requirements on vehicle design, recycled content, type-approval, information/labelling, extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, and export of used vehicles.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Article 1 is a purely descriptive subject-matter provision that lists the topics covered by the Regulation without taking any concrete stance or establishing specific requirements. While EUROFER's position contains specific advocacy on recycling targets and ELV treatment, this provision only defines the regulatory scope and does not act in any direction that could be evaluated against those specific positions.

source_text: An overhaul of the ELV Directive 8. EUROFER looks at the extension of the Directive scope to cover other EU fleets, such as trucks and motorbikes, positively. Putting these two streams into the scope can help in improving EoL management and potentially contribute to more recycling in the EU. 9. ELVs Directive had positive contributions to waste management and to the EU recycling value chain of ELVs. However, it is clear that it has not succeeded in improving vehicle design, i.e. design for higher quality recycling/circular design. Thus, EUROFER fully supports the introduction of product requirements focusing on making vehicle design more circular. Electrification trends, light-weighting and more stringent emission limits, focused only on mitigating climate change, can negatively influence car recyclability and resource efficiency. Moreover, the trade-off between the very high CO2 intensity of light- materials and their extremely low, if not zero, recyclability should not be underestimated. Therefore, ELV should work as an instrument to reduce trade-offs between light-weighting/electrification and recycling and re-balancing the design process towards an optimum outcome. 10. The proposal to set up the requirement for the mandatory use of recycled plastic in new cars could have a positive outcome as long as it is applied only to plastic
context_before: in order to improve the quality of the materials coming out from the ELV shredding process, it is advisable to require the installation of Post-Shredding Technologies (PST) for enhancing a better segregation of different materials; o. the shredding campaigns on ELVs at ATFs should be carried out using normal operating conditions of the shredding equipment; moreover, the ELVs shredding operations should be carried out, to the extent possible, without mixing ELVs with other EoL products, for avoiding cross-contamination. Most of the measures on ATFs mentioned above are already included in the BAT 2 of the Implementing Decision 2018/1147/EU and they will help in creating a win-win synergy. ATFs will become a crucial step for ensuring the true circularity of ELVs in the EU; they will help in closing data gaps and ensuring the necessary conditions for the next high- quality recycling steps. In addition, the proposed measures will help ATFs in increasing revenues from the highest quality fractions and in collecting more valuable materials out of steel for instance. Moreover, the reporting requirements will be instrumental if policy makers are to monitor vehicle design trends, and for the automotive industry to improve its circularity and resource efficiency. The European Steel Association (EUROFER) AISBL | Avenue de Cortenbergh, 172, 1000 Brussels, Belgium P ag e | 5 +32 3 738 79 20 | mail@eurofer.eu | www.eurofer.eu | EU Transparency Register: ID 93038071152-83
context_after: 10. The proposal to set up the requirement for the mandatory use of recycled plastic in new cars could have a positive outcome as long as it is applied only to plastic. The content of ‘recyclates’ in materials and products strongly depends on the dynamic (or existence) of a market for secondary raw materials and on the properties of the specific material under consideration. This requirement for plastic makes sense as an instrument to increase the demand for recycled plastic and to support the improvement of its quality. On the contrary for metals, and for steel in particular, the situation is reversed because the demand for ferrous scrap always outpaces the supply and metals preserve their inherent properties after the recycling processes (if recycling value chain works properly), while this is not yet the case for plastics. However, the recyclability should also be ensured for parts containing ‘recyclates’. Otherwise, after the first recycling cycle the material has to be incinerated or landfilled. Ensuring recyclability is a more powerful instrument for creating markets for secondary raw materials and ensuring the high-quality recycling of ELVs. 11. The IIA proposes to use Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) schemes for ELVs in order to increase the responsibility on car manufacturers. However, it is worth stressing that the first scope of an EPR is to ensure the functioning and the effectiveness of the recycling value chain of the involved products
article_text:
Article 8 — General obligations
1. Manufacturers shall demonstrate that new vehicles that they have manufactured and that are placed on the market, are type-approved in accordance with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2018/858 and of this Regulation.

2. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements in Articles 4, 5, 6 or 7, the manufacturer shall provide the documentation showing compliance with those requirements and shall:
(a)include it in the information folder referred to in Article 24 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858; and
(b)submit it to the type-approval authority in accordance with Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

3. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirement in Article 9 applies, the manufacturer shall submit the circularity strategy to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of Regulation (EU) 2018/858.

4. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 10 apply, the manufacturer shall draw up the information referred to in Article 10(1) and submit it, in accordance with Article 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.

5. For the purposes of type-approval of vehicles to which the requirements set out in Article 11 apply, the manufacturer shall submit the declaration confirming compliance with the requirement set out in Article 11(1), in accordance with 24(1), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2018/858, to the type-approval authority together with the application for type-approval referred to in Article 23 of that Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes general obligations for manufacturers to demonstrate type-approval compliance and submit documentation (including circularity strategies and recycled content declarations) to type-approval authorities as part of the vehicle approval process.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EUROFER explicitly supports the introduction of product requirements focusing on making vehicle design more circular, and Article 8 operationalises exactly this by requiring manufacturers to submit circularity strategies and compliance documentation as part of type-approval. The provision translates the circular design requirements EUROFER advocates for into binding manufacturer obligations, directly responding to EUROFER's call for ELV to function as an instrument improving vehicle design for higher quality recycling and circularity.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6926426 - 19/11/2020 The ELV Directive, which came into force 20 years ago largely delivered on its initial objectives due to the significant efforts made by the automotive and the recycling industry. The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA) fully supports the European Commission’s initiative to ensure a better consistency with the objectives of the European Green Deal, including the Circular Economy Action Plan and the European Climate Law. Product Longevity The approach selected by the European Commission to favour circularity, prioritises the reduction of waste and reuse of materials/articles before recycling. It has always been the automobile industry’s goal to design vehicles as long-time usable products, while ensuring they are reusable, repairable, and recyclable to the highest possible degree. After multiple ownership, the average age of an ELV (15-22 years – Trinomics report 2020), is much higher than most other consumer products and clearly demonstrates the success of the automotive industry’s approach. Unfortunately, this extended product life makes it impossible to assess if a design choice implemented today delivers the expected positive impact in 15-22 years when the time comes for the ELV to be recycled. This is especially true when it comes to shredder technology and other recycling technologies (e.g. chemical recycling) which have certainly improved over such a period
context_after: This is especially true when it comes to shredder technology and other recycling technologies (e.g. chemical recycling) which have certainly improved over such a period. For example, the reduction of SVHCs in today’s new vehicles may not result in simplified material recycling as knowledge about substances and their classification, will continue to evolve over the next decades. Dismantling Information At some considerable cost and effort with the International Dismantling Information System (IDIS) - developed in close cooperation with the economic partners- vehicle manufacturers provide detailed information to ATFs to ensure the environmental treatment of End-of-Life-Vehicles, safely and economically. Automobile manufacturers are requested to deliver relevant information within 6 months after launch of a model. This dismantling information is provided free of charge by 77 car brands on 1000 car models and about 2200 variants in 40 countries and is available in 31 languages. IDIS includes links to the repair & maintenance information (RMI) websites including e.g. spare part catalogues and training information, of the vehicle manufacturers. ATF are welcome to use the same reference as any independent operator/workshop with regard to finding relevant information to dismantle or reuse parts.
article_title: Reusability, recyclability and recoverability of vehicles
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory minimum reusability/recyclability (85%) and recoverability (95%) rates for new vehicle types, along with data collection, calculation, and marking obligations for manufacturers.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text expresses general support for the Commission's initiative and describes the automotive industry's historical commitment to vehicle longevity and recyclability, but does not take a specific directional stance on the minimum percentage thresholds, the calculation methodology, or the specific manufacturer obligations established in Article 4. The observation about the difficulty of assessing design choices over a 15-22 year vehicle lifespan is contextual background rather than a concrete advocacy position for or against the provision's requirements.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6926426 - 19/11/2020 The ELV Directive, which came into force 20 years ago largely delivered on its initial objectives due to the significant efforts made by the automotive and the recycling industry. The European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association (ACEA) fully supports the European Commission’s initiative to ensure a better consistency with the objectives of the European Green Deal, including the Circular Economy Action Plan and the European Climate Law. Product Longevity The approach selected by the European Commission to favour circularity, prioritises the reduction of waste and reuse of materials/articles before recycling. It has always been the automobile industry’s goal to design vehicles as long-time usable products, while ensuring they are reusable, repairable, and recyclable to the highest possible degree. After multiple ownership, the average age of an ELV (15-22 years – Trinomics report 2020), is much higher than most other consumer products and clearly demonstrates the success of the automotive industry’s approach. Unfortunately, this extended product life makes it impossible to assess if a design choice implemented today delivers the expected positive impact in 15-22 years when the time comes for the ELV to be recycled. This is especially true when it comes to shredder technology and other recycling technologies (e.g. chemical recycling) which have certainly improved over such a period

article_text:
Article 9 — Circularity strategy
1. For each vehicle type that is type-approved under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], the manufacturer shall draw up a circularity strategy.

2. The circularity strategy shall describe which actions the manufacturers will take to follow-up on their obligations to ensure that the circularity requirements in Chapter II, which are verified in the type-approval procedures and which are applicable to the vehicle type concerned, are met.

3. The circularity strategy shall contain the elements laid down in Part A of Annex IV.

4. The manufacturer shall provide a copy of the circularity strategy to the Commission within 30 days after the type-approval for the concerned vehicle type has been granted.

5. The manufacturer shall monitor and follow up on the actions contained in the circularity strategy and update the strategy every five years in accordance with Part B of Annex IV. The updated circularity strategy shall be provided to the type-approval authority that issued the type-approval for the vehicle type and to the Commisssion.

6. The Commission shall make the circularity strategies and any updates to those strategies publicly available, except for confidential information.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Part B of Annex IV by adapting the requirements on the content of the circularity strategy and the updates to that strategy to technical and scientific progress in vehicle manufacturing and management of end-of-life vehicles, to market developments in the automotive sector and to regulatory changes.

8. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 83 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] and every six years thereafter, the Commission shall draw up and publish a report on the circularity of the automotive sector. The report shall be based in particular on circularity strategies and updates to such strategies.
provision_effect: Requires vehicle manufacturers to draw up, submit, monitor, update, and publicly disclose a circularity strategy for each type-approved vehicle type, describing actions to meet circularity requirements.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text expresses general support for the Commission's initiative on circularity and the Green Deal, and describes the automotive industry's longstanding commitment to vehicle longevity and recyclability, but does not take any specific directional stance on whether a mandatory circularity strategy mechanism (as established in Article 9) should or should not exist, or how it should be structured. The position is too general and descriptive to constitute a specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against the provision's concrete requirements.

source_text: • In some Member States lower insurance fees for clients accepting repair with second life parts or obligations for automotive repair workshops to offer “circular economy repair parts” (i.e. remanufactured or reused spare parts) are proven to be effective to stimulate growth of the second life parts market. Such examples demonstrate that this can be achieved by stimulating demand rather than setting mandatory targets. Repair, Re-use, Remanufacturing Extending product Life Increasing the longevity of vehicles is actively supported by the members of ACEA through the comprehensive remanufacturing programmes, among others. These programmes exist for many of the mechanical and electronic components. Design for sustainability The automobile industry is actively participating in the Circular Plastic Alliance and is currently supporting discussion on the untapped potential for recyclates in vehicles. Waste reduction, including eco-design of cars to facilitate re-use, repair, remanufacturing, and recycling is a key goal. Recycled content On top of the Circular Economy Action Plan, the European Climate Law defines clear climate and CO2 targets for 2050, suggesting carbon reductions efforts from all sectors. With this in mind, car manufacturers measure environmental performance (e.g. carbon intensity) internally to determine the most appropriate material for use in specific parts and components
context_before: • Treatment of a large number of ELVs of a single vehicle line will result in the availability of many spare parts and reduce demand • Illegal dismantling activities and related online sale lead to an oversupply of spare parts in a “grey sector” which reduces the business profitability of legal ATFs. It is therefore crucial to force online marketplaces to require from sellers a valid ATF registration number and contact details to ensure the spare parts originate from an actual ATF. Furthermore, this would also be a benefit for the customer. • In some cases, fraudulent spare parts (e.g. body parts) made with cheaper material are available on online marketplaces. Beyond the evident safety risk, these parts should also be banned unless evidence of conformity, certified by the Original Equipment manufacturer can be provided. • In some EU member states the whole ELV is considered as waste and every part or material dismantled from it shall automatically be classified as waste, which can generate confusion and legal uncertainty on the status (waste or non-waste) and transboundary shipment of parts dismantled for reuse. Clarifying that parts dismantled from ELVs in legal ATFs for reuse purpose (with all due traceability) are not waste and can be handled, transported and sold as products could prompt legal ATFs to further develop this activity

In some instances, the use of a recycled material instead of a more performant and lightweight virgin material can contradict 19/11/2020 2
article_title: Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
article_text:
Article 33 — Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall take necessary incentives to promote the reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components, whether removed during the use or end-of-life phase of a vehicle.
The incentives referred to in the first subparagraph 1 may include:
(a)a requirement for maintenance and repair operators to offer customers to repair a vehicle with used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components alongside offer to repair the vehicle with new parts and components, provided that such a requirement is formulated not to create excessive costs or administrative burdens for micro- and small enterprises;
(b)the use of economic incentives, including the establishment of a reduced rate of value added tax for used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components.
The Commission shall facilitate the exchange of information and sharing of best practices among Member States on such incentives.

2. The Commission shall monitor the effectiveness of the incentives given by Member States.
provision_effect: Requires Member States to take incentives promoting reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment of vehicle parts, including offering customers circular repair options and reduced VAT rates, while the Commission monitors effectiveness.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: ACEA explicitly advocates for stimulating the second-life parts market through demand-side incentives (e.g., lower insurance fees, obligations for repair workshops to offer remanufactured/reused parts) rather than mandatory targets — and the provision directly implements this approach by requiring Member States to adopt incentives such as offering circular repair options alongside new parts. The provision's mechanism closely mirrors the specific examples ACEA cited as effective, confirming a clear directional alignment.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6930187 - 19/11/2020 +32 2 315 24 60 Avenue de Broqueville 12 info@plasticsrecyclers.eu 1150 Brussels – Belgium www.plasticsrecyclers.eu Feedback on Roadmap for: End-of-life vehicles – revision of EU rules Plastics Recyclers Europe (PRE) represents the interest of the plastics recycling industry. Several of PRE members are specialized in treating the shredder light fraction resulting from the ELV treatment to produce plastics recyclate for use in the traditional plastics compounding and converting industries. A fraction of this material is already being used in the automotive supply chain. It is with great interest that we read the inception impact assessment. In our response we will provide suggestions on how to improve collection of ELV, and why this would be beneficial to increase the recycling activity, we will provide suggestions for improvements in ELV treatment, comment on recycling targets, and suggestions for a system to define recycled content targets. These comments are a start, and we would be available to provide more in later stages. Furthermore, we would be happy to facilitate contact between any desk officers and/or consultants to our members for the impact assessment. In summary: • A reimbursement should be provided to last holder of a vehicle, to address the proper end-of-life treatment of vehicles and to remedy the “missing vehicle” issue
context_after: In summary: • A reimbursement should be provided to last holder of a vehicle, to address the proper end-of-life treatment of vehicles and to remedy the “missing vehicle” issue. • Delegated power should be given to the European Commission to amend the provisions on treatment to ensure separate collection of certain parts for recycling. • Recycling targets should be set for thermoplastic material • Overall vehicle recycled content levels may be set, but this must be complemented by a procedure whereby the European Commission is given delegated power to set recycled content requirements per specific vehicle components. Collection At present many old vehicles do not reach authorized treatment facilities as they are exported, legally or illegally, to countries outside of the EU. Such exports are a natural consequence of economic incentives. While it is beneficial that the holder of an old vehicle can dispose its vehicle free of charge, as otherwise this may or may not come at a fee depending on the state of the vehicle, value of spare parts, and subsequent gains from recovery. However, the holder of such an old vehicle is normally able to find a buyer willing to pay small amount for the vehicle. These buyers then deregister the vehicle and export the vehicle to countries outside of the EU where they can get a higher price for the old vehicle
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory minimum recycled content requirement of 25% post-consumer plastic waste in new vehicle types, with at least 25% of that target sourced from end-of-life vehicles, and empowers the Commission to set similar requirements for steel, aluminium, and other materials.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Plastics Recyclers Europe explicitly states it will provide "suggestions for a system to define recycled content targets" for ELV-derived plastics, and notes its members already supply plastics recyclate into the automotive supply chain — directly advocating for the kind of recycled content targets that Article 6 establishes. The provision delivers concrete mandatory recycled content requirements (25% post-consumer plastic, with an ELV-specific sub-target), which is precisely the direction PRE was pushing for.

article_text:
Article 36 — Shipments of end-of-life vehicles
1. Treatment of end-of-life vehicles may be undertaken outside the Union, provided that the shipment of end-of-life vehicles is in compliance with Regulation (EC) No 1013/2006.

2. Shipments of end-of-life vehicles from the Union to a third country in accordance with paragraph 1 shall only count towards the fulfilment of obligations and targets set out in Article 34 if the exporter of the end-of-life vehicles provides documentary evidence approved by the competent authority of destination demonstrating that the treatment took place in conditions that are broadly equivalent to the requirements laid down in this Regulation and to human health and environmental protection requirements laid down in other Union legislation.
provision_effect: Allows treatment of end-of-life vehicles outside the EU provided shipments comply with Regulation (EC) No 1013/2006, and only counts such treatment toward targets if the exporter proves conditions broadly equivalent to EU requirements.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org identifies illegal and legal exports of end-of-life vehicles outside the EU as a key problem undermining proper treatment and collection, but does not take a specific directional stance on whether exports should be permitted under equivalence conditions or banned outright. The provision addresses the export issue by setting conditions for when overseas treatment counts toward targets, but it is genuinely unclear whether this satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's concern — the org's text diagnoses the problem without advocating for a specific regulatory mechanism to address it.

article_title: Delivery of end-of-life vehicles to authorised treatment facilities
article_text:
Article 24 — Delivery of end-of-life vehicles to authorised treatment facilities
1. All end-of-life vehicles shall be delivered for treatment to authorised treatment facilities.

2. Delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to an authorised treatment facility shall be free of charge for the last owner of a vehicle unless the end-of-life vehicle lacks any of the essential vehicle parts or components, except the electric vehicle battery, or contains waste which has been added to the end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Requires all end-of-life vehicles to be delivered to authorised treatment facilities, with delivery being free of charge for the last owner unless the vehicle lacks essential parts or contains added waste.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for a reimbursement/free-of-charge mechanism for the last holder of a vehicle to incentivise proper end-of-life treatment and address the "missing vehicle" issue caused by vehicles being exported rather than reaching authorised treatment facilities. The provision directly delivers this by mandating delivery to authorised treatment facilities and making it free of charge for the last owner, which aligns with the org's specific ask to address economic disincentives that lead to vehicles bypassing proper treatment channels.

article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes the conditions under which a suspended used vehicle export may be released, requires customs authorities to notify competent authorities after each release for export, and clarifies that release for export does not constitute proof of compliance with Union law.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Plastics Recyclers Europe explicitly identifies illegal and legal exports of old vehicles to non-EU countries as a key problem undermining proper end-of-life treatment, and advocates for addressing this issue. Article 42 establishes a concrete regulatory mechanism — including suspension review timelines, competent authority approval requirements, and post-export notifications — that directly targets the control of used vehicle exports, acting in the direction the organisation advocated for (curbing uncontrolled exports that bypass EU end-of-life treatment requirements).

source_text: +32 2 315 24 60 Avenue de Broqueville 12 info@plasticsrecyclers.eu 1150 Brussels – Belgium www.plasticsrecyclers.eu The inception impact assessment indicates that digital solutions can be found to the issue of “missing vehicles”. While further improvements in digital tracking of vehicles may be part of a solution, the provision of an economic incentive would be a more potent way of ensuring that the end of life stage of the vehicle does not transpire outside of the EU. The provision of a fee to the holder of an old vehicle, would provide such an incentive and ensure that vehicles sold in the EU are treated in the EU. Such a payment to the holder of an old vehicle can be financed though Extended Producer Responsibility mechanisms. The height of the fee should not be decided in the National context, but at EU level in order to ensure that there are no intra-EU transfers of old vehicles to benefit from the differences in the fee provided. Otherwise, the EPR systems in the country with the highest reimbursement would start bearing a disproportional part of the burden of financing the reimbursement. The measure would be beneficial to the absolute amount of plastics recycled within the EU. Treatment Another point for consideration would be greater specification to be included in Annex I on treatment
context_before: These buyers then deregister the vehicle and export the vehicle to countries outside of the EU where they can get a higher price for the old vehicle. Outside of the EU, such vehicles may be given a short second life, whereafter they become waste there or may be scrapped for parts and the remaining husk of the vehicle treated in a non-environmentally sound manner.
context_after: Treatment Another point for consideration would be greater specification to be included in Annex I on treatment. With regards to plastics this is currently worded as follows: removal of tyres and large plastic components (bumpers, dashboard, fluid containers, etc.), if these materials are not segregated in the shredding process in such a way that they can be effectively recycled as materials, Currently dismantling of plastics components is frequently not done as they are either not considered to be “large” or because the polymers can be recovered from the shredder light fraction. While the latter is quite acceptable, the former is something that can be discussed. To give a practical example, car headlights are made from transparent polycarbonate. Transparent polycarbonate is a high value thermoplastic material that is at present not recovered from the shredder light fraction. Disassembly of car headlights would result in a waste stream that would be highly recyclable. Yet this is only rarely done, and one could argue that it is not needed as the component is not “large” (especially when compared with the examples mentioned). Of course, such a dismantling step would result in costs for ELV treatment and it is uncertain whether such cost would outweigh societal benefits
provision_effect: Establishes the scope of financial contributions producers must pay under extended producer responsibility (EPR), covering collection, treatment, awareness campaigns, notification systems, and data reporting costs, with monitoring by competent authorities and guarantees for individual EPR fulfilment.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org specifically advocates for EPR mechanisms to finance a fee/payment to holders of old vehicles as an economic incentive to ensure end-of-life vehicles are treated within the EU, and argues this fee should be set at EU level rather than nationally. The provision establishes EPR financial contributions but covers collection, treatment, awareness, and data costs — it does not include a payment/fee to vehicle holders, nor does it set contribution levels at EU level. There is a genuine connection (both concern EPR financial mechanisms for ELVs), but it is unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the org's specific ask for a holder-incentive fee set at EU level.

source_text: +32 2 315 24 60 Avenue de Broqueville 12 info@plasticsrecyclers.eu 1150 Brussels – Belgium www.plasticsrecyclers.eu The inception impact assessment indicates that digital solutions can be found to the issue of “missing vehicles”. While further improvements in digital tracking of vehicles may be part of a solution, the provision of an economic incentive would be a more potent way of ensuring that the end of life stage of the vehicle does not transpire outside of the EU. The provision of a fee to the holder of an old vehicle, would provide such an incentive and ensure that vehicles sold in the EU are treated in the EU. Such a payment to the holder of an old vehicle can be financed though Extended Producer Responsibility mechanisms. The height of the fee should not be decided in the National context, but at EU level in order to ensure that there are no intra-EU transfers of old vehicles to benefit from the differences in the fee provided. Otherwise, the EPR systems in the country with the highest reimbursement would start bearing a disproportional part of the burden of financing the reimbursement. The measure would be beneficial to the absolute amount of plastics recycled within the EU. Treatment Another point for consideration would be greater specification to be included in Annex I on treatment
context_before: These buyers then deregister the vehicle and export the vehicle to countries outside of the EU where they can get a higher price for the old vehicle. Outside of the EU, such vehicles may be given a short second life, whereafter they become waste there or may be scrapped for parts and the remaining husk of the vehicle treated in a non-environmentally sound manner.
context_after: Treatment Another point for consideration would be greater specification to be included in Annex I on treatment. With regards to plastics this is currently worded as follows: removal of tyres and large plastic components (bumpers, dashboard, fluid containers, etc.), if these materials are not segregated in the shredding process in such a way that they can be effectively recycled as materials, Currently dismantling of plastics components is frequently not done as they are either not considered to be “large” or because the polymers can be recovered from the shredder light fraction. While the latter is quite acceptable, the former is something that can be discussed. To give a practical example, car headlights are made from transparent polycarbonate. Transparent polycarbonate is a high value thermoplastic material that is at present not recovered from the shredder light fraction. Disassembly of car headlights would result in a waste stream that would be highly recyclable. Yet this is only rarely done, and one could argue that it is not needed as the component is not “large” (especially when compared with the examples mentioned). Of course, such a dismantling step would result in costs for ELV treatment and it is uncertain whether such cost would outweigh societal benefits
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory minimum recycled content requirement of 25% post-consumer plastic waste in new vehicle types, with at least 25% of that target sourced from end-of-life vehicles, and empowers the Commission to set similar requirements for steel, aluminium, and other materials.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org's position focuses specifically on EPR-financed financial incentives (fees to holders of old vehicles) to prevent end-of-life vehicles from being treated outside the EU, and on treatment specifications in Annex I — neither of which relates to the mandatory recycled content targets established in Article 6. The org text contains no substantive advocacy position on minimum recycled content requirements in vehicles.

article_title: Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
article_text:
Article 22 — Cost allocation mechanism for vehicles becoming end-of-life vehicles in another Member State
1. Where a vehicle becomes an end-of-life vehicle in another Member State than the Member State within whose territory the vehicle was made available on the market for the first time, the producer of that vehicle or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, the producer responsibility organisation shall ensure that the net costs of waste management operations referred to in Article 20 incurred by waste management operators in other Member States are covered.

2. A producer or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, a producer responsibility organisation shall:
(a)designate by a written mandate an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility in each Member State;
(b)establish cross-border cooperation mechanisms with the waste management operators carrying out waste management operations referred to in Article 20.

3. The Member State where the vehicle became an end-of-life vehicle shall monitor producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’ compliance with paragraphs 1 and 2. The monitoring shall be based on the information reported and verified by producers’ or, where appointed in accordance with Article 18, producer responsibility organisations’, to the competent authorities on the implementation of paragraphs 1 and 2, in particular on the calculation and allocation of costs for the management of end-of-life vehicles referred to in paragraph 1, with due regard for business confidentiality and other concerns regarding competitiveness.

4. Where necessary to ensure compliance with this Article and avoid distortion of the single market, the Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 this Regulation by laying down detailed rules on the obligations of the producers, Member States and waste management operators and the features of the mechanisms referred to in paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a cross-border cost allocation mechanism requiring producers or PROs to cover net waste management costs when vehicles become end-of-life in a different Member State than where they were first placed on the market, including designation of representatives and cross-border cooperation mechanisms.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Both the org and the provision address the problem of vehicles reaching end-of-life outside their country of first sale, and both invoke EPR mechanisms as the solution. However, the org specifically advocates for an EU-level financial incentive (a fee/payment) to vehicle holders to keep end-of-life treatment within the EU, and warns against national-level fee-setting to avoid intra-EU vehicle transfers. The provision instead establishes a cost-coverage obligation on producers/PROs for cross-border ELV management, but does not create a consumer-facing fee or set it at EU level. It is genuinely unclear whether this mechanism satisfies, contradicts, or simply bypasses the org's specific ask for a harmonised holder-payment incentive.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)6930872 - 19/11/2020 19 November 2020 Brussels The EEB’s supporting paper for the Open Public Consultation (feed back) to the road map / inception impact assessment for the review of the End-of-Life Vehicles Directive In this document, the European Environmental Bureau (EEB) provides technical input and policy recommendations for the revision of the End-of-Life-Vehicles (ELVs) Directive, which aims to tackle the environmental impacts of ELVs treatment in and outside the EU. The recommendations outlined below can help the automotive sector move towards more responsible and circular business models. Officially reported (and treated ELVs amount to 6.5 million vehicles in the EU28 (incl. UK) in 2017. According to calculations published by the EC the whereabout of 3.7 million is unknown. It is also unknown if these vehicles are (illegally) exported or (illegally) treated with the EU. Regardless of the problems of the unknown whereabouts the treatment of the ELVs raise several environmental challenges for EU governments. Large amounts of waste could be cut down if national authorities put in place the right policies and followed the Waste Management Hierarchy outlined in the EU’s Circular Economy strategy. According to this strategy, waste streams should be tackled at the source by improving the design of vehicles at production stage, which would help reduce most of the environmental impact of ELVs
context_after: According to this strategy, waste streams should be tackled at the source by improving the design of vehicles at production stage, which would help reduce most of the environmental impact of ELVs. Waste prevention measures should also look to eliminate the toxic content of this waste stream in order to: • Incentivise durability, upgradability and repairability; • Facilitate high quality recycling and avoid the circulation of toxic substances. The following issues are priorities for the EEB and its 168 member associations, which aim to bring the ELVs Directive closer to an overarching policy of the EU’s Circular Economy. Even if important, we have intentionally not covered the issues related to illegal exports, registration and de-registration of vehicles, as these are well covered by other stakeholders. Nevertheless we would like to draw attention to the recently published study of UNEP1 which calls for regulatory action to avoid the export of old and substandard vehicles to developing countries, causing high air pollution and hazardous waste in the developing countries
article_text:
Article 9 — Circularity strategy
1. For each vehicle type that is type-approved under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], the manufacturer shall draw up a circularity strategy.

2. The circularity strategy shall describe which actions the manufacturers will take to follow-up on their obligations to ensure that the circularity requirements in Chapter II, which are verified in the type-approval procedures and which are applicable to the vehicle type concerned, are met.

3. The circularity strategy shall contain the elements laid down in Part A of Annex IV.

4. The manufacturer shall provide a copy of the circularity strategy to the Commission within 30 days after the type-approval for the concerned vehicle type has been granted.

5. The manufacturer shall monitor and follow up on the actions contained in the circularity strategy and update the strategy every five years in accordance with Part B of Annex IV. The updated circularity strategy shall be provided to the type-approval authority that issued the type-approval for the vehicle type and to the Commisssion.

6. The Commission shall make the circularity strategies and any updates to those strategies publicly available, except for confidential information.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Part B of Annex IV by adapting the requirements on the content of the circularity strategy and the updates to that strategy to technical and scientific progress in vehicle manufacturing and management of end-of-life vehicles, to market developments in the automotive sector and to regulatory changes.

8. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 83 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] and every six years thereafter, the Commission shall draw up and publish a report on the circularity of the automotive sector. The report shall be based in particular on circularity strategies and updates to such strategies.
provision_effect: Requires vehicle manufacturers to draw up, monitor, update, and publicly disclose a circularity strategy for each type-approved vehicle type, describing actions to meet circularity requirements.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The EEB explicitly advocates for moving the automotive sector toward "more responsible and circular business models" and tackling waste at the source by improving vehicle design at the production stage — a directional push for circularity obligations on manufacturers. Article 9 directly responds to this by mandating manufacturer-level circularity strategies tied to type-approval, which is a concrete regulatory mechanism in exactly the direction the EEB advocated for.

article_text:
Article 36 — Shipments of end-of-life vehicles
1. Treatment of end-of-life vehicles may be undertaken outside the Union, provided that the shipment of end-of-life vehicles is in compliance with Regulation (EC) No 1013/2006.

2. Shipments of end-of-life vehicles from the Union to a third country in accordance with paragraph 1 shall only count towards the fulfilment of obligations and targets set out in Article 34 if the exporter of the end-of-life vehicles provides documentary evidence approved by the competent authority of destination demonstrating that the treatment took place in conditions that are broadly equivalent to the requirements laid down in this Regulation and to human health and environmental protection requirements laid down in other Union legislation.
provision_effect: Allows treatment of end-of-life vehicles outside the EU provided shipments comply with existing regulation, and conditions exports counting toward targets on demonstrating broadly equivalent treatment conditions.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The EEB's position explicitly acknowledges the environmental challenges of ELV treatment both inside and outside the EU, and raises concerns about illegal exports and unknown whereabouts of vehicles — suggesting a concern about export practices. However, the org text does not take a clear directional stance on whether exports for treatment should be permitted under equivalence conditions (as Article 36 establishes), nor does it explicitly advocate for stricter export bans or endorse the equivalence mechanism. A genuine connection exists between the EEB's concern about environmental impacts of ELV treatment outside the EU and this provision, but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the EEB's position.

source_text: Reuse and preparation for reuse The ELV’s Directive should better reflect the Waste Management Hierarchy, where prevention is the top priority, followed by reuse. The current target does not give enough attention to reuse or preparation for reuse. Not only should this difference from recycling be visible in reporting the rates, but also a separate mandatory target for reuse should be considered to incentivise this activity. Catalunya for instance established a separate reuse target of 5% of weight for 2017, followed by 10% for 2021 and 15% for 2026. Currently the reported reuse rates in the EU differ between zero and > 30% (Eurostat). Obviously, it is necessary to establish an exchange on best practices to encourage and support reuse. There is also the need for a harmonised method for the calculation of reuse (currently two methods can be applied). Rules to incentivise reuse should also be harmonised across the EU to avoid market distortions. We recommend binding requirements to ensure non-destructive disassembly and removal of all reusable modules from the vehicle before shredding, and to store them safely for reuse. The easiness and economic feasibility of dismantling before shredding depends on design for dismantling, therefore that should also be one of the minimum requirements for the design stage. Appropriate dismantling of layers and modules will also facilitate quality recycling
context_before: 1 https://www.unep.org/news-and-stories/press-release/new-un-report-details-environmental- impacts-export-used-vehicles European Environmental Bureau Rue des Deux Eglises, 14-16 - B-1000 Brussels Europe's largest network of Tel.: +32 2 289 10 90 environmental citizens’ organisations Email: eeb@eeb.org www.eeb.org International non-profit association – EC register for interest representatives: Association internationale sans but lucratif Identification number: 06798511314-27
context_after: Appropriate dismantling of layers and modules will also facilitate quality recycling. It also depends on a functioning IDIS (International Dismantling Information System) which would need to include such dismantling information (currently not included instead ‘necessary’ dismantling is addressed only). It might be necessary to consider additional funding schemes within the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework to finance those operations. Recycling targets - calculation methodology The calculation methodology for the targets should be transparent and the same across all Member States to reflect the progress of the sector in terms of reuse and recycling and to make data more reliable and comparative. Alignment of the definition of recycling with the Waste Framework Directive is needed: in contrast to the Waste Framework Directive the ELV Directive allows to account backfilling for recycling and the calculation point for the recycling is not defined. Any double calculation in two different registries should be avoided. Any residues coming from recycling operations should be deducted from the input to recycling in order to avoid inaccurate inflating of reported rates. Additionally, a target for recovery should be taken out of the Directive. Instead we propose a ban of landfilling for shredder/ dismantling residues with organic matters
provision_effect: Requires producer responsibility organisations to modulate financial contributions from producers based on criteria including vehicle weight, drivetrain type, recyclability/reusability rates, dismantling time, problematic materials, recycled content, and hazardous substances.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The EEB explicitly advocates for incentivising reuse and non-destructive disassembly before shredding, and for design-for-dismantling requirements. Article 21's fee modulation criteria directly incorporate dismantling time (21(1)(d)), recyclability and reusability rates (21(1)(c)), and materials hindering quality recycling (21(1)(e)) as financial levers — concrete mechanisms that incentivise the design-for-dismantling and reusability outcomes the EEB called for. This represents a clear directional alignment between the org's specific asks and the provision's action.

article_text:
Article 34 — Reuse, recycling and recovery targets
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the calendar year following 36 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation], Member States shall ensure that the following targets are met by the waste management operators:
(a)the reuse and recovery, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 95 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year;
(b)the reuse and recycling, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 85 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year.

2. From [OP: please insert a date = the first day of the calendar year following 60 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation] Member States shall ensure that waste management operators achieve a yearly target for the recycling of plastics of at least 30 % of the total weight of plastics contained in the vehicles delivered to the waste management operators.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory reuse/recovery and reuse/recycling targets (95% and 85% respectively) for waste management operators, plus a 30% plastics recycling target, but does not set a separate standalone reuse target.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: The EEB explicitly advocates for a separate mandatory reuse target (distinct from recycling), citing examples like Catalunya's standalone reuse targets, and criticises the current approach for not giving enough attention to reuse as a distinct category. The provision, however, continues to bundle reuse together with recovery/recycling in combined targets (95% reuse+recovery; 85% reuse+recycling), with no standalone reuse target — directly contrary to what the EEB asked for.

source_text: However, the circulation of hazardous substances into new products should be avoided. The current (1c) of the ELV Directive does not prevent this aspect, since more emphasis is put on quantity rather than quality, which might even incentivise the dilution of hazardous substances and their recirculation to new production (ie not well sorted plastics from shredder light fraction). The uptake of recycled contents should ensure the same standards apply for recycled materials as for virgin with regards to performance and chemical contents, and the potential incentives and obligations should clearly refer to this. Extended Producer’s Responsibility It is recognised that large amounts of ELVs are treated outside the country where they were first used or even outside the EU. Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) should have access to information regarding ELVs, while EU governments should make sure that information is exchanged transparently across all parties involved. This is also stipulated in of Directive 2018/849, where the use of certificates is suggested – however not enforced well enough by member states. For countries outside the EU, the situation might be more difficult, nevertheless, we encourage the Commission to consider any kind of secured system of financial flow where the EPR fee paid in the EU would follow the vehicle to its last end-of-life stage in order to finance its safe treatment, wherever it will take place
context_before: In a study for the European Commission, supporting the evaluation of the ELVD (2020) a number of self-commitments of car manufacturers are mentioned. E.g. Volvo aims to have by 2025 25% recycled plastic (out of all plastic) in every new car. VW reported in 2009 a total of 501 kg of recycled metals in a VW Golf. Material specific targets could be differentiated per material and staged over 2025 and then 2030 years.
