Vehicle owners who do not pay the insurance risk being fined an amount equal to the average cost of basic coverage plus a penalty of 2 euros per day87. This daily penalty took effect at the start of 2018. If there should be an accident, insurers also play an important role. While the damaged vehicle (whether it is assessed as technically or economically unrepairable) still belongs to the vehicle owner, insurers offer their insurees an online auction service to enable them to get the best price for their vehicle and so that the insurance payment will be as low as possible
article_text:
Article 45 — Electronic systems
1. The MOVE-HUB electronic system developed by the Commission shall be used for exchanging Vehicle Identification Number and information on the vehicle registration and roadworthiness status between national vehicle registers and electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States, as well as, to interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, where necessary for controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. The MOVE-HUB electronic system, referred to in paragraph 1 shall provide at least the following functionalities:
(a)exchange the data in real-time with the national vehicle registers, national electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States interconnected with it;
(b)enable an automated electronic check of data provided in a roadworthiness certificate as referred to in Annex II of Directive 2014/45/EU of the date of first registration of a vehicle, as well as of the Member State where a vehicle was last registered, as referred to in Directive 1999/37/EC, to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with the requirements set out in Article 38, Article 39(1) and Article 40;
(c)interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2399 for the purpose of exchanging data and support the process of exchange of information referred to in Articles 39(1) and Article 40(2), as well as support the notifications referred to in Articles 41 to 43;
(d)for the purpose of cooperation with third countries under Article 44(2), allow electronic exchange of information with the competent authorities of third countries which have notified to the Commission pursuant to Article 40(2) the specific conditions for the import of used vehicles that they apply.

3. Member States shall interconnect their national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness with the MOVE-HUB electronic system referred to in paragraph 1. That interconnection shall be operational within 2 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

4. The Commission shall interconnect the MOVE-HUB system referred to in paragraph 1 to the EU Customs Single Window Certificate Exchange System established in Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2399, so that the automated controls referred to in Article 39 and Article 40(2) and the notifications referred to in Articles 41, 42 and 43 can be performed. That interconnection shall be operational within 4 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

5. The Commission shall adopt the implementing acts laying down the necessary arrangements for the implementation of the functionalities of the MOVE-HUB referred to in paragraph 2, including the technical aspects necessary for the interconnection of national electronic systems to the MOVE-HUB, the conditions of connection to MOVE-HUB, the data to be transmitted by the national systems and the format for the transmission of that data through the interconnected national systems.
The implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Establishes the MOVE-HUB electronic system for exchanging vehicle identification and roadworthiness data between Member States and customs systems to facilitate controls on used vehicle exports.

article_title: Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
article_text:
Article 44 — Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
1. Member States shall mutually assist one another in the implementation of this Section through exchange of information at bilateral level, in particular for the purpose of verifying the status of a vehicle, including the verification of its registration status in the Member State in which it was previously registered.

2. Where appropriate, competent authorities of Member States shall also cooperate with administrative authorities from third countries. Such cooperation may include sharing of relevant information, conducting joint inspections, and other forms of mutual assistance as deemed necessary to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing the export of used vehicles.

3. Customs authorities and competent authorities of Member States shall cooperate in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and exchange information necessary for the fulfilment of their functions under this Regulation, including via electronic means. The customs authorities may communicate, in accordance with Article 12(1) and Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, confidential information acquired by the customs authorities in the course of performing their duties, or provided to the customs authorities on a confidential basis, to the competent authority of the Member State where the operator or trader is established.

4. Where the competent authorities have received information in accordance with paragraphs 1 to 3 of this Article, those competent authorities may communicate that information to competent authorities from other Member States.

5. Risk-related information shall be exchanged as follows:
(a)between customs authorities in accordance with Article 46(5) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)between customs authorities and the Commission in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)between customs authorities and competent authorities, including competent authorities from other Member States, in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework for cooperation and information exchange among Member State authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities regarding the export and registration status of used vehicles.

article_text:
Article 40 — Risk management and customs controls
1. For the purpose of enforcing the provisions laid down in Article 38, customs authorities shall carry out controls on used vehicles to be exported in accordance with Articles 46 and 47 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013. Without prejudice to Article 39, such controls shall primarily be based on risk analysis, as established in Article 46(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

2. In addition to the risk management referred to in paragraph 1, once the interconnection referred to in Article 45 is operational, customs shall use these electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1) to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with specific conditions linked to the protection of the environment or road safety in accordance with paragraph 3 of this Article.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to supplement this Regulation by setting out the compliance conditions referred to in paragraph 2, including specific conditions applied to the import of used vehicles by the third country of import linked to the protection of the environment and road safety, when such conditions have been notified by that third country to the Commission. Those conditions shall be verifiable against the information available in the electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1).
provision_effect: Establishes a risk-based customs control framework for used vehicle exports, including delegated acts to set compliance conditions linked to environmental protection and road safety verifiable through electronic systems.

article_title: Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
article_text:
Article 38 — Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] used vehicles to be exported shall be subject to the controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. This Section is without prejudice to any other provisions of this Regulation as well as to other Union legal acts governing the release for export of goods, in particular Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and its Articles 46, 47, 267 and 269.

3. Used vehicles may be exported only if they are:
(a)not end-of-life vehicles based on the criteria listed in Annex I;
(b)considered roadworthy in the Member State where the vehicles were last registered, in accordance with Article 5(1), points (a) and (b), and Article 8 of Directive 2014/45/EU.

4. The following information shall be provided or made available to customs authorities for each used vehicle to be exported:
(a)the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the used vehicle and the identification of the Member State where the vehicle was last registered;
(b)a statement confirming that the used vehicle fulfills the requirements set out in paragraph.

5. In order to verify the compliance with this Section on allowing a used vehicle to be released for export:
(a)until the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 44, and, where necessary, shall take into account such exchange of information and cooperation in order to allow a used vehicle to be released for export;
(b)once the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, Articles 39, 40(2) and (3) and 42(3) shall apply, and notifications and other exchanges under Articles 41 to 43 shall take place by means of those electronic systems.

6. A used vehicle to be exported shall not:
(a)be placed under a customs procedure based on a simplified declaration pursuant to Article 166 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)be subject to an entry in the declarant’s records pursuant to Article 182 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)be subject to self-assessment pursuant to Article 185 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts, in accordance with Article 50 of this Regulation, to amend the criteria listed in Annex I determining whether a used vehicle is an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes controls and requirements for the export of used vehicles, including roadworthiness checks, VIN identification, and prohibition on simplified customs declarations.

article_title: Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
article_text:
Article 39 — Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
1. Before releasing used vehicles for export, customs shall verify electronically and automatically via the electronic systems referred to in Article 45, that based on the Vehicle Identification Number and the information on the Member State of last registration, the vehicle is considered roadworthy in accordance with Article 38(3), point (b).

2. Where the information provided or made available to customs does not correspond to the information in the national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness pursuant to paragraph 1, customs authorities shall not release that vehicle for export and shall inform the economic operator concerned thereof through these systems.

source_text: 5.4.3.2 Limitations of the scheme As mentioned, while the Czech Republic has an effective registration system, it does have certain limitations:  Cooperation between legal and illegal ELV processing actors: authorised ELV centres issue destruction certificates and illegal operators dismantle the vehicles. This cooperation is advantageous for both parties since illegal operators, which are not under the same economic constraints as legal actors, give a share of their revenues to authorised ELV centres. The purpose of environmental inspections is to prevent this type of illegal cooperation. Identifying authorised ELV centres that collaborate with illegal operators is relatively simple. When the environmental inspection agency identifies and controls an illegal ELV processing facility, it identifies all the VIN numbers of vehicles on the site. Then, it enters these numbers into the MA ISOH to see which ELV centre has issued destruction certificates. However, it is more and more difficult to identify the operators who collaborate since illegal operators destroy the VINs of vehicles so that they can no longer be linked to legal facilities. Furthermore, these facilities often process different types of waste (washing machines, etc.) making it easy to falsify their data. If the authorities identify this type of collaboration, the operators must pay a fine of up to 4,000 euros
context_before: 5.4.3 Overview of the scheme 5.4.3.1 Scheme efficiency The efficiency of the Czech scheme was not quantitatively evaluated. No specific indicator was defined when the system was set up. However, all the actors interviewed agree that the system is relatively resilient and effective to control the ELV processing channel. In fact, the owner, linked to his vehicle in the vehicle registration system of the Ministry of Transportation, is required to pay for his insurance as long as he is registered in the system. Enforcement of the regulation is very effective since it is overseen by the Ministry of Transportation and the Bureau of Insurance Companies. Controlling illegal operators is even more efficient given that the validity of the destruction certificate is verified via the MA ISOH system before the vehicle is withdrawn from the system, dissuading unauthorised ELV centres from processing ELVs. Global Overview of Incentive Schemes aiming to bring ELVs through Authorised Processing Channels | PAGE 94
context_after: If the authorities identify this type of collaboration, the operators must pay a fine of up to 4,000 euros.  Exported and imported vehicles: once a vehicle is declared as exported in the vehicle monitoring and registration system, there is no longer any control possible by the authorities. While a roadworthiness test certificate dated less than 30 days is required and the system keeps a record of the export, there is no information about the vehicle owner. Consequently, if the vehicle is illegally dismantled in another country, the system cannot know about it. As far as imports are concerned, owing to the opening of the borders, many vehicles are imported from neighbouring countries to be destroyed. These non- visible vehicles (not registered in the system of the Ministry of Transportation) are easily illegally dismantled in a country. Furthermore, the experts interviewed agree that most ELVs processed by illegal operators in the Czech Republic come from neighbouring countries and in particular from Poland and Slovakia. In fact, even if an inspection is carried out by customs it is impossible to differentiate an ELV from a used vehicle.  Enforcement of the law depends on private operators: this creates a major paradox since insurance companies can refuse to insure a driver with a poor driving record while vehicle owners are required to take out a minimum of third party insurance
article_title: Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
article_text:
Article 44 — Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
1. Member States shall mutually assist one another in the implementation of this Section through exchange of information at bilateral level, in particular for the purpose of verifying the status of a vehicle, including the verification of its registration status in the Member State in which it was previously registered.

2. Where appropriate, competent authorities of Member States shall also cooperate with administrative authorities from third countries. Such cooperation may include sharing of relevant information, conducting joint inspections, and other forms of mutual assistance as deemed necessary to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing the export of used vehicles.

3. Customs authorities and competent authorities of Member States shall cooperate in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and exchange information necessary for the fulfilment of their functions under this Regulation, including via electronic means. The customs authorities may communicate, in accordance with Article 12(1) and Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, confidential information acquired by the customs authorities in the course of performing their duties, or provided to the customs authorities on a confidential basis, to the competent authority of the Member State where the operator or trader is established.

4. Where the competent authorities have received information in accordance with paragraphs 1 to 3 of this Article, those competent authorities may communicate that information to competent authorities from other Member States.

5. Risk-related information shall be exchanged as follows:
(a)between customs authorities in accordance with Article 46(5) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)between customs authorities and the Commission in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)between customs authorities and competent authorities, including competent authorities from other Member States, in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework for cooperation and information exchange among Member State authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities to verify vehicle status and ensure compliance with regulations governing the export of used vehicles.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text describes limitations of the Czech ELV registration system, specifically the challenge of identifying illegal ELV processing operators who cooperate with authorised centres and destroy VINs to evade detection. This is background/descriptive content about enforcement challenges in a specific national context, with no specific advocacy position or directional ask regarding inter-authority cooperation or information exchange mechanisms. The org text does not advocate for or against the type of cross-border cooperation framework established in Article 44.

article_text:
Article 41 — Suspension
1. Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a used vehicle to be exported may not comply with the requirements of this Section, the customs authorities shall suspend the release for export of that used vehicle. They shall also immediately notify the competent authorities of the suspension and transmit all relevant information needed to determine whether the used vehicle complies with the requirements of this Regulation and may be released for export.

2. For the purpose of determining whether a used vehicle, subject to suspension as referred to in paragraph 1, complies with this Regulation, the competent authorities may request, from any person involved in the export of that used vehicle, additional information, including information on the sale or transfer of vehicle ownership, such as a copy of the invoice or contract, and documentary evidence that that used vehicle is destined for further use.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for customs authorities to suspend the release for export of used vehicles suspected of non-compliance, and allows competent authorities to request additional information to determine compliance.

article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes the conditions under which a used vehicle suspended from export must be released for export, and requires customs authorities to notify the competent authority after each such release.

organisation: Ministry of the Environment of the Czech Republic
source_text: The Czech Republic is aware of and approve the necessarity for an update of ELV directive, mainly because of increasing numbers of electric vehicles put on market these days and necessarity of dealing with electric vehicles at the end of their life time. Below are some points, which should be solved. 1)	First of all, we assume, that it is necessary to settle the rules for handling with electric vehicles in case of tradic accidents because of high probability of fire of lithium batteries. Firemen must have information about batteries (composition, reactivity, means by which to extinguish potential fire after car accident etc.). Nowadays, there is lack of these information. Then there should be concrete rules for authorised treatment facilities (ATFs), how to handle with these batteries. Manufacturers of the electric vehicles (or lithium batteries) who put vehicles with lithium batteries on the market should obligatory provide these essential information to operators of ATFs. Here should be ELV directive complementrary to Waste Battery Directive 2)	European Commission proposes mandatory recycled content in vehicle´s materials. It should be considered for which materials mandatory recycled content is egligible. For example for plastics, there would be problems with safety and quality requirements of such plastics with recycled content
context_after: For example for plastics, there would be problems with safety and quality requirements of such plastics with recycled content. But also nowadays recycling of waste plastics from ELVs is problematic, because of contamination by aditives that make recycling difficult (fuel, minera oil), assuming recycled content in these plastics, recycling of waste plastics could be practically impossible. There is necessary discussion with processors/recyclators of waste plastics from ELVs.  3)	Deregistration of vehicles (ELVs) within EU In the Czech Republic there is ELV Information System of Waste Management (called „MA ISOH“) where operator of ATFs send information about received ELVs. Only Ministry of the Environment, Czech Environment Inspection, Police of Czech Republic and few others have access to detailed information about ELVs. The public can check by identification number of vehicle (VIN), registration mark or number of special code included in Certificate of Destruction, whether and where the vehicle (ELV) was taken over (through web pages of Ministry of the Environment here: https://autovraky.mzp.cz/autovrak/webklient/, it is only in czech language). This information system allow anyone to check, whether the vehicle was put into ATF. Based on this, deregistration of (foreign) vehicle can be much easier, because now it is difficult to verify destruction ELV in ATF because of lack of list of ATFs in some MS
article_title: Information on removal and replacement of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers to provide waste management and repair operators with unrestricted, standardised access to information enabling safe removal and replacement of EV batteries, e-drive motors, critical raw materials, and other specified parts and components.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The Czech Republic explicitly advocates that manufacturers of electric vehicles should be obligatorily required to provide essential information about lithium batteries to operators of authorised treatment facilities (ATFs). Article 11 directly delivers this by mandating that manufacturers provide waste management operators with standardised, unrestricted access to information enabling safe removal and replacement of EV batteries and related components, which clearly responds to the organisation's specific ask.

source_text: WEEE AUDITS Dokument: Dokument ID: Datum: PM – Comments on the revision of the ELV Directive WA-292-2021 2021-08-04 Introduction The EPR legislation for ELV should be coordinate with the WEEE Directive in order to promote better treatment and it is important to include all electrical vehicles not considered to be EEE, according to the WEEE Directive. EEE do have the same recycling and recovery challenges, weather it come from ELV or WEEE. It is important to acknowledge that the circular economy will increase cost and that secondary materials are more expensive than virgin materials. Therefore, a robust financing environment has to be implemented. With an enhanced legislation and enforcement for treatment of ELVs some companies will not manage to demonstrate compliance. This may be bad for these companies but it is good for the environment. Eco Design - targets The prime incentive for the circular economy is targets for recycled material in new products. This is particularly interesting for plastics and would create a market demand. Recycled material is more expensive than virgin material and it is important to facilitate an environment that promote secondary materials in new products. End-of-life and Waste The WFD defines the waste hierarchy in . It would be useful if the ELV directive would base its terminology on the WFD, as WEEE AUDITS also have suggested for the proposed Battery Regulation. Re-use [defined in the WFD] is a term used before end-of life & waste
context_after: Re-use [defined in the WFD] is a term used before end-of life & waste. In this state other terms such as remanufacturing and repurposing [from the proposed Battery Regulation] can be used to describe different processes. When an object has been discarded, it is waste, and the term preparing for re-use can be used for parts that shall be returned to the market. It is important to have a distinct definition of when a product becomes waste. With these definitions all vehicles entering an ATF would be waste and re-use, remanufacturing and repurposing are not relevant processes. Removal New vehicles contain significant amount of electrical and electronic equipment. This equipment should be removed for special treatment, similar to requirements specified in the WEEE Directive. Removal of other parts is also useful from a circular perspective. There are different incentives for removal before shredding: - De-pollution – remove hazardous materials, - Special treatment – remove material in order to obtain better recycling - Preparing for re-use – remove components that can undergo preparing for re-use before returning to the market. Market incentive The waste hierarchy in article 4 in the waste framework directive promote preparing for re-use as opposed to recycling. It is however important to remember that preparing for re-use is depending on a market demand and is very limited, compared to recycling. 2
article_title: Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
article_text:
Article 7 — Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
1. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] shall be designed in a way which does not hinder the removal by authorised treatment facilities of the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the concerned vehicle during the waste phase of the vehicle.

2. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall be designed, as regards joining, fastening and sealing elements, so as to enable, in a readily and non-destructive manner, the removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries and e-drive motors from the vehicle by authorised treatment facilities or repair and maintenance operators during the use phase and waste phase of the vehicle.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend paragraph 2 by revising the list of parts and components that are to be designed for removal and replacement from vehicles, in order to include in that paragraph additional parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, taking into account technical and scientific progress.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts laying down the conditions for the design for removal and replacement of parts and components referred to in paragraph 2 where necessary to ensure harmonised implementation of the obligation set out in paragraph 2.
Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Requires vehicles to be designed to enable non-destructive removal and replacement of specific parts (including EV batteries and e-drive motors) by authorised treatment facilities during use and waste phases.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org's position touches on ELV/WEEE coordination, circular economy, and treatment of electrical vehicles, which is broadly related to the provision's subject of design for disassembly and removal of EV components. However, the org text does not express a specific stance on design-for-removal or design-for-replacement requirements for vehicle parts — its advocacy focuses on EPR coordination, financing, targets for recycled content, and waste hierarchy terminology. There is a real thematic connection (ELV treatment and circular economy), but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision responds to or contradicts any specific position the org holds.

This may be bad for these companies but it is good for the environment. Eco Design - targets The prime incentive for the circular economy is targets for recycled material in new products. This is particularly interesting for plastics and would create a market demand. Recycled material is more expensive than virgin material and it is important to facilitate an environment that promote secondary materials in new products. The submission covers general points about EPR coordination with WEEE, financing, targets for recycled content, and waste terminology — none of which directly address the subject matter of Article 11. article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory depollution requirements for end-of-life vehicles at authorised treatment facilities, including separate collection of fluids, removal of hazardous substances, battery removal, and documentation of the depollution process.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text does not express a specific advocacy position on depollution requirements for end-of-life vehicles. While it broadly supports enhanced ELV treatment legislation and better coordination with WEEE/WFD frameworks, these are general endorsements without a directional stance on the specific depollution mechanisms, fluid collection requirements, or documentation obligations established in Article 29. The org's comments on terminology, financing, and recycled content targets are unrelated to the concrete depollution procedures this provision establishes.

article_text:
Article 34 — Reuse, recycling and recovery targets
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the calendar year following 36 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation], Member States shall ensure that the following targets are met by the waste management operators:
(a)the reuse and recovery, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 95 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year;
(b)the reuse and recycling, as calculated together, shall be a minimum of 85 %, by average weight per vehicle, excluding batteries, and year.

2. From [OP: please insert a date = the first day of the calendar year following 60 months after the date of entry into force of the Regulation] Member States shall ensure that waste management operators achieve a yearly target for the recycling of plastics of at least 30 % of the total weight of plastics contained in the vehicles delivered to the waste management operators.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory reuse, recovery, and recycling targets (95%, 85%, and 30% for plastics) that Member States must ensure waste management operators meet within specified timeframes.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for targets for recycled material in new products (particularly for plastics) as the "prime incentive for the circular economy," and supports enhanced legislation and enforcement for ELV treatment. The provision directly establishes mandatory recycling and recovery targets, including a specific 30% plastics recycling target, which aligns with the organisation's advocacy for stronger recycling targets — especially for plastics — as a driver of circular economy outcomes.

article_text:
Article 1 — Subject matter
1. This Regulation lays down circularity requirements on vehicle design and production related to reusability, recyclability and recoverability and the use of recycled content, which are to be verified at type-approval of vehicles, and on information and labelling requirements on parts, components and materials in vehicles. It also lays down requirements on extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, as well as on the export of used vehicles from the Union to third countries.
provision_effect: Establishes the subject matter of the Regulation, covering circularity requirements (reusability, recyclability, recoverability, recycled content), type-approval verification, information/labelling, extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, and export of used vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for several elements that this provision establishes as subject matter: recycled content targets in new products (circularity/recycled content requirements), extended producer responsibility (EPR legislation for ELV), and treatment/collection of end-of-life vehicles — all of which are directly named in Article 1. The org's pre-proposal text is specifically directed at the ELV Directive revision and calls for action in the same directions the provision concretely establishes.

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Requires assessment of removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, recycling, or other treatment, with labelling and warranty requirements for parts fit for reuse/remanufacturing/refurbishment, and prohibits reuse of certain listed parts.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation raises specific concerns about the correct use of terminology (reuse vs. preparing for reuse, remanufacturing, repurposing) and argues that vehicles entering an ATF are waste, making reuse/remanufacturing/repurposing technically inapplicable as defined terms. The provision, however, uses exactly the terms the organisation questions (reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment) without addressing the definitional concerns raised, and it does not clarify the waste status boundary the organisation emphasises. While there is a clear subject-matter connection, it is genuinely ambiguous whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the organisation's definitional and terminological advocacy.

article_title: Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
article_text:
Article 33 — Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall take necessary incentives to promote the reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components, whether removed during the use or end-of-life phase of a vehicle.
The incentives referred to in the first subparagraph 1 may include:
(a)a requirement for maintenance and repair operators to offer customers to repair a vehicle with used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components alongside offer to repair the vehicle with new parts and components, provided that such a requirement is formulated not to create excessive costs or administrative burdens for micro- and small enterprises;
(b)the use of economic incentives, including the establishment of a reduced rate of value added tax for used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components.
The Commission shall facilitate the exchange of information and sharing of best practices among Member States on such incentives.

2. The Commission shall monitor the effectiveness of the incentives given by Member States.
provision_effect: Requires Member States to take incentives promoting reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment of vehicle parts and components, and mandates Commission monitoring and information exchange on such incentives.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org's text engages with the same subject matter — reuse, remanufacturing, and incentives for parts removal — but its position is primarily definitional and cautionary: it argues that once a vehicle enters an ATF it is waste, making "reuse" and "remanufacturing" technically inapplicable, and notes that preparing for re-use is market-dependent and limited. While the provision does establish incentives for reuse/remanufacturing (including during the use phase, not just end-of-life), it is genuinely unclear whether the org would view this provision as satisfying, contradicting, or simply ignoring its definitional concerns, making the direction of alignment ambiguous.

organisation: CITA - International Motor Vehicle Inspection Committee
source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6266505 - 14/10/2021 INTERNATIONAL MOTOR VEHICLE INSPECTION COMMITTEE CITA input for the review of the ELV Directive Background CITA is the worldwide not-for-profit association of authorities and authorised companies active in the field of vehicle compliance www.citainsp.org. We are one of the partners of the UNEP project “Safer and Cleaners Used Vehicles for Africa”. Scope Our field of expertise is and reason to give our input for this public consultation is the export of used vehicles out of the EU. Inputs Definition of a usable vehicle: the European Union considers the vehicle roadworthiness certificate (Dir 2014/45/EU) as the proof that a vehicle can be used on the road. It is logical to apply the same criteria for exporting: an acceptable vehicle for the EU is an acceptable vehicle to export. Sovereignty of recipient countries: countries recipient of used vehicles shall definer their acceptance criteria. The role of exporting countries: exporting countries, like the EU Member States shall: • Support recipient countries to set up their vehicle compliance schemes. • Facilitate the control of exported used vehicles, according to the criteria of importing countries. • Provide reliable information about each used exported vehicle – IT communication platform between registration authorities in a model like EUCARIS-EREG. Additional aspects to take into account • The enforcement of importing countries is essential
context_after: Additional aspects to take into account • The enforcement of importing countries is essential. If only the EU controls the fitness of used exported vehicles, recipient countries will keep receiving them from somewhere else. That is happening today with substandard new motorcycles in West Africa. • This document only considers the control of design and fitness of used vehicles leaving the Union. Further considerations, like those related to circular economy, are out of the scope. Brussels, October 13th, 2021 For more information, please contact: Eduard Fernández e.fernandez@citainsp.org Page: 1/2 Consultative Status Category II to the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations CITA aisbl | Rue du Commerce 123 | B-1000 BRUSSELS (Belgium) TEL. + 32 (0)2 469 06 70 | secretariat@citainsp.org | www.citainsp.org VAT: BE 0436.921.157
article_title: Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
article_text:
Article 38 — Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] used vehicles to be exported shall be subject to the controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. This Section is without prejudice to any other provisions of this Regulation as well as to other Union legal acts governing the release for export of goods, in particular Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and its Articles 46, 47, 267 and 269.

3. Used vehicles may be exported only if they are:
(a)not end-of-life vehicles based on the criteria listed in Annex I;
(b)considered roadworthy in the Member State where the vehicles were last registered, in accordance with Article 5(1), points (a) and (b), and Article 8 of Directive 2014/45/EU.

4. The following information shall be provided or made available to customs authorities for each used vehicle to be exported:
(a)the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the used vehicle and the identification of the Member State where the vehicle was last registered;
(b)a statement confirming that the used vehicle fulfills the requirements set out in paragraph.

5. In order to verify the compliance with this Section on allowing a used vehicle to be released for export:
(a)until the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 44, and, where necessary, shall take into account such exchange of information and cooperation in order to allow a used vehicle to be released for export;
(b)once the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, Articles 39, 40(2) and (3) and 42(3) shall apply, and notifications and other exchanges under Articles 41 to 43 shall take place by means of those electronic systems.

6. A used vehicle to be exported shall not:
(a)be placed under a customs procedure based on a simplified declaration pursuant to Article 166 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)be subject to an entry in the declarant’s records pursuant to Article 182 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)be subject to self-assessment pursuant to Article 185 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts, in accordance with Article 50 of this Regulation, to amend the criteria listed in Annex I determining whether a used vehicle is an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory controls and requirements for the export of used vehicles, including roadworthiness certification under Directive 2014/45/EU, VIN-based information sharing with customs authorities, and prohibition on end-of-life vehicle exports.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates that the EU roadworthiness certificate (Dir 2014/45/EU) should be the criterion for export eligibility — exactly what Article 38(3)(b) requires. CITA also calls for reliable IT-based information sharing about exported vehicles (referencing EUCARIS-EREG), which is directly reflected in Article 38(4)-(5)'s requirements for VIN-based information provision and electronic system interconnection for customs verification.

article_text:
Article 45 — Electronic systems
1. The MOVE-HUB electronic system developed by the Commission shall be used for exchanging Vehicle Identification Number and information on the vehicle registration and roadworthiness status between national vehicle registers and electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States, as well as, to interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, where necessary for controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. The MOVE-HUB electronic system, referred to in paragraph 1 shall provide at least the following functionalities:
(a)exchange the data in real-time with the national vehicle registers, national electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States interconnected with it;
(b)enable an automated electronic check of data provided in a roadworthiness certificate as referred to in Annex II of Directive 2014/45/EU of the date of first registration of a vehicle, as well as of the Member State where a vehicle was last registered, as referred to in Directive 1999/37/EC, to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with the requirements set out in Article 38, Article 39(1) and Article 40;
(c)interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2399 for the purpose of exchanging data and support the process of exchange of information referred to in Articles 39(1) and Article 40(2), as well as support the notifications referred to in Articles 41 to 43;
(d)for the purpose of cooperation with third countries under Article 44(2), allow electronic exchange of information with the competent authorities of third countries which have notified to the Commission pursuant to Article 40(2) the specific conditions for the import of used vehicles that they apply.

3. Member States shall interconnect their national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness with the MOVE-HUB electronic system referred to in paragraph 1. That interconnection shall be operational within 2 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

4. The Commission shall interconnect the MOVE-HUB system referred to in paragraph 1 to the EU Customs Single Window Certificate Exchange System established in Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2399, so that the automated controls referred to in Article 39 and Article 40(2) and the notifications referred to in Articles 41, 42 and 43 can be performed. That interconnection shall be operational within 4 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

5. The Commission shall adopt the implementing acts laying down the necessary arrangements for the implementation of the functionalities of the MOVE-HUB referred to in paragraph 2, including the technical aspects necessary for the interconnection of national electronic systems to the MOVE-HUB, the conditions of connection to MOVE-HUB, the data to be transmitted by the national systems and the format for the transmission of that data through the interconnected national systems.
The implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Establishes the MOVE-HUB electronic system for real-time exchange of vehicle registration and roadworthiness data between Member States and with customs/third countries to support controls on used vehicle exports.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates for an "IT communication platform between registration authorities in a model like EUCARIS-EREG" to provide reliable information about exported used vehicles and facilitate control of exported used vehicles. The provision directly delivers this by establishing the MOVE-HUB electronic system that interconnects national vehicle registers and roadworthiness systems in real-time, precisely matching CITA's specific ask for a cross-border IT platform for vehicle data exchange in the context of used vehicle exports.

article_text:
Article 41 — Suspension
1. Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a used vehicle to be exported may not comply with the requirements of this Section, the customs authorities shall suspend the release for export of that used vehicle. They shall also immediately notify the competent authorities of the suspension and transmit all relevant information needed to determine whether the used vehicle complies with the requirements of this Regulation and may be released for export.

2. For the purpose of determining whether a used vehicle, subject to suspension as referred to in paragraph 1, complies with this Regulation, the competent authorities may request, from any person involved in the export of that used vehicle, additional information, including information on the sale or transfer of vehicle ownership, such as a copy of the invoice or contract, and documentary evidence that that used vehicle is destined for further use.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for customs authorities to suspend the release for export of used vehicles suspected of non-compliance, and allows competent authorities to request additional information to verify compliance before export.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates that exporting countries (EU Member States) should "facilitate the control of exported used vehicles" and "provide reliable information about each used exported vehicle." The provision directly operationalises this by creating a suspension and verification mechanism at export, enabling competent authorities to check compliance and gather documentary evidence — concrete action in the direction CITA advocated for.

article_title: Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
article_text:
Article 39 — Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
1. Before releasing used vehicles for export, customs shall verify electronically and automatically via the electronic systems referred to in Article 45, that based on the Vehicle Identification Number and the information on the Member State of last registration, the vehicle is considered roadworthy in accordance with Article 38(3), point (b).

2. Where the information provided or made available to customs does not correspond to the information in the national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness pursuant to paragraph 1, customs authorities shall not release that vehicle for export and shall inform the economic operator concerned thereof through these systems.
provision_effect: Requires customs to electronically and automatically verify, via national vehicle registers and roadworthiness systems, that used vehicles are roadworthy before releasing them for export, and to block export and notify the operator if the information does not correspond.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates that the EU should use roadworthiness certificates (Dir 2014/45/EU) as the export eligibility criterion and that Member States should provide reliable vehicle information through an IT communication platform (like EUCARIS-EREG) to facilitate control of exported used vehicles. Article 39 directly delivers both of these asks: it mandates automated electronic verification of roadworthiness status via national registers before export release, which is precisely the mechanism CITA called for.

article_title: Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
article_text:
Article 44 — Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
1. Member States shall mutually assist one another in the implementation of this Section through exchange of information at bilateral level, in particular for the purpose of verifying the status of a vehicle, including the verification of its registration status in the Member State in which it was previously registered.

2. Where appropriate, competent authorities of Member States shall also cooperate with administrative authorities from third countries. Such cooperation may include sharing of relevant information, conducting joint inspections, and other forms of mutual assistance as deemed necessary to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing the export of used vehicles.

3. Customs authorities and competent authorities of Member States shall cooperate in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and exchange information necessary for the fulfilment of their functions under this Regulation, including via electronic means. The customs authorities may communicate, in accordance with Article 12(1) and Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, confidential information acquired by the customs authorities in the course of performing their duties, or provided to the customs authorities on a confidential basis, to the competent authority of the Member State where the operator or trader is established.

4. Where the competent authorities have received information in accordance with paragraphs 1 to 3 of this Article, those competent authorities may communicate that information to competent authorities from other Member States.

5. Risk-related information shall be exchanged as follows:
(a)between customs authorities in accordance with Article 46(5) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)between customs authorities and the Commission in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)between customs authorities and competent authorities, including competent authorities from other Member States, in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework for cooperation and information exchange between Member States' competent authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities regarding the verification and export of used vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates for exporting countries (EU Member States) to "provide reliable information about each used exported vehicle" via an "IT communication platform between registration authorities" and to "facilitate the control of exported used vehicles." Article 44 directly responds to this by establishing mandatory cooperation and information exchange mechanisms between Member States' competent authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities — including verification of vehicle registration status and sharing of relevant information for export compliance — which aligns with CITA's specific ask for cross-border information sharing infrastructure.

article_title: Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
article_text:
Article 38 — Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] used vehicles to be exported shall be subject to the controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. This Section is without prejudice to any other provisions of this Regulation as well as to other Union legal acts governing the release for export of goods, in particular Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and its Articles 46, 47, 267 and 269.

3. Used vehicles may be exported only if they are:
(a)not end-of-life vehicles based on the criteria listed in Annex I;
(b)considered roadworthy in the Member State where the vehicles were last registered, in accordance with Article 5(1), points (a) and (b), and Article 8 of Directive 2014/45/EU.

4. The following information shall be provided or made available to customs authorities for each used vehicle to be exported:
(a)the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the used vehicle and the identification of the Member State where the vehicle was last registered;
(b)a statement confirming that the used vehicle fulfills the requirements set out in paragraph.

5. In order to verify the compliance with this Section on allowing a used vehicle to be released for export:
(a)until the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 44, and, where necessary, shall take into account such exchange of information and cooperation in order to allow a used vehicle to be released for export;
(b)once the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, Articles 39, 40(2) and (3) and 42(3) shall apply, and notifications and other exchanges under Articles 41 to 43 shall take place by means of those electronic systems.

6. A used vehicle to be exported shall not:
(a)be placed under a customs procedure based on a simplified declaration pursuant to Article 166 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)be subject to an entry in the declarant’s records pursuant to Article 182 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)be subject to self-assessment pursuant to Article 185 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts, in accordance with Article 50 of this Regulation, to amend the criteria listed in Annex I determining whether a used vehicle is an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory controls and roadworthiness requirements for used vehicles before they can be exported from the EU, including verification against end-of-life vehicle criteria and compliance with roadworthiness standards.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates for controlling the fitness of used vehicles leaving the EU, which is precisely what Article 38 establishes by requiring exported used vehicles to be roadworthy and not end-of-life. The provision directly delivers the mechanism CITA called for — EU-side controls on the design and fitness of used vehicles at export — matching CITA's stated scope of concern.

article_text:
Article 40 — Risk management and customs controls
1. For the purpose of enforcing the provisions laid down in Article 38, customs authorities shall carry out controls on used vehicles to be exported in accordance with Articles 46 and 47 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013. Without prejudice to Article 39, such controls shall primarily be based on risk analysis, as established in Article 46(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

2. In addition to the risk management referred to in paragraph 1, once the interconnection referred to in Article 45 is operational, customs shall use these electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1) to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with specific conditions linked to the protection of the environment or road safety in accordance with paragraph 3 of this Article.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to supplement this Regulation by setting out the compliance conditions referred to in paragraph 2, including specific conditions applied to the import of used vehicles by the third country of import linked to the protection of the environment and road safety, when such conditions have been notified by that third country to the Commission. Those conditions shall be verifiable against the information available in the electronic systems referred to in Article 45(1).
provision_effect: Establishes a risk-management and customs control framework for used vehicles to be exported, including the use of electronic systems to verify compliance with environmental and road safety conditions set by importing third countries.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: CITA explicitly advocates that the enforcement of importing countries is essential and that controls on used exported vehicles must account for the conditions of recipient countries. Article 40(3) directly responds to this by empowering the Commission to set compliance conditions linked to the protection of the environment and road safety that are specifically tied to conditions notified by the third country of import — institutionalising the role of importing countries in the enforcement framework, which is precisely what CITA called for.

article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes conditions under which a suspended used vehicle export must be released, clarifies that release does not constitute proof of compliance with Union law, and requires customs authorities to notify the competent authority of the Member State of registration after each release for export.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org's position focuses on the importance of enforcement by importing countries and notes that EU-only controls are insufficient, without taking any specific directional stance on the procedural mechanics of releasing suspended vehicles for export. The org text does not advocate for or against the specific conditions, timelines, or notification requirements established in Article 42, making it impossible to assess alignment or opposition.

A vehicle which is in a state to become waste may contain a functionally complete component (an engine, a gearbox etc) which has significant value as a replacement in a historic, or potentially historic, vehicle. These components must be allowed to be saved for use in repair/restoration of historic vehicles. This possibility is necessary and is consistent with the words of : “… either ready for use or stripped into parts…” Vehicle registration In some countries it is possible for a vehicle to be de-registered if it is not being used for a period of time. In those countries, historic vehicles are often de-registered during a period of restoration or just because the vehicle is not planned to be used for a while; equally many vehicles in museums are not registered and some vehicles are active but used off-road and so may not be registered. Linking ELV status to registration would require historic vehicles in this situation to be classified as ELV and so have to be destroyed. This would clearly be wrong. Proposals to address the above concerns Safeguards are needed to realise the intent of of the ELV Directive. I.e, the system must: - Provide for the presumption that only an owner can determine whether a vehicle is “end-of- life” to avoid owners from being prevented from restoring their vehicles
context_before: clearly the intention to restore the vehicle, evidenced also by the fact that the owners (the importers) had spent money buying and transporting the vehicles. Such situations are perverse given that the intended actions of the owners would: 1. Avoid ending the life of a vehicle requiring environmentally costly recycling /treatment of the waste 2. Ensure the reusability of a vehicle 3. Generate work (usually for SMEs) - the restoration of a historic vehicle is a significant economic activity 4. Use the restored vehicle sparingly and safely, and maintain it to a high standard - the time and money is invested because of a passion for the vehicle hence there is an inherent desire to look after it. Dismantling/restoration of a vehicle Often a vehicle is dismantled, even almost completely, for the purpose of renovation. This process needs to be recognised as legitimate – i.e. the vehicle is not an ELV just because it is being dismantled for the purpose of renovation. Also, the economic cost of renovation is commonly much greater than the value of the vehicle - the value of the vehicle itself is realised once the repair/renovation is complete. This needs to be recognised particularly if a vehicle is being sold to an owner in a third country where the restoration is due to take place

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, and specifies that parts fit for reuse/remanufacturing/refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: F.I.V.A. explicitly advocates that functionally complete components (engines, gearboxes, etc.) from vehicles that may become waste must be allowed to be saved and reused in the repair/restoration of historic vehicles. Article 31 directly responds to this by establishing that removed parts assessed as fit for reuse, remanufacturing, or refurbishment shall not be considered waste, which is precisely the outcome F.I.V.A. sought — enabling valuable components to be preserved and reused rather than treated as waste.

article_text:
Article 41 — Suspension
1. Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a used vehicle to be exported may not comply with the requirements of this Section, the customs authorities shall suspend the release for export of that used vehicle. They shall also immediately notify the competent authorities of the suspension and transmit all relevant information needed to determine whether the used vehicle complies with the requirements of this Regulation and may be released for export.

2. For the purpose of determining whether a used vehicle, subject to suspension as referred to in paragraph 1, complies with this Regulation, the competent authorities may request, from any person involved in the export of that used vehicle, additional information, including information on the sale or transfer of vehicle ownership, such as a copy of the invoice or contract, and documentary evidence that that used vehicle is destined for further use.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing customs authorities to suspend the export of used vehicles suspected of non-compliance, and empowers competent authorities to request additional information — including proof of further use — to determine whether the vehicle may be released for export.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: F.I.V.A. advocates for safeguards ensuring that historic vehicles and their components can be exported for restoration or repair (e.g., to a third-country owner), and that only the owner should determine whether a vehicle is end-of-life. The suspension mechanism in Article 41 could either support or hinder this position: on one hand, requiring "documentary evidence that the used vehicle is destined for further use" could protect legitimate historic vehicle exports by confirming restoration intent; on the other hand, the suspension power could be used to block exports of vehicles that appear to be waste but are actually intended for historic vehicle restoration — precisely the scenario F.I.V.A. is concerned about. The direction of the provision's effect on F.I.V.A.'s specific advocacy is genuinely ambiguous from the text alone.

context_after: Such a measure has the potential to impact upon the legitimate extra-EU sale of historic vehicles. Clearly this must be avoided. The definition of a historic vehicle as provided by the Roadworthiness Testing Directive could be helpful in this context as it makes clear the distinction between a vehicle which is just “old” and a historic vehicle. However, as noted above there are some circumstances where a vehicle is being sold and exported because the purchaser intends to restore the vehicle. In these circumstances, the type of vehicle, the nature of the sale and the intent of the purchaser will provide ample information for it to be clear that the export is taking place to preserve a part of motoring heritage rather than being the sale of an old vehicle which is inappropriate for further use in a third country. If measures are implemented in the Directive to ban the sale of “old” vehicles to third countries, the Directive should also make provision for the legitimate sale and export of historic vehicles or of vehicles due for restoration by the new owner. Scrappage schemes A further concern is the possibility that motoring heritage can be lost as a consequence of an owner taking advantage of a scrappage scheme in return for a discount on a new car. For example, in 2017 a 1959 Standard Ten was offered to a Ford dealer in the UK under a voluntary dealer-led scrappage scheme

label: OPPOSING
reasoning: F.I.V.A. explicitly states that export regulations for old vehicles to third countries "has the potential to impact upon the legitimate extra-EU sale of historic vehicles" and that "this must be avoided." The provision creates exactly such a regulatory mechanism — a suspension and scrutiny regime for used vehicle exports — which is the type of measure F.I.V.A. argues should not be applied in a way that affects historic vehicles, effectively opposing its establishment.

article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes the conditions under which a suspended used vehicle export must be released, requires customs authorities to notify competent authorities after each release, and clarifies that release for export does not constitute proof of compliance with Union law.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: F.I.V.A. explicitly advocates that any regulation of old vehicle exports to third countries must not impact the legitimate extra-EU sale of historic vehicles. Article 42 establishes a procedural framework for releasing suspended used vehicle exports, which is directly related to the export control mechanism F.I.V.A. is concerned about. However, it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies or contradicts F.I.V.A.'s concern — the article creates a release mechanism with a 4-working-day window and notification requirements, but it is ambiguous whether this framework adequately protects legitimate historic vehicle exports or whether F.I.V.A. would view it as an acceptable safeguard or an unwelcome bureaucratic hurdle.
