article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes the conditions under which a suspended used vehicle export must be released, clarifies that release does not constitute proof of compliance with Union law, and requires customs authorities to notify competent authorities after each release for export.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The organisation advocates for harmonised rules on de-registration and flags significant export flows of ELVs/used cars as a key issue to address, but does not take a specific directional stance on the procedural mechanics of releasing vehicles for export or the notification requirements that this provision establishes. There is a genuine thematic connection (both concern used vehicle exports), but it is unclear whether the provision's specific procedural framework satisfies, contradicts, or simply ignores the organisation's ask for harmonised de-registration rules.

article_title: Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
article_text:
Article 12 — Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
1. Manufacturers and their suppliers shall use the nomenclature of the component and material coding standards listed in points 1 to 3 of Annex VI for the labelling and identification of parts, components and materials of vehicles.

2. Manufacturers shall ensure that e-drive motors containing permanent magnets bear a conspicuous, clearly legible and indelible label indicating the information listed in point 4 of Annex VI.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VI in order to adapt it to technical and scientific progress.
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers and suppliers to label and identify vehicle parts, components, and materials using standardised nomenclature, and mandates specific labelling for e-drive motors containing permanent magnets.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation advocates for enhanced eco-design measures including reducing the number of different polymers in vehicles, which relates to better material identification and standardisation. The provision directly advances this direction by mandating standardised labelling and coding of parts, components, and materials — facilitating identification, sorting, and ultimately recycling of vehicle materials. This constitutes a concrete action in the direction of improved material traceability that the org called for under eco-design enhancements.

source_text: 4) Invest in innovative technologies Optimizing post-shredder technology (PST) which allows to recover plastic materials is needed in order to increase the recycling rates of ASR. So, further development and implementation of Post-Shredding Technologies (PSTs) for material extraction and sorting should be boosted by economic incentives. 5) Improve Information flow The vehicle produced has to assure, at least, the following goals : a. low energy consumption b. easy dismantling c. suitable recycling d. less toxic metals To fulfil these goals, all the involved stakeholders have to cooperate and exchange relevant information. From its side, the producer will provide the dismantling information and the content of Substances of Concern for each new type of new vehicle put on the market. Taking into account that the average lifespan of a car in use is roughly between 12 and 15 years, legacy substances will also be a main issue. An update is needed by the producers as a new substance becomes of concern. As a general rule, less use of SVHC in products will cause less problems and reduce down-cycling when the product becomes waste. Furthermore, we call for a coherence between the ELV Directive and other EU legislations (Waste Framework Directive, Batteries Directive, Directive on Restrictions of certain Hazardous Substances, REACH, EU rules on type- approval and on registration of vehicles…). For more information/clarifications, please contact info@fead.be
context_before: Solutions for a better dismantling and an increase in recycling rates require to: • clarify that car manufacturers bear the responsibility and the costs for the waste stage of a car’s life cycle • provide incentive on proper dismantling and depollution through EPR schemes • provide a list of the available components in ELVs (engines, electric car batteries and catalysts) at the moment of their deregistration from the appropriate registers (according to local laws). • boost the market of secondary raw materials, through mandatory recycled contents in the automotive sector. The demand for recycled material needs to be increased in order to drive value and make the activity economically viable. 1 (The Plastics Industry Trade Association, 2016) 3

source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719484 - 01/11/2021 Preliminary Views on the EU Commission’s “Public Consultation for the impact assessment supporting the review of the Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles and of the Directive 2005/64/EC on the type approval of motor vehicles with regard to their reusability, recyclability and recoverability” Introduction PlasticsEurope welcomes the opportunity to respond to the European Commission’s public consultation on the end-of-life vehicles directive. Plastics have been a key enabler in the modern construction, performance, safety, efficiency and functionality of cars. Single mould components have helped manufacturers to decrease vehicle assembly time, quickly introduce design innovations and trim costs. Plastics help to make cars lighter, thus reducing fuel demand and greenhouse gas emissions. Reducing a modern car's weight by 100 kg cuts fuel consumption by approximately 0.2 litres per 100 km and reduces CO2 emissions by around 10 g/km1. This paper summarises the views of the European plastic raw material producers about key aspects of the European Commission’s consultation2 (running to October 26th, 2021). In this consultation, the Commission has raised several points around ELV treatment such as the use of plastics, circularity and recycled content in vehicles, EPR, hazardous substances, and others
context_after: In this consultation, the Commission has raised several points around ELV treatment such as the use of plastics, circularity and recycled content in vehicles, EPR, hazardous substances, and others. In the context of the ELV directive, plastics contribute to vehicle’s resource conservation while it is necessary to treat the mix of materials at end-of-life in the most optimal way. Recycled plastics content in vehicles In regard to a potential target for recycled plastics content in vehicles, PlasticsEurope conditionally supports ambitious yet realistic binding legislative targets for recycling of material streams from ELV and for recycled content in automotive under given enabling conditions. These should be developed and agreed in cooperation with the whole value-chain. Aligning the value chain is key in supporting any target, and targets should not compromise the safety, effectiveness, or performance of vehicles. The material requirements and specificities (mechanical, substance requirements, durability, recyclability etc) are critical for this sector and must be acknowledged to ensure that targets are both achievable and beneficial for the environment and society. Recycling technologies and material sourcing The manufacture of a recyclates demands high material specifications and qualities due to product requirements for use in automotive. Therefore, appropriate and qualified sources of secondary material streams are key

article_text:
Article 1 — Subject matter
1. This Regulation lays down circularity requirements on vehicle design and production related to reusability, recyclability and recoverability and the use of recycled content, which are to be verified at type-approval of vehicles, and on information and labelling requirements on parts, components and materials in vehicles. It also lays down requirements on extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, as well as on the export of used vehicles from the Union to third countries.
provision_effect: Establishes the scope of the regulation, covering circularity requirements (reusability, recyclability, recoverability, recycled content), type-approval verification, information/labelling, extended producer responsibility, collection and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, and export of used vehicles.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: PlasticsEurope explicitly advocates for binding legislative targets for recycled plastics content in vehicles and for recycling of material streams from ELVs, as well as addressing EPR and end-of-life treatment — all of which are concrete elements established in this regulation's scope. The provision directly instantiates the regulatory framework (recycled content requirements, EPR, ELV treatment) that PlasticsEurope conditionally supports, making this a clear path (b) alignment.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719470 - 01/11/2021 Tuesday, October 26, 2021 LyondellBasell response to public consultation for the impact assessment supporting the review of the Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles and of the Directive 2005/64/EC on the type-approval of motor vehicles with regard to their reusability, recyclability and recoverability LyondellBasell aspires to be an industry leader in the circular economy. By 2030, our ambition is to produce and market two million metric tons of recycled and renewable-based polymers annually. LyondellBasell supports mandates for recycled content in order to further develop end markets for post-use plastics, driving their value and circularity. We believe that the following targets in vehicles are ambitious yet technically feasible: 5% by 2025 15% by 2030 2040 and 2050 targets could be significantly higher depending on feedstock availability The introduction of such targets needs to be linked to enabling conditions: • Consistent and reliable feedstock supply in sufficient volumes and quality. • Pre-consumer and post-consumer material should both qualify as recycled content in accordance with the ISO 14021 standard. • Both mechanical and chemical (molecular) recycling recognized as eligible sources of recycled content. • Mass balance is used to attribute chemically recycled content to end products at the highest aggregated level, not the article level
context_after: • Mass balance is used to attribute chemically recycled content to end products at the highest aggregated level, not the article level. • There should be free attribution of all chemical recycling products including by-products but excluding process losses. • The mass balance system allocates sustainability benefits to products without physical separation, consistent with the International Sustainability and Carbon Certification (ISCC) system for biomass and bioenergy. • Certification standards and methodology must be transparent and preferably harmonized with broadly used global certification standards such as ISCC+. • All health, safety and performance requirements for each application must be met without exception. • Recycled content targets should be ambitious yet technically feasible and consistent with overall policy objectives • Value chain collaboration is crucial to fully leverage recycling mandates to achieve circularity goals.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory minimum of 25% recycled plastic content (from post-consumer waste) in vehicle types, with at least 25% of that from end-of-life vehicles, and empowers the Commission to set further recycled content requirements for steel, aluminium, and other materials.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: LyondellBasell explicitly supports mandates for recycled content in vehicles and proposes specific percentage targets (5% by 2025, 15% by 2030, with higher targets later), which directly aligns with the provision establishing a mandatory 25% recycled plastic content requirement. Both the org and the provision move in the same direction — mandatory recycled content targets for vehicles — though the provision's 25% target is more ambitious than LyondellBasell's proposed 15% by 2030 trajectory.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719464 - 01/11/2021 MOL Group’s position on the review of the End-of-life Vehicles Directive MOL Group is committed to the EU circular economy objectives and is transforming to adapt to a circular and low- carbon world. Being amongst the top ten polymer producers in Europe and the biggest in Central Europe, we believe that plastics deliver significant benefits to the economy. By reducing weight, for example in automotive applications, it also contributes to reducing carbon emissions. On the other hand, the benefits can only be maintained if the challenges with resource efficiency and plastic waste are solved by moving to a circular model. Transition to a more circular economy requires significant investments, and EU rules on end-of-life vehicles can ensure regulatory certainty and increased incentives for investments in innovative solutions. MOL Group welcomes the objective of the European Commission to ensure that end-of-life vehicles (ELVs) are managed sustainably and to encourage the car industry to adopt a sustainable model for the design and production of cars. Below we expanded our points on recycled content targets that we believe will be crucial to achieve these objectives
context_after: Below we expanded our points on recycled content targets that we believe will be crucial to achieve these objectives. Recycling of pre-consumer plastic waste can increase the circularity of the automotive sector already today, incorporation of post-consumer plastics still requires research & development and availability of good quality feedstock We welcome the Commission’s intention to include in the ELV Directive a recycled plastics content target for new vehicles. While we agree that there are challenges in the wide adoption of post-consumer recyclates in vehicle manufacturing, and that regulatory action could help increase activities in this area, we believe that the targets should be set for both post-consumer and pre-consumer recyclates, as both areas need significant improvements to increase the replacement of virgin raw materials and lead to greater circularity in the automotive sector. Nowadays, dismantling ELV plastics for recycling appears to be a limited practice yet (e.g. ELV car bumpers). The incorporation of post-consumer recyclates still needs significant research and development activities, considering that materials in vehicles are required to fulfil various technical specifications. Since automotive parts and vehicles are long-lasting products (ca. 15 years), the use of recycled plastics from automotive post-consumer waste is hindered by evolution in specification and substances management over time
article_text:
Article 9 — Circularity strategy
1. For each vehicle type that is type-approved under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], the manufacturer shall draw up a circularity strategy.

2. The circularity strategy shall describe which actions the manufacturers will take to follow-up on their obligations to ensure that the circularity requirements in Chapter II, which are verified in the type-approval procedures and which are applicable to the vehicle type concerned, are met.

3. The circularity strategy shall contain the elements laid down in Part A of Annex IV.

4. The manufacturer shall provide a copy of the circularity strategy to the Commission within 30 days after the type-approval for the concerned vehicle type has been granted.

5. The manufacturer shall monitor and follow up on the actions contained in the circularity strategy and update the strategy every five years in accordance with Part B of Annex IV. The updated circularity strategy shall be provided to the type-approval authority that issued the type-approval for the vehicle type and to the Commisssion.

6. The Commission shall make the circularity strategies and any updates to those strategies publicly available, except for confidential information.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Part B of Annex IV by adapting the requirements on the content of the circularity strategy and the updates to that strategy to technical and scientific progress in vehicle manufacturing and management of end-of-life vehicles, to market developments in the automotive sector and to regulatory changes.

8. source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719462 - 01/11/2021 Revision of the End-Of-Life Vehicles Directive European Copper Institute submission to support our response to the Commission consultation (October 2021) Missing vehicles and Illegal export of ELVs A huge amount of critical raw materials are needed to achieve the EU Green Deal goal of decarbonising road transport. The Commission has proposed that all new car sales should be 100% from Zero Emission Vehicles by 2035, a dramatic increase from 5% in 20201. Producing enough battery cells to meet the expected rise in demand for battery electric vehicles will require significant amounts of critical raw materials from mining and recycling. To meet this demand, and in line with the EU’s Circular Economy agenda, it is therefore important to facilitate the recovery and recycling of these raw materials from end-of-life vehicles (ELV). To this end, it is important to improve the ELVD provisions aiming to reduce the number of ELVs at unknown whereabouts. Problem: In 2014, 4.66 million ELVs in the EU, representing 39% of the total vehicles being decommissioned, were at ‘unknown whereabouts’2. This share has remained at a constant level since 20073. The high number of ELVs at ‘unknown whereabouts’ is for a large part due to vehicle dismantling at illegal sites and exports of ELVs outside of Europe as used cars4
context_after: The high number of ELVs at ‘unknown whereabouts’ is for a large part due to vehicle dismantling at illegal sites and exports of ELVs outside of Europe as used cars4. ECI proposal: We believe that action at EU level is justified to reduce the number of ELVs at ‘unknown whereabouts’, given that national measures alone have not been effective to date. To effectively reduce the number of vehicles at ‘unknown whereabouts’, we suggest adding the following provisions in the revised ELVD. These proposals are to a large extent based on the existing legal frameworks in the Netherlands, Italy and Austria, which we believe provide a good, workable basis for improving the ELVD: 1. Registration of any road transport vehicle when the owner is a resident (or registered company) in the given Member State. From that moment the owner will be bound by at least two payment obligations (insurance and roadworthiness test) which will work as incentives to dispose of the vehicle in an authorised treatment facility (ATF) when it reaches its end of life. 2. Provide two exemptions from the obligation to pay insurance and roadworthiness test: i. For vehicles with suspended registrations, for a maximum of three years to be renewed every year. While suspended, the vehicle is no longer authorised to be in use

article_text:
Article 45 — Electronic systems
1. The MOVE-HUB electronic system developed by the Commission shall be used for exchanging Vehicle Identification Number and information on the vehicle registration and roadworthiness status between national vehicle registers and electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States, as well as, to interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, where necessary for controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. The MOVE-HUB electronic system, referred to in paragraph 1 shall provide at least the following functionalities:
(a)exchange the data in real-time with the national vehicle registers, national electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States interconnected with it;
(b)enable an automated electronic check of data provided in a roadworthiness certificate as referred to in Annex II of Directive 2014/45/EU of the date of first registration of a vehicle, as well as of the Member State where a vehicle was last registered, as referred to in Directive 1999/37/EC, to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with the requirements set out in Article 38, Article 39(1) and Article 40;
(c)interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2399 for the purpose of exchanging data and support the process of exchange of information referred to in Articles 39(1) and Article 40(2), as well as support the notifications referred to in Articles 41 to 43;
(d)for the purpose of cooperation with third countries under Article 44(2), allow electronic exchange of information with the competent authorities of third countries which have notified to the Commission pursuant to Article 40(2) the specific conditions for the import of used vehicles that they apply.

3. Member States shall interconnect their national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness with the MOVE-HUB electronic system referred to in paragraph 1. That interconnection shall be operational within 2 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

4. The Commission shall interconnect the MOVE-HUB system referred to in paragraph 1 to the EU Customs Single Window Certificate Exchange System established in Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2399, so that the automated controls referred to in Article 39 and Article 40(2) and the notifications referred to in Articles 41, 42 and 43 can be performed. That interconnection shall be operational within 4 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

5. The Commission shall adopt the implementing acts laying down the necessary arrangements for the implementation of the functionalities of the MOVE-HUB referred to in paragraph 2, including the technical aspects necessary for the interconnection of national electronic systems to the MOVE-HUB, the conditions of connection to MOVE-HUB, the data to be transmitted by the national systems and the format for the transmission of that data through the interconnected national systems.
The implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Establishes the MOVE-HUB electronic system for exchanging vehicle identification, registration, and roadworthiness data between Member States and customs systems to support controls on used vehicle exports.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The European Copper Institute explicitly advocates for EU-level action to reduce ELVs at 'unknown whereabouts,' largely caused by illegal exports of ELVs as used cars, and calls for provisions to track and control vehicles through registration and roadworthiness frameworks. The MOVE-HUB provision directly takes concrete action in that direction by creating an electronic system that cross-checks vehicle registration and roadworthiness data to determine whether used vehicles being exported comply with relevant requirements — a mechanism that directly addresses the export loophole the ECI identified as a root cause of the problem.

article_title: Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
article_text:
Article 39 — Automated verification of the information on vehicle status
1. Before releasing used vehicles for export, customs shall verify electronically and automatically via the electronic systems referred to in Article 45, that based on the Vehicle Identification Number and the information on the Member State of last registration, the vehicle is considered roadworthy in accordance with Article 38(3), point (b).

2. Where the information provided or made available to customs does not correspond to the information in the national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness pursuant to paragraph 1, customs authorities shall not release that vehicle for export and shall inform the economic operator concerned thereof through these systems.
provision_effect: Requires customs to electronically verify vehicle roadworthiness status via Vehicle Identification Number before releasing used vehicles for export, and to block export if the information does not match national vehicle registers.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The ECI explicitly advocates for EU-level action to reduce ELVs at 'unknown whereabouts' caused by illegal exports of ELVs as used cars, and proposes provisions including roadworthiness test obligations tied to registration. The provision directly addresses this concern by requiring automated customs verification of roadworthiness status before export, blocking vehicles that fail the check — a concrete mechanism to prevent ELVs from being exported as used cars, which is precisely the problem ECI sought to address.

article_text:
Article 41 — Suspension
1. Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a used vehicle to be exported may not comply with the requirements of this Section, the customs authorities shall suspend the release for export of that used vehicle. They shall also immediately notify the competent authorities of the suspension and transmit all relevant information needed to determine whether the used vehicle complies with the requirements of this Regulation and may be released for export.

2. For the purpose of determining whether a used vehicle, subject to suspension as referred to in paragraph 1, complies with this Regulation, the competent authorities may request, from any person involved in the export of that used vehicle, additional information, including information on the sale or transfer of vehicle ownership, such as a copy of the invoice or contract, and documentary evidence that that used vehicle is destined for further use.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for customs authorities to suspend the release for export of used vehicles suspected of non-compliance, and allows competent authorities to request additional information to verify compliance before export.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The European Copper Institute explicitly advocates for EU-level action to reduce ELVs at 'unknown whereabouts' caused by illegal exports of ELVs as used cars, and proposes specific provisions to address this. Article 41 directly responds to this concern by creating a concrete enforcement mechanism — suspension of export release and documentary verification — that targets exactly the problem of ELVs being exported outside Europe under the guise of used vehicles, aligning with the org's call for effective EU-level measures to curb such exports.

article_text:
Article 42 — Release for export
1. Where the release for export of a used vehicle has been suspended in accordance with Article 41, that used vehicle shall be released for export where all the other requirements and formalities relating to such release have been fulfilled and where any of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or
(b)the competent authorities have informed the customs authorities of their approval for release for export pursuant to this Section.

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

3. After each release for export of a used vehicle, customs authorities shall notify that release for export to the competent authority of the Member State where the vehicle in question was registered at the time of export.
provision_effect: Establishes conditions under which a used vehicle suspended from export must be released for export, and requires customs authorities to notify competent authorities after each such release.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's position concerns suspended vehicle registrations and conditions for vehicles being authorised for use, not the export release process for used vehicles. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the export suspension and release mechanism established by this provision.

Recycled content targets ECI supports the aim to reduce landfill and incentivize more recycling to make sure that losses of high value recycled copper are avoided. Taking the example of vehicles’ wiring harnesses, the major recycling value lies within copper conductors in cables. Ensuring that ease of dismantling is put at the forefront of the full life cycle considerations at the design stage of a vehicle is of paramount importance. This will allow dismantling companies to immediately detect those high-value parts of the vehicle and recover high-grade copper, instead of dismantling other wire parts with less value that are used in many different sub-quantities, thus making it more difficult and time-consuming to assess whether they can be recycled for the same electrical application as used for before. Besides wires, this rationale can be extended to all vehicle parts where most value of materials needs to be maintained at End of Life (EoL). article_title: Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
article_text:
Article 38 — Controls and requirements on the export of used vehicles
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] used vehicles to be exported shall be subject to the controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. This Section is without prejudice to any other provisions of this Regulation as well as to other Union legal acts governing the release for export of goods, in particular Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and its Articles 46, 47, 267 and 269.

3. Used vehicles may be exported only if they are:
(a)not end-of-life vehicles based on the criteria listed in Annex I;
(b)considered roadworthy in the Member State where the vehicles were last registered, in accordance with Article 5(1), points (a) and (b), and Article 8 of Directive 2014/45/EU.

4. The following information shall be provided or made available to customs authorities for each used vehicle to be exported:
(a)the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the used vehicle and the identification of the Member State where the vehicle was last registered;
(b)a statement confirming that the used vehicle fulfills the requirements set out in paragraph.

5. In order to verify the compliance with this Section on allowing a used vehicle to be released for export:
(a)until the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 44, and, where necessary, shall take into account such exchange of information and cooperation in order to allow a used vehicle to be released for export;
(b)once the interconnection referred to in Article 45(4) is operational, Articles 39, 40(2) and (3) and 42(3) shall apply, and notifications and other exchanges under Articles 41 to 43 shall take place by means of those electronic systems.

6. A used vehicle to be exported shall not:
(a)be placed under a customs procedure based on a simplified declaration pursuant to Article 166 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)be subject to an entry in the declarant’s records pursuant to Article 182 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)be subject to self-assessment pursuant to Article 185 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

7. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts, in accordance with Article 50 of this Regulation, to amend the criteria listed in Annex I determining whether a used vehicle is an end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory controls and requirements for the export of used vehicles, including that they must not be end-of-life vehicles and must be considered roadworthy in the Member State where last registered, with specific customs and information obligations.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocated for requiring a valid roadworthiness certificate to allow a vehicle to be exported outside the EU as a used car (point 5), and the provision directly delivers this by requiring used vehicles to be considered roadworthy (Article 38(3)(b)) before export. Additionally, the org's point 4(iii) called for presenting a customs declaration for export as a condition for deregistration, which aligns with the provision's customs authority controls and information requirements.

article_title: Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
article_text:
Article 44 — Cooperation among authorities and exchange of information
1. Member States shall mutually assist one another in the implementation of this Section through exchange of information at bilateral level, in particular for the purpose of verifying the status of a vehicle, including the verification of its registration status in the Member State in which it was previously registered.

2. Where appropriate, competent authorities of Member States shall also cooperate with administrative authorities from third countries. Such cooperation may include sharing of relevant information, conducting joint inspections, and other forms of mutual assistance as deemed necessary to ensure compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing the export of used vehicles.

3. Customs authorities and competent authorities of Member States shall cooperate in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013 and exchange information necessary for the fulfilment of their functions under this Regulation, including via electronic means. The customs authorities may communicate, in accordance with Article 12(1) and Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, confidential information acquired by the customs authorities in the course of performing their duties, or provided to the customs authorities on a confidential basis, to the competent authority of the Member State where the operator or trader is established.

4. Where the competent authorities have received information in accordance with paragraphs 1 to 3 of this Article, those competent authorities may communicate that information to competent authorities from other Member States.

5. Risk-related information shall be exchanged as follows:
(a)between customs authorities in accordance with Article 46(5) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(b)between customs authorities and the Commission in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013;
(c)between customs authorities and competent authorities, including competent authorities from other Member States, in accordance with Article 47(2) of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.
provision_effect: Establishes a framework for cooperation and exchange of information among Member State authorities, customs authorities, and third-country authorities to verify vehicle status and ensure compliance with laws governing the export of used vehicles.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org advocates for monitoring vehicle exports (e.g., requiring customs declarations for export outside the EU and certificates of registration in a second Member State for intra-EU exports), which is related to the provision's subject of inter-authority cooperation on used vehicle exports. However, the org's position focuses on deregistration conditions and documentation requirements rather than the cooperation mechanisms between authorities, making it genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific asks.

(a)the competent authorities have not requested, within four working days from the beginning of the suspension, the customs authorities to maintain the suspension, or

2. The release for export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation or Regulation (EU) No 952/2013.

label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org specifically advocates for deregistration to be conditional on presenting a customs declaration for export outside the EU (point 4iii), and for requiring a valid roadworthiness certificate for export outside the EU (point 5), indicating a clear interest in the export control framework for used vehicles. However, Article 42 deals with the procedural mechanics of releasing a suspended export (timelines, approval, notification), not with the substantive conditions the org advocates for (roadworthiness certificates, customs declarations as deregistration prerequisites). It is genuinely unclear whether this procedural release mechanism satisfies, contradicts, or is simply orthogonal to the org's specific asks.

source_text: 1) Increase collection of ELVs for proper treatment and reduce ELVs of unknown whereabouts More ELVs need to be collected for proper treatment. There is a need for a reduction and subsequent elimination of non-compliant activities both amongst dismantlers and treatment operators dealing with ELVs. This could be achieved by increasing the volumes of collected ELVs and by ensuring that these ELVs are treated properly by Authorized Treatment Facilities (ATFs) and treatment operators (such as e.g. shredders). 2) Introduce well-framed incentives for last owners of ELVs It is important that the last owner of a vehicle fulfils the responsibility to deliver the ELV to an ATF when it has reached its end of life. The need for better incentives for last owners of ELVs to properly treat those vehicles is explained by the fact that many ELVs are not being delivered in for recycling when required. Instead, they are being kept or dumped by their last owners. Such practices lead to unnecessary costs for Member States and their municipalities which must appropriately deal with these vehicles. To reduce these costs, the introduction of a small fee (or tax) for each registered vehicle should be explored. The fee would be used to pay an incentive to the owner when delivering the ELV for proper treatment and deregistration at an ATF, encouraging them to dispose of their car safely and within the frame of the law
context_before: However, some problems persist, namely: • We need to collect more ELVs for proper treatment. There are too many ELVs not being delivered for recycling, going missing or being illegally treated. • We need a better monitoring and control of the system from the authorities, both related to ATFs/ shredders and other actors exporting and trading ELVs. • The owners of vehicles need to take more responsibility and deliver the ELV to an ATF, and we propose a system providing incentives for the last owner when delivering the ELV to an ATF. • There is a need to ensure safe conditions in the neutralization of the rapidly increasing number of pyrotechnical units, such as airbags, and obviously in the treatment of ELVs containing high voltage batteries at the end of life. Unless all these areas function properly, we cannot have a level playing field. The system currently opens up for non-compliant and illegal actors. Taking this into consideration, Stena Recycling recommends that the European Commission considers the following recommendations when evaluating the End-of-life (ELVs) Directive:
context_after: The measure would allow the authorities to distinguish between legal and illegal actors, since the latter ones cannot deregister their ELVs. Consequently it will be easier for the last owner to understand where ELVs need to be delivered to an ATF. Such an incentive can be financed differently depending on the conditions in each Member State, for example an annual fee for each owner. The fee can be on a low level, since every owner is paying the fee, and the level could be adjusted depending on actual experience, so it would be both flexible and efficient. Other ways to incentive the delivering of ELVs could include improved information campaigns on the necessity of owners to dispose of ELVs in a safe, secure and sustainable way. 3) Better monitoring and improved inspections of Authorized Treatment Facilities (ATFs) Better monitoring and control of the ATF systems from authorities would also improve the disposal rates for ELVs. Authorities of EU Member States should have both the ability and the resources to perform sufficient inspections of ATFs. This could be achieved by increasing the number of inspections of ATFs and by ensuring that such inspections are carried out by personnel with expertise on that matter. What is more, to increase the quality of the way the ELVs are treated, there should be a demand for third party certificates for all ATFs. Shredders and traders must not be allowed to accept ELVs unless treated by an ATF
provision_effect: Requires producers or producer responsibility organisations to set up comprehensive collection systems for end-of-life vehicles covering the whole territory of a Member State, ensuring free delivery to authorised treatment facilities and collection of waste parts from repairs.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Stena Recycling explicitly advocates for increasing collection of ELVs for proper treatment at Authorised Treatment Facilities (ATFs), which is precisely what Article 23 establishes by mandating comprehensive, territory-wide collection systems ensuring delivery of all ELVs free of charge to ATFs. The provision directly acts in the direction the organisation advocated — increasing ELV collection and routing them to compliant treatment facilities — even if it does not adopt the specific fee/incentive mechanism Stena also proposed.

article_title: Delivery of end-of-life vehicles to authorised treatment facilities
article_text:
Article 24 — Delivery of end-of-life vehicles to authorised treatment facilities
1. All end-of-life vehicles shall be delivered for treatment to authorised treatment facilities.

2. Delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to an authorised treatment facility shall be free of charge for the last owner of a vehicle unless the end-of-life vehicle lacks any of the essential vehicle parts or components, except the electric vehicle battery, or contains waste which has been added to the end-of-life vehicle.
provision_effect: Requires all end-of-life vehicles to be delivered to authorised treatment facilities, and mandates that this delivery is free of charge for the last owner (with exceptions for missing parts or added waste).
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Stena Recycling explicitly advocates for increasing ELV collection through Authorised Treatment Facilities and introducing incentives for last owners to deliver ELVs for proper treatment. The provision directly mandates delivery of all ELVs to ATFs and establishes a free-of-charge mechanism for last owners, which aligns with the org's call for better incentives and obligations to ensure last owners properly dispose of their vehicles at ATFs.

source_text: should therefore be dealt with in the upcoming review of the ELV Directive, as well as in the upcoming decision on the wording of the Batteries Directive. 5) Better control of the waste streams from ELVs There is a need for improved control of waste streams from ELVs. One way in which this could be achieved is by obliging recycling companies and treatment operators to report how they have treated the material from ELVs. This requirement would allow also to distinguish between actors who contribute to the achievement of the objectives of the ELV Directive, and those who are not. 6) Better incentives to design cars in a more sustainable way An Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) has many purposes. One of them is to influence design of products put on market with the objective of reduced negative environmental impact often monitored as recycling rates. To reduce weight of cars with the objective to reduce energy, the trend in the automotive sector today is to use less steel and metals and replace these materials with new composites which are very difficult and expensive to recycle. As a result, vehicles put on the market today contain much more plastics, including plastics which are fibre reinforced, compared with the vehicles put on the market 15 years ago which are currently recycled
context_before: Shredders and traders must not be allowed to accept ELVs unless treated by an ATF. This should apply to shredders and operators located in EU and non-EU countries. 4) Higher consistency with related EU legislation Stena Recycling welcomes the European Commission’s attempt to ensure higher consistency between the ELV Directive and other related EU secondary legislation such as e.g. Batteries Directive. In that regard, some inconsistencies have been observed between the ELV Directive and the Batteries Directive. One of them concerns recycling rates and second use of batteries. These inconsistencies
context_after: Requirements to include recycled material fibre reinforced plastics from ELV waste streams into products with good societal value is therefore unfortunately not realistic, for it is too difficult currently to be recycled safely. However, recycled plastics from other recycling streams (such as post-consumer products) could be used in the production of vehicles. There is a need for detailed information on specific materials, components and technologies to be provided by the manufacturers. This information needs to be available for ATFs and waste management operators. Proven technologies and tools need to be available at the time of the application for a vehicle type-approval. Composite materials such as carbon-fibre-reinforced plastics cannot be recycled in a proper way and still many cars contain a large proportion of such material. For safety reasons it is essential that information, training and tools are available for ATFs and waste management operators. It is important to have safe processes and tools for example to neutralize all pyrotechnical units at ATFs. The rapidly increasing number of pyrotechnical units in a car mean a serious risk in the end of life treatment. High voltage batteries impose such a risk for the ATFs, and the logistics and transportation needed, that training and standards for the treatment need to be provided by the manufacturers, even more so for batteries deemed critical for treatment, storage and transportation
article_text:
Article 18 — Producer Responsibility Organisation
1. Producers may choose to fulfil their extended producer responsibility obligations either individually or may entrust a producer responsibility organisation authorised in accordance with Article 19 to fulfill the extended producer responsibility obligations on their behalf.

2. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure the confidentiality of the data in their possession as regards proprietary information or information directly attributable to individual producers or their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

3. In addition to the information referred to in Article 8a(3), point (e), of Directive 2008/98/EC, producer responsibility organisations shall publish on their websites at least each year, subject to commercial and industrial confidentiality, the information on the collection of end-of-life vehicles and achievement of targets on reuse and recycling, reuse and recovery and plastic recycling by the producers which entrusted the producer responsibility organisation.

4. Producer responsibility organisations shall ensure a fair representation of producers and waste management operators in their governing bodies.
provision_effect: Establishes rules for Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs), including confidentiality obligations, transparency/reporting requirements, and fair representation of producers and waste management operators in governing bodies.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Stena Recycling advocates for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) as a mechanism to incentivise sustainable vehicle design and improve recycling outcomes, which connects to the provision establishing PROs. However, the org's position focuses on EPR's role in influencing design and recycling rates, while the provision deals with the operational structure of PROs (confidentiality, reporting, governance) — making it genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores Stena's specific advocacy concerns.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719480 - 01/11/2021 Comments to Public Consultation Impact assessment - Review Directive 2000/53/EC (ELVs) and of Directive 2005/64/EC (type-approval) EUROFER additional comments to the options and inputs provided to the OPC questionnaire Draft date: 25 October 2021 Introduction This paper presents background information, rationales and comments related to the EUROFER responses introduced into the questionnaire of the Open Public Consultation on the revision of the End-of-Life Vehicles (ELVs) Directive. The document contains extensive inputs to some of the free text question boxes because of its limited characters. Comment to Question 5 "Do you think that specific target(s) for reuse should be implemented separately from recycling with the aim to support the transition to the circular economy within the EU?" The option of reuse of vehicle parts allows supporting Circular Economy with relevant environmental benefits. In particular, the reuse of complex parts of cars is an established market that has developed in recent decades. The background rationale of this market is based on the following aspects. (1) Mechanically complex parts of engines, gears or clutches of old cars, or no more produced, create a sustained, although very niche, demand. (2) Complex parts of the car usually made by high resistance, versatility and ductility materials can often be dismantled without damage
context_after: (2) Complex parts of the car usually made by high resistance, versatility and ductility materials can often be dismantled without damage. (3) These complex parts can be very quickly reconditioned (or slightly repaired if needed); however, if these parts are too damaged, they have to be recycled. (4) A very light oversizing of these complex mechanical parts of the vehicle will allow fulfilling the conditions reported under points (1) to (3), prolonging life expectations. According to our knowledge about vehicle parts' reuse or remanufacturing activities, almost all mechanical parts reused or remanufactured in this niche market are made with steel. article_title: Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
article_text:
Article 33 — Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall take necessary incentives to promote the reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components, whether removed during the use or end-of-life phase of a vehicle.
The incentives referred to in the first subparagraph 1 may include:
(a)a requirement for maintenance and repair operators to offer customers to repair a vehicle with used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components alongside offer to repair the vehicle with new parts and components, provided that such a requirement is formulated not to create excessive costs or administrative burdens for micro- and small enterprises;
(b)the use of economic incentives, including the establishment of a reduced rate of value added tax for used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components.
The Commission shall facilitate the exchange of information and sharing of best practices among Member States on such incentives.

2. The Commission shall monitor the effectiveness of the incentives given by Member States.

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, including labelling, warranty, and documentation obligations, and prohibits certain parts from being reused.
label: ALIGNED

Article 31 directly operationalises this by creating a formal framework for assessing removed parts for reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment, and by clarifying that parts fit for reuse shall not be considered waste — concrete action in the direction EUROFER advocated for.

context_after: treatment of vehicles and the ambitions towards a real circular economy created the need for more high-quality recycling. Quality improvements of the recycled materials have to pass through an increment of the separation of different fractions recovered from ELVs and their recycling. However, the definition of materials specific recycling targets should not lead to creating other mandatory recycling targets. Therefore, the only compulsory target shall always refer to the entire vehicle. Indeed, a push for more materials separation and their recycling could generate additional costs for the treatment facility, but, at the same time, the availability of more homogeneous materials fractions will definitively increase revenue opportunities for the ELVs treatment facility

article_title: Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
article_text:
Article 12 — Labelling of parts, components and materials present in vehicles
1. Manufacturers and their suppliers shall use the nomenclature of the component and material coding standards listed in points 1 to 3 of Annex VI for the labelling and identification of parts, components and materials of vehicles.

2. Manufacturers shall ensure that e-drive motors containing permanent magnets bear a conspicuous, clearly legible and indelible label indicating the information listed in point 4 of Annex VI.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VI in order to adapt it to technical and scientific progress.
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers and suppliers to use standardised nomenclature for labelling and identifying parts, components, and materials in vehicles, and mandates specific labelling for e-drive motors containing permanent magnets.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text discusses reuse, remanufacturing, and recycling targets for vehicle parts, with a focus on steel materials, but does not express any specific advocacy position on labelling or identification standards for vehicle parts and components. The text contains no directional stance on the labelling mechanism established by Article 12.

source_text: Therefore, we do not see the recycled content target as a valid recycling metric for all the components or materials of the vehicles, but only for a specific set. Moreover, at the moment, it seems also unfeasible to set a recycled content target for the entire vehicle because this could lead to sub-optimised solutions. As a final comment, we would like to add that, in circular terms, it would be more effective and efficient to impose a recyclability target for the vehicle because this will automatically imply the simultaneous fulfilment of the recovery and recycling targets and the availability, after the treatment of ELVs, of high quality secondary raw materials. Comment to Question 16 "Which, if any, additional criteria for evaluating exemptions from the list of substance prohibitions are necessary to allow a more differentiated assessment? Please indicate which criteria should be integrated into the exemption assessment mechanism" The management of substances that show hazardous properties has been an element of concern in all recent revisions of the EU laws. However, it is necessary to merge the growing scientific knowledge about the substances with their implications with practical risks and socio- economic impacts
context_before: will not find particular issues in depolluting and processing these types of ELVs. Moreover, trucks and bikes are already steel-intensive products, and, thus, the proposed recovery and recycling targets do not raise any concern because of steel recyclability. On the contrary, the establishment of reuse and recycled content targets can find some issues for their compliance. The fulfilment of reuse targets needs the existence of a well-structured and mature market for reconditioned parts, which is less developed – informal, tiny companies or for amateurs (motorbikes) – than the one for vehicles. The establishment of targets on recycled content is the primary source of concerns for the steel sector, not because of the nature of this recycling metric but more about its use and implementation. The recycled content makes sense for those materials or parts that are not recycled enough. Therefore, it makes sense to use this metric for pushing more for their recycling. On the contrary, steel parts of all vehicles are almost entirely recycled; more specifically, certain steel products are produced using predominantly ferrous scrap, while others mix primary and secondary sources. The limitation of the ferrous scrap intake of some steel qualities is due to the mismatch between the quality of the secondary raw material (ferrous scrap) recovered from waste and the expected final properties of the steel product

Please indicate which criteria should be integrated into the exemption assessment mechanism" The management of substances that show hazardous properties has been an element of concern in all recent revisions of the EU laws. However, it is necessary to merge the growing scientific knowledge about the substances with their implications with practical risks and socio- economic impacts
context_before: will not find particular issues in depolluting and processing these types of ELVs. Moreover, trucks and bikes are already steel-intensive products, and, thus, the proposed recovery and recycling targets do not raise any concern because of steel recyclability. On the contrary, the establishment of reuse and recycled content targets can find some issues for their compliance. The fulfilment of reuse targets needs the existence of a well-structured and mature market for reconditioned parts, which is less developed – informal, tiny companies or for amateurs (motorbikes) – than the one for vehicles. The establishment of targets on recycled content is the primary source of concerns for the steel sector, not because of the nature of this recycling metric but more about its use and implementation. The recycled content makes sense for those materials or parts that are not recycled enough. Therefore, it makes sense to use this metric for pushing more for their recycling. On the contrary, steel parts of all vehicles are almost entirely recycled; more specifically, certain steel products are produced using predominantly ferrous scrap, while others mix primary and secondary sources. The limitation of the ferrous scrap intake of some steel qualities is due to the mismatch between the quality of the secondary raw material (ferrous scrap) recovered from waste and the expected final properties of the steel product

article_title: Declaration on recycled content present in vehicles
provision_effect: Requires manufacturers to declare the share of recycled content (including for steel) present in each vehicle type, distinguishing between pre-consumer and post-consumer waste origins.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: EUROFER explicitly argues that recycled content targets should apply only to a specific set of materials/components rather than the entire vehicle, opposing a blanket approach. The provision aligns with this position by establishing a targeted declaration requirement for a defined list of specific materials (including steel), rather than imposing a vehicle-wide recycled content target. The provision's selective, material-specific scope directly reflects the direction EUROFER advocated for.

source_text: Comment to Question 35 "Please indicate other materials for which recycled content targets should be considered and explain shortly why" The definition of targets for the recycled content should concern the materials with no recycling or a meagre recycling rate. The rare earth elements (REEs) and critical raw materials (CRMs) are perfect examples. REEs, Gallium, Magnesium, and Niobium have the highest supply risk in the list of CRMs released in 2020 by the EU Commission. Moreover, the recycling of these is extremely low if not absent. Therefore, it could make sense to establish targets on recycled content for these CRMs; however, it should also be taken into account that often these materials are a component of certain alloys or used in certain specific products: for instance, REEs in magnets or electronic parts, for which it is challenging to extract or separate CRMs. It will also be relevant to consider the recycled content for alloying elements such as Nickel and Chromium used in several value chains, other than automotive. Therefore, imposing a recycled content target will not be feasible without supporting a provision for separating the parts containing the CRMs, as selected on purpose in question 18

Comment to Question 36 "Do you see a need for policy/regulatory measure regarding requirements on ethical sourcing of raw materials contained in vehicles?" We selected the option 'No' because the EU already has its own Regulation on ethical sourcing of raw materials, the Regulation on due diligence (EU) 2017/821. This Regulation is already in force, and industries have already started their compliance process to its requirements. The ethical sourcing of raw materials has to be dealt with only by specific regulations. Therefore, there is no need for additional policy/regulatory measures. However, question 36 in this respect is somewhat unclear because it practically gives two options for saying no: e.g. selecting the first two options (Yes and then a)) or just choosing the No (second-to-last) option. For the sake of simplicity, we just selected the No. Comment to Question 37 "Do you see a need for policy/regulatory measure regarding requirements on carbon footprint of raw materials used in vehicles?" The progress and improvements in tackling environmental impacts during the use phase of products have been possible thanks to life-cycle thinking and assessment

source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719478 - 01/11/2021 October 2021 HWE Feedback - End-of-life vehicles – revision of EU rules HWE is an EU association representing hazardouswastemanagementoperatorsactiveinthewholevaluechainof hazardous waste management, from collection to final treatment.Ouraimistoavoiddispersionofpollutantsboth into the environment and thewidereconomyaccording tothezeropollutionambitionfor anontoxicenvironment. We are present in 9 European countries with 160 state of the art installations representing 6 millions tonnes capacity. Our integrated network of state-of-the-art installations in Europe cover a wide range of processes (regeneration, recycling, recovery, hazardous waste incineration, physico-chemical treatments, soil remediation, hazardous waste landfilling). There are many challenges in the revision of the ELV Directive, among them the need to take into account new legislative, technical and scientific knowledge. Vehicles are complex objects made up of articles containing substances of concern in specific parts and components. The revised directive should fully take into account the risks due to these substances of concern and prescribe the adequate treatment of such specific parts and components in order to avoid contaminating the recovered materials and to comply with the non-toxic material cycle objective of the Green Deal. We will focus on this issue
context_after: We will focus on this issue. ● Many parts and components of end of life vehicles contain substances of concern ELVs are a decisive waste stream in order to meet the EU recycling targets and objectives of the Green Deal, notably with regards to plastic. Plastics and foams represent a large part in cars. They are protected against fire with, among others, brominated flame retardants (BFRs), some of which are now classified as persistent organic pollutants (POPs). Fabrics and carpets are protected against stains and may contain PFAS. Besides, the average age of end of life vehicles isbetween14to20years,whichismuchhigherthanmostofthepost-consumergoods. Consequently, it implies that the equipment/articles composing this very complex object may contain a lot of substances of concern, currently acknowledged legacy substances like POP-BFR or some PFAS but also not yet acknowledged legacy substances and currently acknowledged SVHC but also not yet acknowledged SVHC. ● The SCIP DB should help to identify the presence of substances of concern The new SCIP DB will act as a very valuable tool with regards to recycling of ELV to identify the presence of substances of concern in the different parts of the vehicle. It will indicate to the waste operators thelocationand concentration of those substances of concern present in the different parts of components of the cars
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to review and report on the application of the ELV Regulation, including an evaluation of measures concerning substances of concern in vehicles and their impact on high-quality recycling at end-of-life.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: HWE explicitly advocates that the revised ELV rules should fully account for substances of concern in vehicle parts/components and prescribe adequate treatment to avoid contaminating recovered materials and achieve a non-toxic material cycle. Article 55(2)(b) directly mandates that the Commission's review evaluate measures on substances of concern in vehicles and the need for further provisions addressing substances of concern that may affect high-quality recycling at end-of-life — precisely the direction HWE advocates for.

article_title: Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
article_text:
Article 7 — Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
1. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] shall be designed in a way which does not hinder the removal by authorised treatment facilities of the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the concerned vehicle during the waste phase of the vehicle.

2. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall be designed, as regards joining, fastening and sealing elements, so as to enable, in a readily and non-destructive manner, the removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries and e-drive motors from the vehicle by authorised treatment facilities or repair and maintenance operators during the use phase and waste phase of the vehicle.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend paragraph 2 by revising the list of parts and components that are to be designed for removal and replacement from vehicles, in order to include in that paragraph additional parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, taking into account technical and scientific progress.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts laying down the conditions for the design for removal and replacement of parts and components referred to in paragraph 2 where necessary to ensure harmonised implementation of the obligation set out in paragraph 2.
Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Requires vehicles to be designed so that specific parts and components (including EV batteries and e-drive motors) can be readily and non-destructively removed and replaced by authorised treatment facilities or repair operators during use and waste phases.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The org advocates for provisions on dismantling, separation, and extraction of parts containing substances of concern (particularly POPs) prior to treatment operations — which relates to the same subject of component removal from ELVs. However, the org's focus is specifically on decontamination and extraction of hazardous/POP-containing fractions to prevent contamination of material cycles, while the provision focuses on design-for-removal requirements for EV batteries and e-drive motors. There is a real connection (both concern removal of parts/components from vehicles at end-of-life), but it is genuinely unclear whether this design-for-removal provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply does not address the org's specific ask about hazardous substance extraction and decontamination obligations.

article_text:
Article 5 — Requirements for substances in vehicles
1. The presence of substances of concern in vehicles and in their parts and components shall be minimised as far as possible.

2. In addition to the restrictions set out in Annex XVII to Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006 and, as applicable, to the restrictions set out in Annexes I and II to Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 and in Regulation (EU) 2023/[OP: Batteries], any vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall not contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium.

3. By way of derogation from paragraph 2, vehicle types may contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium under the conditions and up to the maximum concentration values laid down in Annex III.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III in order to adapt it to scientific and technical progress by:
(a)establishing maximum concentration values up to which the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in specific parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles is to be tolerated;
(b)exempting certain parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from the prohibition on the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium set out in paragraph 2 where the following conditions are fulfilled:
(i) the use of those substances is unavoidable;
(ii) it is demonstrated that socio-economic benefits outweigh the risk to human health or the environment arising from the use of those substances;
(iii) there are no suitable alternative substances or technologies.
(c)deleting parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from Annex III, if the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium is avoidable;
(d)designating those parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles that shall be removed before further treatment and require them to be labelled or made identifiable by other appropriate means.
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III, in particular by removing certain exemptions for homogenous materials and components from the list, in case the specific exemption is addressed under other Union legislation.

5. Upon request from the Commission, and within 12 months from the request, the European Chemicals Agency (the ‘Agency’) shall prepare a report on the technical and economic feasibility of alternatives pertaining to existing exemptions listed in Annex III and, based on such assessment, a motivated proposal for the specific amendment of the exemption.

6. As soon as it receives the request from the Commission, the Agency shall publish on its website a notice that a report on a possible amendment of an exemption in Annex III will be prepared and invite all interested parties to submit comments within eight weeks from the date of publication of the notice. The Agency shall publish on its website all comments received from the interested parties.

7. At the latest nine months following the submission of the report referred to in paragraph 4 to the Commission, the Committee for Socio-economic Analysis of the Agency, set up pursuant to Article 76(1), point (d), of Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006, shall adopt an opinion on the report and on the specific amendments proposed. The Agency shall submit that opinion to the Commission without delay.

8. The Commission shall adopt the delegated acts referred to in paragraph 4 and shall take into account the socio-economic impact of introducing, modifying or deleting an exemption to the restriction in the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in vehicle types, including the availability of alternatives and the impacts on human health and the environment across the full lifecycle of vehicles.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements to minimise substances of concern in vehicles, prohibits lead, mercury, cadmium, and hexavalent chromium in new vehicle types, and empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts on exemptions, concentration values, and designation of parts requiring removal before treatment.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: HWE specifically advocates that parts and components containing substances of concern (including POPs) should be identified, separated, and extracted prior to any treatment operations to avoid contamination of material cycles. Article 5(4)(d) directly responds to this by empowering the Commission to designate parts and components that shall be removed before further treatment and require them to be labelled or made identifiable — which is precisely the decontamination/pre-treatment separation mechanism HWE calls for.
