article_title: Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
article_text:
Article 30 — Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, are removed from an end-of-life vehicle prior to shredding, after the depollution operations referred to in Article 29, have been completed.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if an authorised treatment facility demonstrates, that post-shredder technologies separates materials from parts and components listed in Part C, entries 13 to19, of Annex VII, as efficiently as manual dismantling processes or semi-automated disassembly processes.
For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the authorised treatment facility shall provide the information listed in Part G of Annex VII.

3. In addition to the obligations set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall maintain records of the end-of-life vehicles that are processed without the prior removal of parts, components and materials in accordance with paragraph 2, including the name and address of the treatment facilities, and the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the end-of-life vehicles concerned.
The authorised treatment facilities shall provide the information in the records referred to in the first subparagraph to the competent authority in accordance with Article 49(6).
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to remove specified parts and components from end-of-life vehicles prior to shredding, with a limited exception where post-shredder technologies are demonstrated to be equally efficient.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly advocates for dismantling and separating contaminated parts from vehicles before any shredding operations to prevent dispersion of pollutants and contamination of material streams. Article 30 directly delivers this by mandating the removal of listed parts and components prior to shredding, which is precisely the mechanism the organisation called for.

source_text:  Material restrictions The CRM-A believes that substances should be regulated under REACH and not under the ELV Directive to avoid future incoherencies and duplications. Additionally, the restriction of hazardous substances should be dictated by a risk-based approach and not by the hazard classification of the material. Industry has proactively adopted environmental, customer and worker friendly solutions and these must be considered when assessing the need to reduce the use of a hazardous substance in vehicles. At the same time, restricting certain hazardous materials may halt the innovation process where the substitution of CRMs often leads to poorer performing products, early obsolescence and, in some cases, reduced recyclability. For instance, in the case of batteries, phasing out NMC batteries on the basis of effectively negligible real-world risk, to switch to a non-recyclable chemistry would be a massive unintended consequence. Finally, the production and use of certain substances in products is the result of decades of investments, research, technology developments and know how. The restriction or phasing out of substances under EU regulatory frameworks on the sole basis of their hazardous classification will render difficult if not impossible for their production and use in the future were they to be considered as “essential”
context_before: In the present position paper, the CRM-A would like to address several key challenges for CRMs in vehicles and submit recommendations. Our Recommendations  Realistic and feasible targets for re-use and recycling The introduction of re-use and recycling targets per material in the ELV Directive will negatively impact the image of the substance on the market and arbitrarily force downstream users to substitute a material that produces the best results to reach the desired requirements, leading to performance losses. While several CRMs feature good recycling opportunities, for others recycling can be extremely challenging, not feasible and/or not economically viable. For example, some CRMs are used in alloys in very small quantities to improve specific characteristics of the alloying material. In this case, the small quantities of CRMs used render their recovery and recycling extremely difficult, often with greater economic and environmental costs. A ‘one size fits all’ approach towards re-use and recycling targets would be market-distorting, economically challenging and overall counterproductive. We therefore call on the European Commission to consider the social, economic and environmental costs of re-use and recycling targets per material via an in-depth impact assessment and to rethink the ‘reuse supply chain’ considering the above-mentioned challenges when reviewing the ELV Directive.
context_after:  Sustainable and ethical sourcing, and carbon footprint Product and sector specific sustainable and ethical sourcing obligations risk create a very fragmented and incoherent regulatory landscape resulting in overburden of industry and legal uncertainty. Over the years, industry has proactively adopted environmental, sustainable and due diligence solutions. Industry’s best practices and successful tools should be considered by the European Commission when evaluating possible regulatory requirements on ethical sourcing and carbon footprint of raw materials.  Regulatory and administrative burden CRMs are subject to increasing regulatory targeting and notification requirements. This is often the result of an accumulation of different initiatives, which negatively impact the innovation and competitiveness of EU industries. Special attention should be given to CRMs as key enablers of the transitions to a carbon-neutral and energy efficient economy.  Regulatory coherence and inconsistency The high number of regional, national, EU, and international regulatory frameworks targeting CRMs and waste management often feature contrasting and/or overlapping provisions, rendering it difficult for industry to identify the applicable legislation and to comply with its provisions. The CRM-A therefore calls on the European Commission to avoid future overlap and ensure coherence with other

label: OPPOSING
reasoning: The CRM Alliance explicitly argues that substance restrictions should be governed by REACH (not the ELV Directive), should follow a risk-based rather than hazard-classification approach, and warns that phasing out certain substances can harm innovation and recyclability. The provision does the opposite: it embeds substance restrictions directly in the ELV framework and maintains prohibitions on specific substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium) based on their hazardous nature, with derogations only under narrow conditions — directly contradicting the org's advocacy for a risk-based, REACH-centred approach.

article_title: Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
article_text:
Article 7 — Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
1. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] shall be designed in a way which does not hinder the removal by authorised treatment facilities of the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the concerned vehicle during the waste phase of the vehicle.

2. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall be designed, as regards joining, fastening and sealing elements, so as to enable, in a readily and non-destructive manner, the removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries and e-drive motors from the vehicle by authorised treatment facilities or repair and maintenance operators during the use phase and waste phase of the vehicle.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend paragraph 2 by revising the list of parts and components that are to be designed for removal and replacement from vehicles, in order to include in that paragraph additional parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, taking into account technical and scientific progress.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts laying down the conditions for the design for removal and replacement of parts and components referred to in paragraph 2 where necessary to ensure harmonised implementation of the obligation set out in paragraph 2.
Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Requires vehicles to be designed to enable non-destructive removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries, e-drive motors, and other listed parts by authorised treatment facilities during both the use and waste phases.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The organisation explicitly called for the new directive to clarify the dismantling process of electric ELVs (point 2), and Article 7 directly addresses this by mandating vehicle design that enables the non-destructive removal and replacement of EV batteries and e-drive motors. This is a clear and specific tie between the org's advocacy and the provision's concrete action.

article_text:
Article 27 — Obligations for authorised treatment facilities
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials, as well as waste parts from repairs of vehicles, are accepted and treated in compliance with the conditions set out in their permits, as well as in accordance with this Regulation.

2. Authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that all treatment for end-of-life vehicles comply, as a minimum, with Articles 28, 29, 30, 31, 34 and 35 and Annex VII of this Regulation, and shall apply best available techniques as defined in Article 3(10) of Directive 2010/75/EU.

3. Authorised treatment facilities shall:
(a)store, even temporarily, all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII;
(b)depollute all end-of-life vehicles, in compliance with Article 29 and the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII;
(c)remove the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the end-of-life vehicle, prior to shredding shredding or compacting by means of manual dismantling or (semi-) automated disassembly in a non-destructive way for components with a reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment potential;
(d)treat all end-of-life vehicles and their parts, components and materials in accordance with the waste hierarchy and the general requirements laid down in Article 4 of Directive 2008/98/EC, and with Articles 32, 34, 35 and 36 of this Regulation.
In addition to the requirements set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall electronically store the record of the performed treatment operations of end-of-life vehicles for 3 years, and be able to present this information, upon request by relevant national authorities.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex VII by adapting the minimum treatment requirements for end-of-life vehicles to scientific and technical progress.

5. Member States shall encourage authorised treatment facilities to introduce certified environmental management systems in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 1221/2009.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory obligations for authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) regarding acceptance, storage, depollution, dismantling, and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, including minimum requirements set out in Annex VII and the application of best available techniques.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FER represents Spanish ATFs and explicitly advocates for specific treatment requirements for ELVs, including the manual removal of end-of-life tyres at dismantlers — a requirement that falls directly within the scope of Article 27's obligations on ATFs, particularly the dismantling requirements in Article 27(3)(c) and Annex VII. The provision concretely establishes and reinforces the kind of mandatory treatment and dismantling obligations that FER is advocating for, aligning with their position that such requirements should be included in the future ELV legislation.

article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to depollute end-of-life vehicles as soon as possible after delivery, including separate collection of fluids, removal of hazardous substances, separate removal of batteries, and documentation of the depollution process.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FER explicitly represents Spanish authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) for ELVs and advocates for specific depollution requirements — notably that end-of-life tyres must be manually removed at car dismantlers, referencing Annex I minimum technical requirements of Directive 2000/53/EC. Article 29 directly establishes and codifies mandatory depollution requirements for ATFs, including the removal of parts/components containing hazardous substances and compliance with minimum requirements in Annex VII, which is the same type of concrete depollution obligation FER was advocating to include in the revised legislation.

article_title: Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
article_text:
Article 30 — Mandatory removal of parts and components for reuse and recycling prior to shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities shall ensure that the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, are removed from an end-of-life vehicle prior to shredding, after the depollution operations referred to in Article 29, have been completed.

2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if an authorised treatment facility demonstrates, that post-shredder technologies separates materials from parts and components listed in Part C, entries 13 to19, of Annex VII, as efficiently as manual dismantling processes or semi-automated disassembly processes.
For the purposes of the first subparagraph, the authorised treatment facility shall provide the information listed in Part G of Annex VII.

3. In addition to the obligations set out in Article 35 of Directive 2008/98/EC, the authorised treatment facilities shall maintain records of the end-of-life vehicles that are processed without the prior removal of parts, components and materials in accordance with paragraph 2, including the name and address of the treatment facilities, and the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of the end-of-life vehicles concerned.
The authorised treatment facilities shall provide the information in the records referred to in the first subparagraph to the competent authority in accordance with Article 49(6).
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to manually remove specified parts and components from end-of-life vehicles prior to shredding, with an exception if post-shredder technologies are demonstrated to be equally efficient.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FER explicitly advocates that certain components (e.g. end-of-life tyres) must be removed manually at car dismantlers prior to shredding, which directly aligns with Article 30's mandatory pre-shredding removal requirement for listed parts and components. The provision concretely delivers the type of mandatory manual dismantling obligation that FER was calling for in its position paper.

article_text:
Article 28 — General requirements for shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities and other waste management operators shall request that end-of-life vehicles delivered to them for shredding are accompanied by the following:
(a)documentation which includes the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) for the end-of-life vehicle concerned;
(b)a copy of the certificate of destruction that has been issued for the end-of-life vehicle concerned.

2. Authorised treatment facilities and waste management operators receiving end-of-life vehicles not compliant with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 shall:
(a)report the non-compliance to the competent authority;
(b)refrain from using those end-of-life vehicles in their shredding operations unless the competent authority authorises such operations.

3. Waste management operators conducting shredding of end-of-life vehicles shall not mix end-of-life vehicles, their parts, components and materials with packaging waste and waste electrical and electronic equipment.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory documentation requirements (VIN and certificate of destruction) for end-of-life vehicles delivered for shredding, reporting obligations for non-compliance, and prohibits mixing ELVs with packaging waste and WEEE during shredding operations.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org text is largely introductory/background material welcoming the consultation and listing general objectives, with the only specific proposal visible being about manual removal of end-of-life tyres at dismantlers. There is no substantive advocacy position expressed regarding documentation requirements for shredding, certificates of destruction, non-compliance reporting, or the prohibition on mixing ELVs with other waste streams — the subjects of Article 28.

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Requires assessment of removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, establishes that parts fit for reuse/remanufacturing/refurbishment are not waste, and sets labelling and warranty requirements for such parts.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org's position paper lists several proposals for future ELV legislation, but the excerpt provided only discusses end-of-life tyres being manually removed at dismantlers — it does not express a specific advocacy position on the assessment, classification, labelling, or warranty requirements for removed parts and components that Article 31 establishes. The text cuts off without presenting a directional stance relevant to this provision.

article_text:
Article 29 — Depollution of end-of-life vehicles
1. As soon as possible after delivery of an end-of-life vehicle to the authorised treatment facility, that facility shall depollute those vehicles before they are further treated, in compliance with the minimum requirements set out in Part B of Annex VII.

2. The fluids and liquids listed in Part B of Annex VII shall be separately collected and stored, in line with the requirements set out in Part A of Annex VII. Waste oils shall be collected and stored separately from the other fluids and liquids and be treated in accordance with Article 21 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

3. The parts, components and materials containing substances referred to in Article 5(2) shall be removed from the end-of-life vehicles, and handled in accordance with Article 17 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

4. The batteries shall be separately removed from end-of-life vehicles and stored in a designated area for further treatment in accordance with Article 70(3) of Regulation (EU) 2023/ [OP: Batteries Regulation].

5. The parts, components and materials, that have been depolluted, shall be handled and labelled in accordance with Articles 18 and 19 of Directive 2008/98/EC.

6. The authorised treatment facility shall document the depollution of end-of-life vehicles, by recording the information listed in Part B, point 3, of Annex VII.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to depollute end-of-life vehicles by separately collecting fluids, removing hazardous components and batteries, and documenting the depollution process before further treatment.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The org explicitly advocates for adding safety remarks to depollution requirements — specifically around the removal/neutralisation of explosive components (e.g. airbags) and batteries — which are directly addressed in Article 29(3) and (4). The org's proposal to reinforce these requirements in the regulation aligns with the provision's concrete mandates on removing hazardous parts and batteries from end-of-life vehicles, even if the provision does not include the exact safety-note wording the org requested.

article_text:
Article 28 — General requirements for shredding
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] authorised treatment facilities and other waste management operators shall request that end-of-life vehicles delivered to them for shredding are accompanied by the following:
(a)documentation which includes the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) for the end-of-life vehicle concerned;
(b)a copy of the certificate of destruction that has been issued for the end-of-life vehicle concerned.

2. Authorised treatment facilities and waste management operators receiving end-of-life vehicles not compliant with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 shall:
(a)report the non-compliance to the competent authority;
(b)refrain from using those end-of-life vehicles in their shredding operations unless the competent authority authorises such operations.

3. Waste management operators conducting shredding of end-of-life vehicles shall not mix end-of-life vehicles, their parts, components and materials with packaging waste and waste electrical and electronic equipment.
provision_effect: Establishes mandatory documentation requirements (VIN and certificate of destruction) for end-of-life vehicles delivered for shredding, and prohibits mixing ELVs with packaging waste or WEEE during shredding operations.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FER explicitly advocates for traceability of depolluted vehicles sent to shredder facilities and for clear regulatory requirements linking depollution to shredding operations, including ensuring no extraneous substances accompany the vehicle. Article 28's mandatory documentation (VIN + certificate of destruction) and compliance reporting requirements directly respond to FER's call for traceability and clearer regulation of the depollution-to-shredder chain, acting concretely in the direction FER advocated.

organisation: FNADE (French Private Companies Association for Waste Management)
source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719508 - 01/11/2021 Commission Relations Contractuelles Mise à jour le 22/04/2014 FNADE POSITION PAPER Revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles 25/10/2021 FNADE, the French association of environmental services companies, welcomes the Commission's new initiative on the Revision of Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles, which will lead to update and adapt the current legislation to economic reality and to improve its coherence with the aims of the green deal and the circular economy action plan. FNADE members, as key players of recycling but also of the waste value chain, would like to highlight the most crucial points in order to reach the Green Deal objectives in favour of a more circular economy and a Zero-Pollution ambition. Better design for better dismantling and recycling Eco-design is the first step to improve the recyclability of various parts, as well as ensuring the efficiency of recycling processes. Thus it is crucial to make dismantling and sorting easier and more thorough before the final shredding step. Once a vehicle is shredded, recovering materials become much more complex and costly so the onus should be on prior steps. Beyond dismantling operations, the intrinsic recyclability of specific parts should be ensured by working together with waste management companies and potential clients for Secondary Raw Materials: without actual outlets, recyclability remains theoretical even if technically feasible
context_after: Phasing out Substances of Concern and decontaminating materials to ensure high-quality recycling To improve recycling performance, FNADE calls for a reduction of additives contained in polymers or other materials. Substances of concerns impair the quality of recyclates and should, therefore, only be used when stricly necessary. Alternatively, a prior decontamination step for specific streams can be required to ensure a safe return back into the loop. Complex products such as vehicles require more than one waste treatment option When dismantling end-of-life vehicles, different types of liquid and solid waste streams are extracted and should be treated in accordance with the waste treatment hierarchy. While recycling remains the most desirable option for most waste streams, others should be either safely recovered or disposed of to avoid any harm to the environment or human health. The need for steady, efficient recycling markets FNADE strongly supports the Commission’s proposal to introduce mandatory minimum recycled contents in the current legislation. Creating secondary raw material markets is a crucial lever to reach the ambitious recycling targets and further reduce GHG emissions, particularly through avoided emissions due to recycling. This requirement should apply to the most common material streams such as glass, plastics and metal
article_title: Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
article_text:
Article 7 — Design to enable removal and replacement of certain parts and components in vehicles
1. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] shall be designed in a way which does not hinder the removal by authorised treatment facilities of the parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII from the concerned vehicle during the waste phase of the vehicle.

2. Each vehicle belonging to a vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall be designed, as regards joining, fastening and sealing elements, so as to enable, in a readily and non-destructive manner, the removal and replacement of electric vehicle batteries and e-drive motors from the vehicle by authorised treatment facilities or repair and maintenance operators during the use phase and waste phase of the vehicle.

3. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend paragraph 2 by revising the list of parts and components that are to be designed for removal and replacement from vehicles, in order to include in that paragraph additional parts and components listed in Part C of Annex VII, taking into account technical and scientific progress.

4. The Commission may adopt implementing acts laying down the conditions for the design for removal and replacement of parts and components referred to in paragraph 2 where necessary to ensure harmonised implementation of the obligation set out in paragraph 2.
Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Requires vehicles to be designed so that authorised treatment facilities can readily and non-destructively remove and replace specific parts and components (including EV batteries and e-drive motors) during both the use and waste phases.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FNADE explicitly advocates for eco-design requirements that make dismantling and sorting easier before shredding, arguing this is "the first step to improve the recyclability of various parts" and that "it is crucial to make dismantling and sorting easier." Article 7 directly delivers this by mandating vehicle designs that enable non-destructive removal of specific parts by authorised treatment facilities — precisely the design-for-dismantling outcome FNADE called for.

article_text:
Article 5 — Requirements for substances in vehicles
1. The presence of substances of concern in vehicles and in their parts and components shall be minimised as far as possible.

2. In addition to the restrictions set out in Annex XVII to Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006 and, as applicable, to the restrictions set out in Annexes I and II to Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 and in Regulation (EU) 2023/[OP: Batteries], any vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall not contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium.

3. By way of derogation from paragraph 2, vehicle types may contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium under the conditions and up to the maximum concentration values laid down in Annex III.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III in order to adapt it to scientific and technical progress by:
(a)establishing maximum concentration values up to which the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in specific parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles is to be tolerated;
(b)exempting certain parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from the prohibition on the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium set out in paragraph 2 where the following conditions are fulfilled:
(i) the use of those substances is unavoidable;
(ii) it is demonstrated that socio-economic benefits outweigh the risk to human health or the environment arising from the use of those substances;
(iii) there are no suitable alternative substances or technologies.
(c)deleting parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from Annex III, if the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium is avoidable;
(d)designating those parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles that shall be removed before further treatment and require them to be labelled or made identifiable by other appropriate means.
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III, in particular by removing certain exemptions for homogenous materials and components from the list, in case the specific exemption is addressed under other Union legislation.

5. Upon request from the Commission, and within 12 months from the request, the European Chemicals Agency (the ‘Agency’) shall prepare a report on the technical and economic feasibility of alternatives pertaining to existing exemptions listed in Annex III and, based on such assessment, a motivated proposal for the specific amendment of the exemption.

6. As soon as it receives the request from the Commission, the Agency shall publish on its website a notice that a report on a possible amendment of an exemption in Annex III will be prepared and invite all interested parties to submit comments within eight weeks from the date of publication of the notice. The Agency shall publish on its website all comments received from the interested parties.

7. At the latest nine months following the submission of the report referred to in paragraph 4 to the Commission, the Committee for Socio-economic Analysis of the Agency, set up pursuant to Article 76(1), point (d), of Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006, shall adopt an opinion on the report and on the specific amendments proposed. The Agency shall submit that opinion to the Commission without delay.

8. The Commission shall adopt the delegated acts referred to in paragraph 4 and shall take into account the socio-economic impact of introducing, modifying or deleting an exemption to the restriction in the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in vehicle types, including the availability of alternatives and the impacts on human health and the environment across the full lifecycle of vehicles.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements to minimise substances of concern in vehicles, prohibits lead, mercury, cadmium, and hexavalent chromium in new vehicle types, and provides a framework for exemptions and delegated acts to adapt restrictions based on technical and scientific progress.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: FNADE explicitly calls for a reduction of substances of concern in materials, stating they "should only be used when strictly necessary" — directly mirroring the provision's requirement to minimise substances of concern and its prohibition of specific hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium) with limited exemptions only where use is unavoidable. The provision takes concrete action in the same direction FNADE advocates for, making this a clear path (b) alignment.

organisation: EUROBAT - Association of European Automotive and Industrial Batteries Manufacturers
source_text: Ref. Ares(2021)6719507 - 01/11/2021 EUROBAT comments on the Public Consultation for the impact assessment supporting the review of the Directive 2000/53/EC on end-of-life vehicles and of the Directive 2005/64/EC on the type-approval of motor vehicles with regard to their reusability, recyclability and recoverability Additional comment on Q15: Substances used in automotive batteries and their exposure risks are already regulated through REACH and Occupational Health & Safety. The majority of currently available battery chemistries contain hazardous substances in some form. However, we emphasise that these substances are contained in the battery article within sealed units, and are not intended to be released during normal or reasonably foreseeable conditions of use. Risks from battery materials are therefore largely limited to the workplace, which makes OSH the right risk management option. Here, our industries already promote high standards of worker and environmental protection. World-leading European recyclers also work to ensure that battery materials are safely treated and recovered at their end-of-life, both for existing and new battery types. We therefore urge the Commission to solve this legislative overlap and use the Batteries Regulation as key legislative instrument on batteries, and REACH and OSH as key instruments on exposure risks and chemicals management
context_after: Additional comment on Q37: carbon footprint requirement could be considered for the individual components, more than for the raw materials. The Batteries Regulation could be an example in this sense.
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to review and report on the application of the ELV Regulation, including an evaluation of measures concerning substances of concern in vehicles and their impact on high-quality recycling at end-of-life.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: EUROBAT specifically advocates that the Batteries Regulation — not the ELV framework — should be the key legislative instrument for batteries, and that REACH/OSH should govern exposure risks, urging the Commission to resolve legislative overlap. Article 55(2)(b) mandates a review of measures on substances of concern in vehicles and whether further provisions are needed, which directly touches on the legislative overlap EUROBAT is concerned about. However, it is genuinely unclear whether this review provision would lead to an outcome aligned with EUROBAT's position (i.e., deferring to the Batteries Regulation) or could instead result in expanded ELV-level substance requirements that EUROBAT opposes — the provision is neutral on direction and merely triggers a future evaluation.

label: ALIGNED

The provision directly responds to this by cross-referencing Regulation (EU) 2023/[Batteries] as an applicable instrument alongside REACH restrictions, effectively carving out the Batteries Regulation as the governing framework for battery-related substances — consistent with what EUROBAT advocated for.

source_text: End-of-life vehicles contain very valuable second-hand parts, except for older end-of-life vehicles. Targets for reuse will ensure that parts are reused more, less waste is generated and there is a lower overall environmental impact. On the other hand, when having targets, it's important to also work with measured/validated reuse and that's not easy in practice. Question 6, detail Material-specific targets would indeed probably increase the recycling. Depending on which material and the current recycling processes of the different PST installations, material-specific targets could result in more seperate recycling or in more quality of recycling: a recycling proces needs to focus on a few metals and can't capture all types of metals. Depending on the level of the targets, more investments in PST or dismantling of specific parts could be needed to reach the targets. In that case the recycling costs increase. Off course, recycled materials of higher quality will increase revenues from sale. Question 10 The first, second and third options are, in our opinion, to be combined: export of vehicles should only be possible when there is a valid roadworhtiness certification, the vehicle complies with certain environmental criteria and a certain age or mileage has not been reached. The fifth option is also indicated. Better enforcement on the ban on export of ELVs, but also enforcement on new export regulations mentioned in option 1,2 and 3 is needed

article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, including labelling, warranty, and restrictions on reuse of certain parts.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: OVAM explicitly advocates for reuse targets for parts from end-of-life vehicles to ensure more reuse, less waste, and lower environmental impact, and also raises the practical challenge of measuring/validating reuse. The provision directly establishes a concrete framework for assessing, labelling, and enabling the reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment of removed parts — acting in the same direction OVAM advocates for. While OVAM specifically calls for targets (which this article does not set), the provision takes concrete action to operationalise and formalise the reuse pathway that OVAM supports.

source_text: If the vehicle fulfils one of these criteria and is thus an ELV, it has to be delivered to an ATF within: a) one month after the period of one month to show the valid registration and valid technical inspection document; b) two years after the last validation date of the technical inspection; c) 6 years after the first use when the vehicle never had a technical inspection; d) one month after the declaration of technical total loss (unless an official rehabilitation is set up). Exemptions: - old-timers registered in the register of motor vehicles and trailers meeting the inspection requirement for old-timers or vehicles of historical value older than 25 years that are only exceptionally on the public road and that meet the inspection requirement for old-timers; - vehicles that are kept as collector's items with good care, at least roofed and equipped with a drip tray under potentially leaking mechanical parts; - vehicles that are the subject of a judicial investigation, a confiscation or a liability investigation in the context of an accident and for which the investigation is still pending or which have not yet been released; - vehicles used for educational purposes; - vehicles used for rallycross meeting the following requirements : a) they are equipped with the necessary strengthenings (roll cage or strengthening bars); b) the glass has been removed; c) the seats of the vehicle has been removed except the driver's seat
context_before: for registration authorities to make a distinction between deregistration and final deregistration and not have a trace of the owner after (a not final) deregistration or export. When there is a full and digital traceability obliged and done, we would know the holder/owner and would be able to automatically stimulate or oblige them to deliver a vehicle that has become an ELV to an ATF. Therefore, also an objective definition of ELVs is needed. Only then, a digital and automatic system needed to enforce the collection of all ELV, could take place. ELVs can be defined based on the data of the documents of the vehicles, legally it’s a further defining of “must discard” of the general waste definition. In our legislation a vehicle is considered as an ELV if it meets one of the following criteria: a) a vehicle becomes a waste when the holder/owner cannot provide a valid registration and/or a valid technical inspection document within a month (for vehicles with a registration and inspection obligation); b) the validity of the technical inspection has expired for two years (when there is an obligation to have a technical inspection for the vehicle); c) the vehicle is 6 years old and never had a technical inspection (when there is an obligation to have a technical inspection for this vehicle); d) the vehicle is declared as technical total loss by an automobile expert

The most important advantage is in our opinion the avoidance of environmental harms thanks to minimum requirements for end-of-life treatment.
article_text:
Article 26 — Obligations for the vehicle owner
1. The owner of a vehicle that becomes an end-of-life vehicle shall:
(a)deliver the end-of-life vehicle to an authorised treatment facility or, in cases referred to in Article 23(4), to a collection point, without undue delay after receiving information that the vehicle meets any of the criteria for irreparability laid down in Part A, points 1 and 2, of Annex I;
(b)present a certificate of destruction to the relevant registration authority.
provision_effect: Requires the owner of an end-of-life vehicle to deliver it to an authorised treatment facility (or collection point) without undue delay after receiving information that the vehicle meets irreparability criteria, and to present a certificate of destruction to the registration authority.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: OVAM's position explicitly advocates for mandatory delivery of ELVs to an authorised treatment facility (ATF) once a vehicle meets ELV criteria, with specific timelines and defined exemptions — directly supporting the same obligation the provision establishes. The provision's core requirement (owner must deliver ELV to an ATF without undue delay) aligns with OVAM's advocacy for a binding delivery obligation, even if the provision does not replicate OVAM's specific timelines or exemption categories.

article_text:
Article 5 — Requirements for substances in vehicles
1. The presence of substances of concern in vehicles and in their parts and components shall be minimised as far as possible.

2. In addition to the restrictions set out in Annex XVII to Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006 and, as applicable, to the restrictions set out in Annexes I and II to Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 and in Regulation (EU) 2023/[OP: Batteries], any vehicle type that is type-approved as of [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 72 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation], under Regulation (EU) 2018/858 shall not contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium.

3. By way of derogation from paragraph 2, vehicle types may contain lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium under the conditions and up to the maximum concentration values laid down in Annex III.

4. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III in order to adapt it to scientific and technical progress by:
(a)establishing maximum concentration values up to which the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in specific parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles is to be tolerated;
(b)exempting certain parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from the prohibition on the presence of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium set out in paragraph 2 where the following conditions are fulfilled:
(i) the use of those substances is unavoidable;
(ii) it is demonstrated that socio-economic benefits outweigh the risk to human health or the environment arising from the use of those substances;
(iii) there are no suitable alternative substances or technologies.
(c)deleting parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles from Annex III, if the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium is avoidable;
(d)designating those parts, components and homogenous materials of vehicles that shall be removed before further treatment and require them to be labelled or made identifiable by other appropriate means.
The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 50 to amend Annex III, in particular by removing certain exemptions for homogenous materials and components from the list, in case the specific exemption is addressed under other Union legislation.

5. Upon request from the Commission, and within 12 months from the request, the European Chemicals Agency (the ‘Agency’) shall prepare a report on the technical and economic feasibility of alternatives pertaining to existing exemptions listed in Annex III and, based on such assessment, a motivated proposal for the specific amendment of the exemption.

6. As soon as it receives the request from the Commission, the Agency shall publish on its website a notice that a report on a possible amendment of an exemption in Annex III will be prepared and invite all interested parties to submit comments within eight weeks from the date of publication of the notice. The Agency shall publish on its website all comments received from the interested parties.

7. At the latest nine months following the submission of the report referred to in paragraph 4 to the Commission, the Committee for Socio-economic Analysis of the Agency, set up pursuant to Article 76(1), point (d), of Regulation (EC) No 1907/2006, shall adopt an opinion on the report and on the specific amendments proposed. The Agency shall submit that opinion to the Commission without delay.

8. The Commission shall adopt the delegated acts referred to in paragraph 4 and shall take into account the socio-economic impact of introducing, modifying or deleting an exemption to the restriction in the use of lead, mercury, cadmium or hexavalent chromium in vehicle types, including the availability of alternatives and the impacts on human health and the environment across the full lifecycle of vehicles.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements to minimise substances of concern in vehicles, prohibits lead, mercury, cadmium, and hexavalent chromium in new vehicle types, and empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts to manage exemptions based on technical and economic feasibility.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: OVAM explicitly advocates for bans on substances of concern in vehicles within a specific directive, noting the added value of directly relating restrictions to specific applications, while also calling for alignment with REACH to avoid double burdens. The provision does exactly this — it prohibits specific hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium) in vehicles and cross-references REACH (Regulation 1907/2006), directly responding to OVAM's position on substance bans in vehicle-specific legislation aligned with broader chemical regulation.

article_text:
Article 17 — Register of producers
1. By [OP: Please insert the date = the last day of the month following 35 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall establish a register of producers which shall serve to monitor compliance of producers with the requirements of this Chapter.
The register shall provide links to other national registers of producers’ websites to facilitate, in all Member States, registration of producers or appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility.

2. Producers shall register in the register referred to in paragraph 1. They shall to that end submit an application for registration in each Member State where they make a vehicle available on the market for the first time.
Producers shall submit the application for registration via an electronic data-processing system as referred to in paragraph 8, point (a).
Producers shall only make available vehicles on the market of a Member State, if they or, in case of authorisation, their appointed representatives for the extended producer responsibility, are registered in such Member State.

3. The application for registration shall include the information listed in Annex VIII. Member States may request additional information or documents, as necessary, to use the register of producers in an efficient manner.

4. By way of derogation from paragraph 3, the information referred to in point 1 (d) of Annex VIII shall be provided either in the application for registration under paragraph 3 or in the application for authorisation under Article 19.

5. Where a producer has appointed a producer responsibility organisation in accordance with Article 18, the obligations under this Article shall be met by that organisation mutatis mutandis unless otherwise specified by the Member State in which the vehicle has been made available on the market for the first time.

6. The obligations under this Article may be fulfilled on a producer’s behalf by an appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility.

7. Member States may decide that the registration procedure pursuant to this Article and the authorisation procedure pursuant to Article 19 constitute a single procedure, provided that the application for authorisation meets the requirements set out in paragraphs 3 to 6 of this Article.

8. The competent authority shall:
(a)make available on its website information about the application process via an electronic data-processing system;
(b)grant registrations and provide a registration number within a maximum period of 12 weeks from the moment that all the information required under paragraphs 2 and 3 is provided.

9. The competent authority may:
(a)lay down modalities with regard to the requirements and process of registration without adding substantive requirements to those laid down in paragraphs 2 and 3;
(b)charge cost-based and proportionate fees to producers for the processing of the applications referred to in paragraph 2.

10. The competent authority may refuse to register a producer or withdraw the producer’s registration where the information referred to in paragraph 3 and related documentary evidence are not provided or are not sufficient, or where the producer no longer meets the requirements laid down in Annex VIII, point 1 (d).
The competent authority shall withdraw the producer’s registration if the producer has ceased to exist.

11. The producer, or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation appointed on behalf of the producers it represents shall without undue delay notify the competent authority of any changes to the information contained in the registration and of any permanent cessation as regards the making available on the market within the territory of the Member State of the vehicles referred to in the registration.

12. The producer or, where applicable, the producer’s appointed representative for the extended producer responsibility or the producer responsibility organisation shall report to the competent authority responsible for the register on the performance of extended producer responsibility obligations.
provision_effect: Establishes a mandatory register of producers for vehicles, requiring producers to register in each Member State where they place vehicles on the market, and setting out procedures for registration, reporting, and withdrawal of registration.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: OVAM's position touches on deregistration conditions (e.g., only upon issuance of a Certificate of Destruction or proven export) and the importance of electronic notification procedures, which are related to the register of producers. However, Article 17 addresses producer registration and withdrawal of registration based on failure to meet requirements or cessation of existence — not the specific ELV deregistration/CoD conditions OVAM advocates for. There is a real thematic connection (registration/deregistration systems), but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores OVAM's specific advocacy on CoD-linked deregistration conditions.

organisation: Mutuelle d'Assurance des Instituteurs de France (MAIF)
source_text: 1. Reinforcing the existing right for a better traceability of vehicles and automotive components According to the evaluation of the existing ELV directive, about 4 million vehicles escape controls from both national and European authorities every year. If higher vehicle traceability would increase the amount of re-used automotive components available, security still comes first. Frauds and “vehicle laundering” must be prevented at all costs in order to limit the illegal circulation of defective vehicles. The limitation of parallel channels of vehicle treatment could be achieved by: - A requirement that authorized treatment facilities issue a certificate of destruction through an electronic notification procedure to the registration authority along with the delivery of the certificate of destruction of the last owner. - The establishment of a notification procedure between Member States when a certificate of destruction is issued for a vehicle last registered in another Member State. - A better traceability of changes of vehicles’ certificates. Moreover, to fight better against fraudulent dismantling chains and increase the amount of re- used automotive components available, the identification and traceability of these components should be improved. This could be achieved through the implementation of a computer-based system that could track automotive components from their origin and all along their life cycle. 2
context_before: In parallel to its response to the current public consultation, MAIF wants to share its propositions to make the automotive sector – from construction to dismantling – truly circular. MAIF would like to underline how important a better traceability of vehicles and automotive components and a full commitment of all actors working along a vehicle life cycle would be to make the entire automotive sector circular. First of all, one should bear in mind that MAIF is: - A downstream actor in the end-of-life vehicles treatment chain. Therefore, it is difficult for MAIF to anticipate the impact that further obligations would have on vehicles manufacturers (for example). MAIF did not answer when questions addressed subjects for which we have only a few data. - An insurer which mainly operates on the automotive market. MAIF does not have enough data to investigate the impact of a scope extension of the directive. However, the enlargement of recycling and reemployment to all motor vehicles follows a virtuous logic. P ag e 1 | 5
context_after: This could be achieved through the implementation of a computer-based system that could track automotive components from their origin and all along their life cycle. 2. Imposing recycling obligations by component on dismantlers could be counter- productive Nowadays, authorized treatment facilities (ATFs) dismantle an automotive component only if its recycling is economically valuable. In other words, the offer of a specific re-used automotive component should match a similar demand from consumers. Therefore, imposing recycling obligations on ATFs for specific components could force them to stock automotive parts which they may not sell. Both dismantlers’ costs and re-used automotive components’ prices would increase. Our objective to make re-used components attractive would not be reached. Such obligations would, in addition, shake up downstream actors which are currently being challenged on their core activities. The French management system of end-of-life vehicles has proven that the dismantling market is able to adapt as soon as a demand of recycling component appears. 3. It is essential to consider the whole life cycle of the vehicle to better recycle it To develop the circularity of the automotive sector, manufacturers must be fully committed, in two ways: - Ensuring that there is a better sharing of information from the manufacturer to the dismantler or repairer to make the vehicle dismantling easier: P ag e 2 | 5
article_text:
Article 45 — Electronic systems
1. The MOVE-HUB electronic system developed by the Commission shall be used for exchanging Vehicle Identification Number and information on the vehicle registration and roadworthiness status between national vehicle registers and electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States, as well as, to interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, where necessary for controls and requirements laid down in this Section.

2. The MOVE-HUB electronic system, referred to in paragraph 1 shall provide at least the following functionalities:
(a)exchange the data in real-time with the national vehicle registers, national electronic systems on roadworthiness of the Member States interconnected with it;
(b)enable an automated electronic check of data provided in a roadworthiness certificate as referred to in Annex II of Directive 2014/45/EU of the date of first registration of a vehicle, as well as of the Member State where a vehicle was last registered, as referred to in Directive 1999/37/EC, to determine whether a used vehicle to be exported complies with the requirements set out in Article 38, Article 39(1) and Article 40;
(c)interconnect to the EU Single Window Environment for Customs, in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2399 for the purpose of exchanging data and support the process of exchange of information referred to in Articles 39(1) and Article 40(2), as well as support the notifications referred to in Articles 41 to 43;
(d)for the purpose of cooperation with third countries under Article 44(2), allow electronic exchange of information with the competent authorities of third countries which have notified to the Commission pursuant to Article 40(2) the specific conditions for the import of used vehicles that they apply.

3. Member States shall interconnect their national vehicle registers and national electronic systems on roadworthiness with the MOVE-HUB electronic system referred to in paragraph 1. That interconnection shall be operational within 2 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

4. The Commission shall interconnect the MOVE-HUB system referred to in paragraph 1 to the EU Customs Single Window Certificate Exchange System established in Article 4 of Regulation (EU) 2022/2399, so that the automated controls referred to in Article 39 and Article 40(2) and the notifications referred to in Articles 41, 42 and 43 can be performed. That interconnection shall be operational within 4 years after the adoption of the implementing act referred to in paragraph 5.

5. The Commission shall adopt the implementing acts laying down the necessary arrangements for the implementation of the functionalities of the MOVE-HUB referred to in paragraph 2, including the technical aspects necessary for the interconnection of national electronic systems to the MOVE-HUB, the conditions of connection to MOVE-HUB, the data to be transmitted by the national systems and the format for the transmission of that data through the interconnected national systems.
The implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 51(2).
provision_effect: Establishes the MOVE-HUB electronic system for real-time cross-border exchange of vehicle identification, registration, and roadworthiness data between Member States and customs authorities to support controls on used vehicle exports.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: MAIF explicitly advocates for electronic notification procedures between Member States when certificates of destruction are issued, better traceability of vehicle certificates, and a computer-based system to track vehicles and components — all of which align with the MOVE-HUB provision's establishment of a real-time cross-border electronic system for exchanging vehicle registration and roadworthiness data. The provision takes concrete action in the same direction MAIF advocated: creating the digital infrastructure for inter-Member State vehicle data exchange to prevent fraud and improve traceability.

organisation: Mutuelle d'Assurance des Instituteurs de France (MAIF)
source_text: 1. Reinforcing the existing right for a better traceability of vehicles and automotive components According to the evaluation of the existing ELV directive, about 4 million vehicles escape controls from both national and European authorities every year. If higher vehicle traceability would increase the amount of re-used automotive components available, security still comes first. Frauds and “vehicle laundering” must be prevented at all costs in order to limit the illegal circulation of defective vehicles. The limitation of parallel channels of vehicle treatment could be achieved by: - A requirement that authorized treatment facilities issue a certificate of destruction through an electronic notification procedure to the registration authority along with the delivery of the certificate of destruction of the last owner. - The establishment of a notification procedure between Member States when a certificate of destruction is issued for a vehicle last registered in another Member State. - A better traceability of changes of vehicles’ certificates. Moreover, to fight better against fraudulent dismantling chains and increase the amount of re- used automotive components available, the identification and traceability of these components should be improved. This could be achieved through the implementation of a computer-based system that could track automotive components from their origin and all along their life cycle. 2
context_before: In parallel to its response to the current public consultation, MAIF wants to share its propositions to make the automotive sector – from construction to dismantling – truly circular. MAIF would like to underline how important a better traceability of vehicles and automotive components and a full commitment of all actors working along a vehicle life cycle would be to make the entire automotive sector circular. First of all, one should bear in mind that MAIF is: - A downstream actor in the end-of-life vehicles treatment chain. Therefore, it is difficult for MAIF to anticipate the impact that further obligations would have on vehicles manufacturers (for example). MAIF did not answer when questions addressed subjects for which we have only a few data. - An insurer which mainly operates on the automotive market. MAIF does not have enough data to investigate the impact of a scope extension of the directive. However, the enlargement of recycling and reemployment to all motor vehicles follows a virtuous logic. P ag e 1 | 5
context_after: This could be achieved through the implementation of a computer-based system that could track automotive components from their origin and all along their life cycle. 2. Imposing recycling obligations by component on dismantlers could be counter- productive Nowadays, authorized treatment facilities (ATFs) dismantle an automotive component only if its recycling is economically valuable. In other words, the offer of a specific re-used automotive component should match a similar demand from consumers. Therefore, imposing recycling obligations on ATFs for specific components could force them to stock automotive parts which they may not sell. Both dismantlers’ costs and re-used automotive components’ prices would increase. Our objective to make re-used components attractive would not be reached. Such obligations would, in addition, shake up downstream actors which are currently being challenged on their core activities. The French management system of end-of-life vehicles has proven that the dismantling market is able to adapt as soon as a demand of recycling component appears. 3. It is essential to consider the whole life cycle of the vehicle to better recycle it To develop the circularity of the automotive sector, manufacturers must be fully committed, in two ways: - Ensuring that there is a better sharing of information from the manufacturer to the dismantler or repairer to make the vehicle dismantling easier: P ag e 2 | 5
article_text:
Article 25 — Certificate of destruction
1. Authorised treatment facilities shall issue a certificate of destruction for every treated end-of-life vehicle to the last owner of the end-of-life vehicle. The certificate of destruction shall contain the information listed in Annex IX.

2. The certificate of destruction shall be issued in an electronic format and provided through an electronic notification procedure to the relevant authorities of the Member State, including the competent authorities designated under Article 14.

3. In case the end-of-life vehicle, for which a certificate of destruction has been issued in a Member State, is registered in another Member State, the vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the certificate of destruction was issued shall inform the relevant vehicle registration authorities of the Member State where the vehicle is registered that a certificate of destruction has been issued for the vehicle in question.

4. The relevant authorities of a Member State shall cancel the registration of an end-of-life vehicle only after receiving the certificate of destruction for that vehicle.

5. Certificates of destruction issued in a Member State shall be recognised in all other Member States.
provision_effect: Requires authorised treatment facilities to issue electronic certificates of destruction to the last owner and notify relevant authorities, establishes cross-border notification between Member States when a certificate is issued for a vehicle registered elsewhere, and mandates recognition of certificates across all Member States.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: MAIF explicitly advocated for two specific mechanisms that Article 25 directly delivers: (1) a requirement that authorised treatment facilities issue a certificate of destruction through an electronic notification procedure to the registration authority alongside delivery to the last owner, and (2) a cross-border notification procedure between Member States when a certificate of destruction is issued for a vehicle registered in another Member State. The provision implements both of these asks almost verbatim, making this a clear case of path (a) alignment.
