context_after: o Technical information about how to dismantle a vehicle are currently sold by manufacturers. Dismantlers are therefore forced to adapt themselves to every type or brand of vehicles. o It is necessary to: ▪ Make this information more available for ATFs so that they can proceed to an optimal dismantling and ensure the reemployment of components. ▪ Imagine data-sharing solutions that would allow ATFs to exploit the reemployment potential of each automotive component: • Build catalogues which would gather methodologies. • Develop data aiming at reporting equivalences between components in order to re-use them in another type or brand of vehicle. - Improving each step of a vehicle life cycle through the development of its reparability, the recyclability of its materials and the increasing of the use of recycled materials since the conception. Convinced of the major role that insurance companies have to play in the management and treatment of end-of-life vehicles, MAIF has developed since 2010 a chain to re-use automotive components. Thanks to its partnerships with officially certified recyclers and repairers, MAIF deconstructs every economically irretrievable vehicles of 8 years old and vehicles technically irretrievable. Then, parts of these vehicles are recycled and MAIF can propose these re-used components to its insured members to repair their vehicles
article_title: Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
article_text:
Article 31 — Requirements concerning the removed parts and components
1. All parts and components that have been removed from an end-of-life vehicle pursuant to Article 30(1), shall be assessed to determine whether they are fit for:
(a)reuse, in accordance with Part D, point 1(a), of Annex VII;
(b)remanufacturing or refurbishment, in accordance with Part D, point 1(b), of Annex VII;
(c)recycling; or
(d)other treatment operations, taking into account the specific treatment requirements in Part F of Annex VII
The parts and components that are fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall not be considered waste.
The assessment shall be carried out taking into account in particular technical feasibility of conducting the processes referred to in the first subparagraph and vehicle safety requirements.
Documentation confirming the conducted assessment shall be, upon request, made available to the relevant national authorities, including when the parts and components are transported for the purpose of reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment.

2. The removed parts and components fit for reuse, remanufacturing or refurbishment shall be:
(e)labelled in compliance with Part D, point 2, of Annex VII;
(f)accompanied by a warranty, if the parts and components are transferred to or used by another person.

3. The parts and components listed in Part E of Annex VII shall not be reused.
provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, including labelling, warranty, and documentation obligations.
label: OPPOSING
reasoning: MAIF explicitly argues that imposing recycling/dismantling obligations on authorised treatment facilities (ATFs) for specific components would be counter-productive, increase costs, and undermine the attractiveness of reused components. Article 31 does precisely this — it mandates that removed parts be assessed and categorised for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, and imposes labelling, warranty, and documentation obligations on dismantlers, which is the type of component-level obligation MAIF warns against.

organisation: Mutuelle d'Assurance des Instituteurs de France (MAIF)
source_text: o Preventing damaged vehicles to be used again As a consequence, MAIF pleads to increase the number of re-used automotive components available Since January 2019, MAIF has been requesting to every recyclers-partners that they systematically dismantle vehicles older than 8 years that have become economically irretrievable. By doing so, MAIF pursues its objective to increase the amount of re-used automotive components available, which is today too low on the French market to have a significant impact.  In France, in 2020, only 3% of components that have been replaced were by re-used components1.  The implementation of the systematic deconstruction of vehicles that have been declared economically irretrievable older than 8 years has allowed MAIF to reach its target of 10% of the 300 000 vehicles taken in charge repaired with re-used components (an objective that should have initially been reached in 2022). Currently, when an insurer transfers a vehicle economically irretrievable to a recycler, it is not obliged to deconstruct it and dismantle the parts that could be re-used. The extension to every insurer-repairer relations of this model would significantly increase the use of re-used automotive components.  At the scale of MAIF, the dismantling of every 14 000 economically irretrievable vehicles ceded by its insured members every year would allow the repairing of about 90 000 vehicles older than 8 years
context_before: Then, parts of these vehicles are recycled and MAIF can propose these re-used components to its insured members to repair their vehicles. The structuring of such a chain has several positive impacts for MAIF’s insured members, economic actors associated and on the environment: - Environmental o Limiting the use of raw materials (oil-based) o Preventing from a waste increase o Adding value to wastes by reemploying them - Economic o Contributing to a better cost management of a car repair o Ensuring the sustainability of MAIF’s partners o Allowing repairers to expand their offer o Transforming the repairing and recycling sector - Social o Creating new jobs and competencies o Maintaining or developing a local workforce - Security o Ensuring quality, reliability and traceability of car components o Limiting illegal reselling chains of vehicles and components P ag e 3 | 5
context_after: The introduction of this “older than 8 years” criteria is justified by the necessity to keep two equilibriums: - An economic equilibrium of the end-of-life vehicle recycling sector. Not all the actors of this chain have dismantling and repairing activities. - An equilibrium between the production of re-used components and the possibility to finally repair vehicles older than 8 years that have been declared economically irretrievable but that can be saved thanks to re-used parts. Indeed, if re-used automotive components become more available, some vehicles would be prevented from being declared “economically irretrievable” and their owner would not have to buy a new car. Apart from environmental advantages, the development of re-used parts would have positive impacts on: - The repairing cost: a re-used automotive components represents an average of 60% of the price of a brand-new automotive part. - Employment, thanks to the development of jobs across regions. 1 SRA (Sécurité et Réparation Automobiles), link P ag e 4 | 5
article_title: Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
article_text:
Article 33 — Reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components
1. From [OP: Please insert the date = the first day of the month following 36 months after the date of entry into force of this Regulation] Member States shall take necessary incentives to promote the reuse, remanufacturing and refurbishment of parts and components, whether removed during the use or end-of-life phase of a vehicle.
The incentives referred to in the first subparagraph 1 may include:
(a)a requirement for maintenance and repair operators to offer customers to repair a vehicle with used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components alongside offer to repair the vehicle with new parts and components, provided that such a requirement is formulated not to create excessive costs or administrative burdens for micro- and small enterprises;
(b)the use of economic incentives, including the establishment of a reduced rate of value added tax for used, remanufactured or refurbished spare parts and components.
The Commission shall facilitate the exchange of information and sharing of best practices among Member States on such incentives.

2. The Commission shall monitor the effectiveness of the incentives given by Member States.
provision_effect: Requires Member States to take incentives promoting the reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment of vehicle parts and components, including potentially requiring repair operators to offer used/remanufactured parts alongside new ones.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: MAIF explicitly advocates for increasing the availability and use of reused automotive components, calling for systemic dismantling of end-of-life vehicles to supply more reusable parts. Article 33 directly responds to this by mandating Member States to promote reuse, remanufacturing, and refurbishment of vehicle parts, including potentially requiring repair operators to offer used/remanufactured parts — which is precisely the direction MAIF advocates for.

Indeed, if re-used automotive components become more available, some vehicles would be prevented from being declared “economically irretrievable” and their owner would not have to buy a new car. Apart from environmental advantages, the development of re-used parts would have positive impacts on: - The repairing cost: a re-used automotive components represents an average of 60% of the price of a brand-new automotive part. - Employment, thanks to the development of jobs across regions. 1 SRA (Sécurité et Réparation Automobiles), link P ag e 4 | 5

provision_effect: Establishes requirements for assessing removed parts and components from end-of-life vehicles for reuse, remanufacturing, refurbishment, or recycling, and sets labelling and warranty obligations for parts fit for reuse or remanufacturing.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: MAIF explicitly advocates for the development and availability of reused automotive components, arguing they reduce repair costs, support employment, and prevent vehicles from being declared economically irretrievable. The provision directly establishes a framework that promotes reuse of removed parts and components (including exempting them from waste classification when fit for reuse), which is precisely the direction MAIF advocates for — increasing the availability and legitimacy of reused automotive parts.
